target_output.title: Insurance Fraud Detection AI System
target_output.executive_summary: A real-time AI fraud detection system for a specialty insurance carrier built on AKS with FastAPI, Azure OpenAI GPT-4o for claim narrative analysis, and scikit-learn anomaly scoring. Claim submissions trigger a two-stage scoring pipeline: synchronous structured anomaly scoring (<500ms) followed by async LLM narrative consistency analysis via Celery. Fraud alerts surface in a React 18 SIU investigator queue with AI-generated evidence summaries. PostgreSQL row-level security enforces model decision immutability for FCRA compliance. EU AI Act Article 14 human oversight enforced at the SIU referral API level.
target_output.architecture_overview: Claims system sends webhooks to the FastAPI ScoringService on claim submission. ScoringService runs the scikit-learn anomaly model synchronously against claim features (claim type, loss amount, provider ID, claimant history) and returns a preliminary anomaly_score in <500ms. A Celery task is enqueued to invoke Azure OpenAI GPT-4o with a pseudonymized claim narrative prompt, returning structured narrative_flags (inconsistency types, timeline anomalies, narrative_risk level). Combined fraud_probability_score is written to fraud_alerts table and the SIU queue. React 18 SIU UI polls for new alerts. Investigators review evidence summaries, set investigator_reviewed=True, then can submit siu_referral disposition via PATCH /alerts/{id}/decision. FastAPI dependency enforce_human_review() blocks siu_referral if investigator_reviewed is not set, satisfying EU AI Act Article 14. All model_decisions rows are append-only via PostgreSQL row-level security policy for FCRA adverse action auditability.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: React 18 TypeScript, React Query, Zustand, MSAL for Azure AD. Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, Celery with Redis broker, scikit-learn for anomaly models, azure-ai-openai SDK for GPT-4o. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server. Cache: Azure Cache for Redis 7. LLM: Azure OpenAI Service GPT-4o with structured output mode. Model Storage: Azure Blob Storage for scikit-learn model pickle artifacts. Hosting: AKS West US 2, Azure Container Registry, Azure Key Vault. Monitoring: Application Insights, Grafana, Azure DevOps CI/CD.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Real-Time Scoring Pipeline: Webhook receiver on POST /score/claim triggers synchronous anomaly scoring and async LLM narrative task, creates fraud_alert with preliminary score. LLM Narrative Analysis: Celery worker pseudonymizes PII fields, constructs GPT-4o prompt with structured output schema, merges narrative_risk into final fraud_probability_score. SIU Alert Queue: Paginated React table sorted by fraud_probability_score; investigator can filter by claim type, date range, alert status. Claim Evidence Panel: Structured anomaly score breakdown, LLM narrative flags with confidence levels, claim history timeline. Human Oversight Gate: Investigator sets reviewed flag in UI; PATCH /alerts/{id}/decision API enforces investigator_reviewed=True before accepting siu_referral disposition. Model Decision Audit: All scoring events written to append-only model_decisions table with model version, feature hash, and score for FCRA adverse action documentation.
target_output.system_components: FastAPI ScoringService pod (claim webhook receiver, anomaly model inference, Celery task enqueue). Celery worker pods (Azure OpenAI GPT-4o inference, score merging, alert update). FastAPI SIUService pod (investigator queue, decision management, evidence retrieval). React 18 SIU frontend served via Azure Static Web Apps. PostgreSQL Flexible Server with row-level security on model_decisions. Azure Cache for Redis (Celery broker and fraud score cache). Azure OpenAI Service GPT-4o deployment in West US 2. Azure Blob Storage for model artifacts and LLM prompt templates. Azure Key Vault for DB credentials, OpenAI API key, Azure AD client secret. AKS cluster with namespaces fraud-scoring and fraud-siu. Application Insights for APM and distributed traces.
target_output.workflows: Real-Time Claim Scoring: Claims system POSTs claim webhook to ScoringService. ScoringService loads current active anomaly model version from Redis cache (refreshed on model promotion). Anomaly model scores claim features and returns anomaly_score. Celery task enqueued with claim_id and pseudonymized narrative. fraud_alerts row created with preliminary anomaly_score and llm_status=pending. ScoringService returns {alert_id, preliminary_score, status: pending_llm} in <500ms. Celery LLM Narrative Task: Worker retrieves claim from DB, applies PII pseudonymization (claimant_name -> CLAIMANT_A, SSN/DOB omitted, provider_name -> PROVIDER_1), constructs GPT-4o prompt with narrative text and structured output schema. Invokes Azure OpenAI with max_tokens=2000. Parses response: {narrative_flags, timeline_anomalies, overall_narrative_risk}. Computes combined fraud_probability_score as weighted average (0.6 anomaly + 0.4 narrative_risk_numeric). Updates fraud_alerts row; writes immutable model_decisions row. SIU Investigator Review with Human Oversight Gate: Investigator opens Claim Detail in SIU UI. Reviews anomaly breakdown, narrative flags, and claim timeline. Clicks 'Mark Reviewed' button which PATCHes investigation_cases setting investigator_reviewed=True. 'Refer to SIU' button becomes enabled. On submission, PATCH /alerts/{id}/decision with disposition=siu_referral. FastAPI enforce_human_review() dependency queries investigation_cases; if investigator_reviewed=False returns HTTP 422 with EU AI Act Article 14 message. On pass, siu_referral_number assigned and investigation_cases row updated.
target_output.data_model: claim_submissions (id UUID PK, claim_number VARCHAR UNIQUE, claim_type ENUM property/liability/medical, claimant_id UUID FK, adjuster_id UUID FK, policy_id UUID FK, submission_date TIMESTAMPTZ, loss_description_text TEXT, medical_narrative_text TEXT, total_claimed_amount DECIMAL(14,2), status). fraud_alerts (id UUID PK, claim_id UUID FK, fraud_probability_score DECIMAL(5,4), anomaly_score DECIMAL(5,4), llm_narrative_risk ENUM low/medium/high/critical, llm_status ENUM pending/complete/failed, alert_date TIMESTAMPTZ, status ENUM new/under_investigation/referred_siu/dismissed, assigned_investigator_id UUID). model_decisions (id UUID PK, claim_id UUID FK, model_version_id UUID FK, input_features_hash VARCHAR, anomaly_score DECIMAL(5,4), llm_narrative_risk ENUM, combined_score DECIMAL(5,4), decision_timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ, llm_task_id VARCHAR) -- append-only via RLS blocking UPDATE/DELETE. investigation_cases (id UUID PK, alert_id UUID FK, investigator_id UUID FK, opened_at TIMESTAMPTZ, investigator_reviewed BOOL DEFAULT FALSE, reviewed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, disposition ENUM fraud_confirmed/no_fraud/inconclusive/siu_referral, siu_referral_number VARCHAR, closed_at). claimant_networks (claimant_id UUID, related_claimant_id UUID, relationship_type VARCHAR, shared_attribute VARCHAR, detected_at TIMESTAMPTZ). model_versions (id UUID PK, model_name, version_tag, artifact_blob_url, is_active BOOL, promoted_at, promoted_by).
target_output.api_design: POST /score/claim - receive claim webhook, return {alert_id, preliminary_anomaly_score, llm_status: pending} in <500ms. GET /alerts?status=new&claim_type=medical&page=1 - paginated SIU alert queue (investigator/admin roles). GET /alerts/{id}/evidence - structured anomaly breakdown, LLM narrative flags, claim history timeline. PATCH /alerts/{id}/decision - body: {disposition: siu_referral|pursue|dismiss, notes: str}. FastAPI dependency enforce_human_review(): if disposition=siu_referral and investigation_cases.investigator_reviewed=False return HTTP 422 {detail: 'EU AI Act Article 14: investigator review required before SIU referral'}. PATCH /investigations/{id}/reviewed - set investigator_reviewed=True and reviewed_at timestamp. GET /analytics/alerts/summary - daily volume, score distribution, claim type breakdown (analyst role). GET /models - list model versions; POST /models/{id}/activate - set active model version (admin role).
target_output.security_and_compliance: Azure AD App Registration with MSAL; FastAPI JWT dependency validates Azure AD tokens on every request; role claims: investigator (SIU queue full access), adjuster (own claims only), analyst (aggregate reports), admin (model config). TLS 1.3 for all internal AKS service mesh communication via Istio mTLS. PII pseudonymization before LLM prompt: claimant_name -> CLAIMANT_A, SSN and DOB fields omitted, provider names -> PROVIDER_1 through PROVIDER_N; pseudonymization applied in Celery worker before Azure OpenAI API call. model_decisions append-only: PostgreSQL row-level security policy 'CREATE POLICY model_decisions_no_update ON model_decisions FOR UPDATE USING (false); CREATE POLICY model_decisions_no_delete ON model_decisions FOR DELETE USING (false)'. EU AI Act Article 14 human oversight: enforce_human_review() FastAPI dependency blocks siu_referral disposition unless investigator_reviewed=True; this control is documented in the EU AI Act technical file. FCRA adverse action: every model_decisions row retained 3 years with model version and input hash for adverse action letter support. SOC 2 Type II: all API requests logged with user identity, action, claim_id, and outcome to Application Insights; Azure Key Vault for all secrets with access audit logs.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: AKS West US 2 with two namespaces: fraud-scoring (ScoringService 3 replicas, Celery workers 5 replicas auto-scaled by Redis queue depth), fraud-siu (SIUService 2 replicas). Azure DevOps pipeline: pytest 80% coverage gate, mypy strict, ruff lint, Docker build and push to ACR, Helm chart upgrade to staging namespace, integration test suite (scoring latency p95 validation, human oversight gate enforcement test, model_decisions immutability test), manual approval gate for production. Secrets injected via Azure Key Vault CSI driver to AKS pods. Production HPA on ScoringService: min 3, max 10 pods on CPU 70% and Redis Celery queue depth >100. Celery workers scale on queue depth metric exported to Azure Monitor via KEDA. Rollback: Helm rollback to previous chart version; model version rollback via POST /models/{id}/activate admin API.
target_output.observability: Application Insights SDK in FastAPI services: distributed traces across ScoringService -> Celery -> SIUService, dependency tracking for PostgreSQL and Azure OpenAI calls. Custom Grafana dashboards: fraud alert daily volume by claim_type, fraud_probability_score distribution histogram, LLM inference p50/p95 latency, Celery queue depth, SIU queue depth by status, model_decisions per day by model_version. PagerDuty alerts: ScoringService p95 latency >3s, Celery queue depth >500 (LLM backlog), Azure OpenAI 5xx error rate >5%, SIU queue depth >200 unreviewed alerts, model_decisions immutability policy violation attempt.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $6,800/month: AKS node pools $2,200, Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server $380, Azure Cache for Redis $180, Azure OpenAI GPT-4o at 2000 tokens/claim x estimated 3,000 claims/day x $0.005/1K tokens $900/month, Azure Blob Storage $50. Token budget cap of 2,000 per claim narrative controls LLM cost linearly with claim volume. Celery auto-scaling on queue depth prevents idle worker cost during off-peak hours.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (14 weeks): Claims webhook integration, scikit-learn anomaly model training pipeline, Azure OpenAI GPT-4o narrative analysis with PII pseudonymization, Celery async inference, fraud_alerts and model_decisions schema with RLS immutability, SIU alert queue React UI, human oversight gate enforcement in API, Azure AD RBAC, Application Insights APM, AKS deployment via Helm. Phase 2 (10 weeks): Claimant network graph analysis using PostgreSQL recursive CTEs for ring detection, model performance monitoring with drift detection alerts, FCRA adverse action letter generation, analyst reporting dashboard, model champion-challenger promotion workflow. Phase 3 (6 weeks): Real-time Kafka streaming for high-volume carrier integration, EU AI Act technical file automation, model explanation API for investigator drill-down.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with mypy --strict. ruff for linting. pytest with 80% branch coverage gate in CI. Critical tests: scoring p95 latency under 3 seconds in staging integration test, human oversight gate rejecting siu_referral without investigator_reviewed (must be a mandatory CI test), model_decisions immutability (attempt UPDATE/DELETE and assert PostgreSQL error). No raw PII in any test fixtures; synthetic claim data using Faker. All Azure OpenAI calls mocked in unit tests; integration tests use Azure OpenAI staging deployment. Model versioning: every model artifact stored in Azure Blob with version_tag; never overwrite existing artifacts. PR review required for changes to enforce_human_review() dependency, pseudonymization logic, or model_decisions RLS policy.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Azure OpenAI Service latency spikes degrade SIU queue freshness; mitigation: async Celery design means scoring webhook returns preliminary anomaly score immediately; LLM result fills in asynchronously with fallback to anomaly-only score if LLM fails after 3 retries. Risk: Scikit-learn anomaly model drift over time as fraud patterns evolve; mitigation: Phase 2 model monitoring with PSI drift detection and automatic retraining pipeline trigger. Risk: False positive rate causes investigator alert fatigue and dismissal of genuine fraud; mitigation: threshold tuning by claim type, analyst dashboard tracking false positive rate per model version. Tradeoff: PostgreSQL over Neo4j for claimant network graph in Phase 1 because team has no Neo4j expertise; recursive CTE approach handles initial ring detection at <500K claimant scale; Neo4j re-evaluated if graph queries exceed 10-second threshold at scale.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: EU AI Act human oversight enforcement implemented at API level via enforce_human_review() FastAPI dependency; complies with Article 14 meaningful human oversight requirement. Confirmed PII pseudonymization approach: field-level substitution with consistent aliases within a claim for narrative coherence. Confirmed model_decisions immutability via PostgreSQL RLS over application-level controls for tamper-evidence. Claimant network graph deferred to Phase 2 using PostgreSQL recursive CTEs before committing to Neo4j.

metadata.notes: AI fraud detection system revision round 3; resolves EU AI Act human oversight enforcement gap; LLM narrative analysis with PII pseudonymization; FCRA-compliant append-only model decisions.

profile.domain: Lloyd's Marine Cargo Insurance Educational Website

project_goal.value: Public-facing educational website for a Lloyd's of London syndicate explaining specialty marine cargo insurance products, coverage conditions, and claims procedures to freight brokers and logistics companies
project_goal.rationale: Core marketing and education objective enabling freight brokers to self-serve product information without broker calls

target_users.value: Freight brokers, logistics companies, and supply chain managers seeking information on marine cargo coverage for goods in transit
target_users.rationale: Primary audience identified in marketing research; secondary audience is Lloyd's market participants researching syndicate appetite

project_class.rationale: Pure informational site with no server-side runtime; content managed via Git-backed workflow and built at deploy time

capabilities.rationale: Static HTML/CSS site with Google Analytics 4 for traffic measurement; no backend, no auth, no database

complexity_level.rationale: Static site with 20-30 pages; no dynamic content, no user accounts, no payment flows

risk_level.rationale: No user data collected; no authentication; no financial transactions; risk limited to content accuracy and availability

data_sensitivity.rationale: No user PII collected; GA4 configured with IP anonymization; no cookies beyond analytics consent

external_exposure.rationale: Fully public website with no authentication required for any page

access_model.value: Public read-only; no authentication for site visitors; underwriter content editors have Git repository access for content updates
access_model.rationale: Informational site requires no user accounts; content workflow managed via Git pull requests

feature_scope.value: Product pages by cargo type (perishables, containerized, bulk, project cargo), coverage condition summaries (ICC A/B/C, SRCC, War), claims procedure guides, industry sector pages, contact office directory, news and market commentary section
feature_scope.rationale: Full content scope covering all marine cargo product lines the syndicate underwrites

mvp_scope.value: Core product pages for containerized and perishable cargo, ICC coverage condition summaries, claims procedure guide, contact office directory, and GA4 analytics integration
mvp_scope.rationale: Covers highest-traffic content categories identified in broker research

security_baseline.value: HTTPS only via CloudFront with TLS 1.2 minimum, HSTS header, CSP header disabling inline scripts, no server-side code execution, S3 bucket not publicly accessible (CloudFront OAC only)
security_baseline.rationale: Static site security baseline; CSP prevents XSS risk from any future inline script additions

frontend_stack.value: Hugo static site generator with Tailwind CSS; content authored in Markdown with Hugo front matter
frontend_stack.rationale: Hugo provides fast build times (<5 seconds for full site), excellent multilingual support for future Lloyd's market international pages, and no Node.js runtime dependency

backend_stack.value: None; no server-side runtime
backend_stack.rationale: Static site architecture requires no backend services

data_platform.value: None; no database; S3 for static asset hosting; PDF policy wording documents hosted on S3
data_platform.rationale: Static content only; policy wording PDFs served directly from S3 via CloudFront

hosting_target.value: AWS CloudFront CDN backed by S3 origin in us-east-1
hosting_target.rationale: CloudFront provides global edge delivery for freight broker audiences in multiple geographies; S3 for static asset storage with CloudFront OAC

privacy_retention_policy.value: No personal data collected; GA4 configured with IP anonymization enabled; analytics data retained in GA4 per Google retention settings (14 months); no first-party user data stored
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: No PII collection means minimal privacy obligations; GA4 IP anonymization satisfies UK GDPR requirement for Lloyd's London-domiciled syndicate

future_scope.value: French and Spanish language versions for Latin American cargo markets, interactive cargo coverage comparison tool, broker portal with login for quote requests
future_scope.rationale: International expansion planned for Year 2; Hugo multilingual support makes language addition straightforward

constraints.value: Build time must complete under 5 minutes for CI; PDF policy wording documents may not be modified by this project and are served read-only from existing S3 prefix
constraints.rationale: Hugo build speed target ensures fast deployment feedback; existing S3 PDF documents are owned by the syndicate's document management team

observability_baseline.value: Google Analytics 4 for page views, cargo type page engagement, and PDF download events; CloudWatch for CloudFront error rates and cache hit ratio
observability_baseline.rationale: GA4 provides marketing team with broker engagement data; CloudWatch monitors CDN health

execution_preference.value: Git-based content workflow with pull requests for underwriter-submitted content changes; GitHub Actions CI/CD; infrastructure via Terraform
execution_preference.rationale: Git PR workflow gives underwriters structured review process for content accuracy before publication

project.goal: Educational static website for Lloyd's marine cargo syndicate targeting freight brokers
project.target_users: Freight brokers and logistics companies
project.timeline: MVP 6 weeks

frontend.build: Hugo binary, no Node.js runtime for build

security.s3_access: CloudFront OAC only; S3 bucket not publicly accessible

data.storage: S3 for built static assets and PDF policy wording documents

devops.ci: GitHub Actions: Hugo build, HTML validation, Lighthouse CI score gate, S3 sync, CloudFront cache invalidation
devops.iac: Terraform for CloudFront distribution, S3 bucket, OAC, Route53 record, ACM certificate

constraints.pdf_docs: Read-only from existing S3 prefix; not modified by this project

open_questions.cms: Evaluate Decap CMS (Git-based) vs Contentful for future underwriter self-service editing

reasoner_reviews.product: The product scope matches the target audience of freight brokers who need self-serve access to cargo coverage conditions and claims procedures. Organizing product pages by cargo type (containerized, perishable, bulk, project cargo) aligns with how brokers search for coverage information. The PDF download of policy wordings directly from S3 is the correct pattern for broker due diligence workflows.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Hugo is an excellent choice for a static site of this scale. The CloudFront + S3 architecture with OAC is the current AWS best practice for static hosting. The Git PR content workflow with underwriter review is a sound governance pattern for a regulated insurance entity. Multilingual Hugo support makes future French and Spanish language expansion straightforward.
reasoner_reviews.security: S3 bucket must not be publicly accessible; CloudFront OAC is correctly identified. CSP header is essential for a site that may add third-party scripts (chat widgets, analytics). HSTS with long max-age prevents protocol downgrade attacks. No user data collected, so privacy obligations are minimal; GA4 IP anonymization satisfies UK GDPR for the London-domiciled syndicate.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Hugo full build under 5 minutes is easily achievable for a 30-page site. CloudFront cache invalidation on deploy should use path pattern /index.html,/*.html,/*.css,/*.js to avoid invalidating PDF documents from the existing S3 prefix unnecessarily.

security.auth_design: No authentication required for site visitors. GitHub repository access controls for underwriter content editors (branch protection on main, required PR reviews). AWS IAM role for GitHub Actions S3 sync with least-privilege PutObject on site prefix only.
security.abuse_prevention: CloudFront WAF with AWS managed rule groups (AWSManagedRulesCommonRuleSet) to block common web exploits against CloudFront origin; rate limiting at CloudFront to prevent crawler abuse of PDF download links.
security.privacy_controls: GA4 configured with anonymize_ip: true; no cookies used except GA4 measurement cookie with 14-month retention; GDPR-compliant cookie consent banner via simple first-party JS with no external consent management platform required given low data collection footprint.

data.entities: Hugo content types: product_pages (cargo_type, coverage_lines[], icc_conditions[], industry_sectors[], pdf_wording_s3_key), coverage_conditions (icc_class, description, inclusions[], exclusions[], typical_use_cases[]), claims_guides (cargo_type, step_by_step_procedure[], required_documents[], contact_office_id), contact_offices (location, address, phone, email, regional_markets[]), news_articles (title, date, author, body, tags[])
data.storage_design: Built HTML/CSS/JS assets synced to S3 site prefix via GitHub Actions aws s3 sync. PDF policy wording documents at existing S3 docs prefix served via CloudFront /docs/* path pattern. No database; all content baked into Hugo build output.
data.analytics_events: page_view (automatic GA4), cargo_type_page_view (custom event with cargo_type parameter), pdf_download_click (GA4 event on anchor click for /docs/*.pdf links), contact_office_view (custom event with location parameter)

devops.deployment_topology: S3 bucket (site prefix) -> CloudFront distribution with OAC -> Route53 A alias record. ACM certificate for custom domain in us-east-1 (required for CloudFront). Separate S3 bucket for access logs.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions workflow on push to main: hugo --minify build, html-validate for broken links, Lighthouse CI with performance score >90 and accessibility score >95 gate, aws s3 sync --delete for site prefix, CloudFront create-invalidation for /*
devops.observability_stack: CloudWatch dashboard for CloudFront metrics: 5xx error rate (alert if >1%), cache hit ratio (alert if <85%), total requests. GA4 for content engagement metrics surfaced in Google Analytics dashboard for marketing team.
devops.cost_controls: CloudFront estimated <$30/month for anticipated broker traffic volume; S3 storage <$5/month for static assets; no compute costs. CloudFront price class PriceClass_100 (US, Canada, Europe) covers primary broker geography.

frontend.app_structure: Hugo static site generator with Tailwind CSS; educational content in Markdown with Hugo front matter; multilingual support via Hugo's i18n system
frontend.pages_and_flows: Home, Product Pages by Cargo Type (perishables/containerized/bulk/project), Claims Guidance, Glossary, Contact
frontend.accessibility_notes: Marine cargo tables with coverage details require proper th scope and caption; all diagrams need text alternatives
frontend.performance_notes: Hugo builds static HTML with Tailwind CSS purging; images processed via Hugo image pipelines; CDN-cached with long TTLs for static assets

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a static website for a Lloyd's marine cargo insurance syndicate. Hugo + CloudFront + S3 is the correct architecture for a public informational site with Git-based content workflow. No backend, no database, no auth. Key considerations: CloudFront OAC for S3 access security, CSP headers for XSS protection, GA4 with IP anonymization for UK GDPR compliance, and Hugo content taxonomy organized by cargo type matching freight broker browsing patterns. Plan quality strong per manifest.
target_output.title: Lloyd's Marine Cargo Insurance Educational Website
target_output.executive_summary: A public static website built with Hugo and Tailwind CSS, deployed to AWS CloudFront backed by S3, providing freight brokers and logistics companies with self-serve access to specialty marine cargo insurance product information, ICC coverage conditions, claims procedures, and contact offices for a Lloyd's of London syndicate. Content managed via Git pull request workflow with underwriter review gates. GitHub Actions CI/CD rebuilds and deploys the full site in under 3 minutes on merge to main. Google Analytics 4 with IP anonymization tracks broker engagement by cargo type and PDF policy wording downloads.
target_output.architecture_overview: Hugo static site generator builds all content from Markdown files with front matter into minified HTML/CSS/JS in under 3 seconds for a 30-page site. GitHub Actions CI runs html-validate for broken links, Lighthouse CI with accessibility and performance score gates, then syncs built assets to S3 using an IAM role with least-privilege PutObject permissions. CloudFront distribution serves assets globally with OAC restricting direct S3 access. A separate S3 docs prefix (managed by the syndicate's document team) serves PDF policy wording documents via the same CloudFront distribution under /docs/* path pattern. Route53 alias record points the custom domain to CloudFront. ACM certificate in us-east-1 provides HTTPS. CloudFront response headers policy enforces HSTS, CSP blocking inline scripts, and X-Frame-Options. GA4 measurement code loaded with IP anonymization; a simple cookie consent banner manages the GA4 analytics cookie.
target_output.technology_stack: Static Site Generator: Hugo 0.124 with Tailwind CSS 3 for utility-first styling. Templating: Hugo Go templates with Hugo shortcodes for reusable insurance product components (coverage condition tables, cargo type cards, claims step lists). Content: Markdown with Hugo front matter (YAML). CI/CD: GitHub Actions with Hugo binary, html-validate, Lighthouse CI, AWS CLI for S3 sync and CloudFront invalidation. Hosting: AWS CloudFront with OAC + S3. DNS and TLS: Route53 + ACM. Analytics: Google Analytics 4. IaC: Terraform for CloudFront distribution, S3 buckets, OAC, ACM certificate, Route53 record, WAF WebACL with AWS managed rules.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Product Pages by Cargo Type: Separate Hugo content sections for containerized general cargo, perishable refrigerated cargo, bulk commodities, and project/heavy-lift cargo; each page lists applicable ICC conditions, typical coverage extensions, and links to PDF policy wordings. Coverage Condition Reference: Standalone pages for ICC A (all risks), ICC B (named perils), ICC C (restricted), SRCC (strikes), and War coverage explaining inclusions, exclusions, and when each applies to freight broker client shipments. Claims Procedure Guides: Step-by-step claim notification, survey appointment, and documentation requirements with links to blank claim forms hosted in S3 docs prefix. Industry Sector Landing Pages: Sector-specific pages for automotive, pharmaceutical, aerospace, and food and beverage cargo highlighting relevant coverage considerations. Contact Office Directory: Hugo data file (offices.yaml) rendering cards for London, Singapore, and New York offices with regional market coverage details. News and Commentary: Hugo content section for syndicate underwriter market commentary on piracy trends, climate-related cargo losses, and supply chain disruption.
target_output.system_components: Hugo binary for static site build (no Node.js runtime). Tailwind CSS CLI for production CSS purge and minify. GitHub Actions runner for CI/CD execution. S3 bucket (site prefix) for built static assets; S3 bucket (docs prefix, read-only for this project) for PDF policy wording documents. CloudFront distribution with two origins: S3 OAC for site assets, same OAC for docs prefix. CloudFront WAF WebACL with AWSManagedRulesCommonRuleSet. CloudFront response headers policy for HSTS, CSP, and X-Frame-Options. ACM certificate in us-east-1. Route53 hosted zone with A and AAAA alias records. S3 access logs bucket. GA4 measurement ID injected via Hugo site config.
target_output.workflows: Underwriter Content Update: An underwriter authors a new cargo product page as a Markdown file in content/products/project-cargo.md with Hugo front matter specifying cargo_type, icc_conditions, and pdf_wording_s3_key. Opens a pull request against main branch in GitHub. A senior underwriter and the compliance team review content accuracy and regulatory language. On approval and merge, GitHub Actions workflow triggers: hugo --minify builds the site in <3 seconds, html-validate checks for broken internal links, Lighthouse CI runs against the built output asserting performance score >90 and accessibility score >95, aws s3 sync --delete syncs built assets to S3 site prefix, CloudFront create-invalidation with paths ['/index.html', '/*.html', '/css/*', '/js/*'] refreshes cached pages without invalidating PDF documents in /docs/* prefix. Changes are live globally via CloudFront within 3 minutes of merge. Freight Broker Content Discovery: A freight broker navigates to the perishable cargo product page, reviews ICC A coverage conditions applicable to refrigerated container shipments, clicks through to the PDF policy wording for marine cargo open cover hosted in S3 docs prefix (GA4 fires pdf_download_click event), and contacts the Singapore office using the office directory.
target_output.data_model: Hugo content types defined in archetypes and layouts. product_pages: front matter fields cargo_type (string), coverage_lines (array), icc_conditions (array of strings referencing ICC A/B/C/SRCC/War), industry_sectors (array), pdf_wording_s3_key (string for CloudFront /docs/* URL construction), draft (bool). coverage_conditions: front matter icc_class, short_description, inclusions (array), exclusions (array), typical_cargo_types (array). claims_guides: front matter cargo_type, required_documents (array), step_count (int). contact_offices: Hugo data file offices.yaml with fields id, city, address, phone, email, regional_markets (array). news_articles: front matter title, date, author, tags, summary. No database tables; all content compiled into static HTML at build time.
target_output.api_design: No API endpoints. Client-side interactions: GA4 event tracking via gtag() calls in Hugo partial template for pdf_download_click (onclick handler on PDF anchor tags) and cargo_type_page_view (fired on DOMContentLoaded for product page templates). Hugo generates all page URLs following pattern /products/{cargo-type}/, /coverage/{icc-class}/, /claims/{cargo-type}/, /offices/{location}/, /news/{year}/{slug}/. S3 docs prefix URLs constructed in Hugo templates as https://{cloudfront_domain}/docs/{pdf_filename} for policy wording download links.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HTTPS enforced: CloudFront distribution configured with redirect-to-https viewer protocol policy, TLS 1.2 minimum (TLSv1.2_2021 security policy). HSTS: CloudFront response headers policy sets Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload. CSP: Content-Security-Policy header configured as default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' https://www.googletagmanager.com; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; img-src 'self' data: https:; blocking all inline scripts and unauthorized external script origins. S3 security: Bucket public access blocked at account and bucket level; CloudFront OAC policy grants only s3:GetObject to CloudFront service principal; S3 bucket versioning enabled. WAF: CloudFront WAF WebACL with AWSManagedRulesCommonRuleSet (XSS, SQL injection, known bad inputs) and rate-based rule 2000 requests/5 minutes per IP to limit scraper abuse of PDF links. UK GDPR compliance: GA4 IP anonymization configured; cookie consent banner for GA4 measurement cookie; no other cookies set; privacy policy page linked in site footer.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Terraform manages: S3 site bucket with OAC bucket policy, CloudFront distribution with two origins (site and docs) and cache behaviors, WAF WebACL association, ACM certificate validation, Route53 A and AAAA alias records, CloudFront response headers policy, S3 access logs bucket. GitHub Actions on push to main: run hugo version check, hugo --minify --gc build, html-validate ./public for broken links, npx lighthouse-ci autorun with performance >90 and accessibility >95 assertions, aws s3 sync ./public s3://{site-bucket} --delete --cache-control max-age=31536000 for assets, --cache-control max-age=0 for HTML, aws cloudfront create-invalidation for HTML and asset paths. Terraform changes reviewed via Terraform Cloud plan in PR and applied on merge. No staging environment needed for static site; Lighthouse CI on built output serves as pre-deploy validation.
target_output.observability: CloudWatch dashboard: CloudFront 5xx error rate (alarm if >1% over 5 minutes triggers PagerDuty), CloudFront cache hit ratio (informational, target >85%), total CloudFront requests (traffic spike visibility), S3 4xx errors (monitors for broken PDF links). GA4 reports: top cargo type pages by sessions, PDF policy wording download event count by document, contact office page clicks with regional breakdown, search query report (GA4 site search configured on Hugo search if added in future). Lighthouse CI results archived in GitHub Actions artifacts for performance trend tracking.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $35/month total: CloudFront data transfer $20 for anticipated 50GB/month freight broker traffic, S3 storage $3 for 500MB static assets, S3 requests $2, WAF $10/month base. CloudFront scales globally to any traffic volume with no configuration changes; Hugo static files handle traffic spikes from market events (e.g., major cargo loss news coverage) without capacity planning. No compute costs. CloudFront PriceClass_100 (US, Canada, Europe, Israel) covers primary freight broker geography at lowest price class.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): Hugo project setup with Tailwind CSS, content taxonomy design for cargo types and ICC conditions, core product pages for containerized and perishable cargo, ICC A/B/C coverage condition summaries, claims procedure guide, contact office directory with offices.yaml data file, GA4 integration with IP anonymization and pdf_download_click event, Terraform for CloudFront + S3 + WAF + ACM, GitHub Actions CI with Lighthouse score gates, cookie consent banner. Phase 2 (4 weeks): Bulk commodity and project cargo product pages, SRCC and War coverage condition pages, industry sector landing pages for pharmaceutical and automotive, news and market commentary section, Hugo multilingual configuration baseline for future French/Spanish expansion. Phase 3 (optional, Year 2): French and Spanish language versions, Decap CMS integration for underwriter self-service content editing without Git knowledge, broker portal planning and specification.
target_output.development_guidelines: Hugo content structured under /content with directories matching URL taxonomy: /content/products/, /content/coverage/, /content/claims/, /content/news/. Tailwind CSS purged via Hugo pipe on build; no unused CSS in production. Hugo shortcodes for reusable components: {{< icc-conditions-table >}}, {{< cargo-type-card >}}, {{< claims-steps >}}. All PDF links constructed via Hugo partial reading the CloudFront domain from site config; no hardcoded S3 URLs in Markdown. Accessibility: all cargo type icons have descriptive alt text; tables use proper thead/tbody markup; Lighthouse accessibility score >95 enforced in CI. html-validate run in CI fails build on any broken internal link or malformed HTML. Content changes require PR with at least one underwriter approval before merge to main.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Underwriter content accuracy errors published to public before compliance review; mitigation: branch protection requires at least one underwriter plus one compliance team member approval for all PRs modifying content/products/ and content/coverage/ directories. Risk: PDF policy wording S3 docs prefix managed by another team could change URL structure, breaking download links; mitigation: Hugo partial constructing PDF URLs is the single source of truth; integration test in CI validates CloudFront /docs/*.pdf URLs return 200. Tradeoff: Hugo over Next.js (static export) chosen for zero Node.js runtime dependency in CI and sub-3-second build times; Next.js would enable future interactive coverage calculator without site architecture change but adds npm toolchain complexity not needed for current scope. Tradeoff: No headless CMS for MVP; underwriters use Git PRs for content which is more governance-appropriate for regulated insurance content than a standalone CMS with direct publish capability.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Hugo over Eleventy for faster build times and built-in multilingual support for future international expansion. Confirmed CloudFront OAC over legacy Origin Access Identity as current AWS best practice. GA4 IP anonymization confirmed sufficient for UK GDPR compliance given no other personal data collected. Decap CMS deferred to Phase 3 pending underwriter training; Git PR workflow used for MVP to maintain compliance review gates.

metadata.notes: Static website for Lloyd's marine cargo syndicate; Hugo + CloudFront + S3; Git-based underwriter content workflow; GA4 with UK GDPR IP anonymization; strong domain specificity throughout.

project_goal.value: Real-time streaming infrastructure for a telematics-based auto insurance carrier to ingest driving behavior events from connected vehicles, enabling per-trip scoring and immediate premium adjustment notifications to policyholders
project_goal.rationale: Core telematics platform enabling usage-based insurance pricing based on actual driving behavior

target_users.value: Policyholders receiving driving score and premium adjustment notifications via mobile app; actuarial and pricing teams consuming trip scoring data
target_users.rationale: End consumers are policyholders; internal consumers are actuarial teams using aggregated driving data for pricing model updates

project_class.rationale: Core deliverable is the Kafka streaming topology, Kafka Streams processing jobs, and Cassandra data storage infrastructure; not a user-facing application

capabilities.rationale: Streaming data ingestion, Kafka Streams processing, Cassandra persistence, EKS deployment infrastructure, and mobile push notification integration

complexity_level.rationale: Millions of connected vehicles generating continuous GPS, accelerometer, and braking events; sub-2-second trip scoring latency; Cassandra cluster for high-write time-series storage

risk_level.rationale: Driving behavior and location data is personal; scoring errors could lead to incorrect premium adjustments; risk mitigated by human-readable score explanations and policyholder dispute process

data_sensitivity.rationale: GPS location tracking and driving behavior patterns are personal data under CCPA/CPRA; policyholder consent required for telematics program enrollment

external_exposure.rationale: Vehicle telematics units authenticate with Cognito before streaming events; policyholder mobile app authenticates to receive notifications; no public unauthenticated endpoints

access_model.value: AWS Cognito for vehicle device authentication (OAuth 2.0 client credentials); policyholder mobile app Cognito user pool for notification API; internal actuarial read-only Cognito user pool for batch data access
access_model.rationale: Device credential flow for vehicle units; user auth for mobile app; separate pool for internal data consumers

feature_scope.value: Vehicle telematics event ingestion via Kafka, Kafka Streams per-trip scoring (hard braking, speeding, cornering, night driving), trip summary generation, monthly driving score aggregation, premium adjustment computation, policyholder push notification via mobile app, actuarial batch data export
feature_scope.rationale: Full telematics platform scope for usage-based insurance pricing

mvp_scope.value: Kafka event ingestion from vehicle telematics units, Kafka Streams trip scoring for hard braking and speeding events, trip_summaries write to Cassandra, monthly score aggregation, push notification trigger to mobile app; actuarial batch export deferred to Phase 2
mvp_scope.rationale: Core telematics scoring pipeline validates infrastructure before adding cornering, night driving, and actuarial export features

security_baseline.value: Vehicle device mutual TLS for Kafka producer authentication; Cognito OAuth 2.0 client credentials for device API authorization; all telematics event data encrypted at rest in Cassandra via AWS KMS; CCPA opt-out support in policyholder mobile app
security_baseline.rationale: Device authentication prevents spoofed telematics data injection; KMS encryption protects policyholder location data at rest

frontend_stack.value: None; infrastructure project with no browser UI; policyholder notifications sent to existing mobile app via FCM/APNS push
frontend_stack.rationale: Infrastructure project; policyholder-facing UI is the existing mobile app not in scope of this project

backend_stack.value: Java 21 with Kafka Streams for stream processing; Kafka Streams application deployed on EKS
backend_stack.rationale: Kafka Streams provides exactly-once processing semantics and native integration with MSK; Java 21 virtual threads available for notification dispatch

data_platform.value: Apache Cassandra 4 for telematics time-series storage (vehicle_trips, telematics_events, driving_scores); Redis 7 for in-flight trip state during Kafka Streams processing
data_platform.rationale: Cassandra optimized for high-write time-series workloads from millions of vehicle sensors; Redis holds per-vehicle trip accumulation state during active trip

hosting_target.value: AWS EKS for Kafka Streams jobs; Amazon MSK for managed Kafka; us-east-1
hosting_target.rationale: MSK manages Kafka cluster operations; EKS for containerized Kafka Streams applications with HPA scaling

privacy_retention_policy.value: Granular telematics events retained 13 months for CCPA data subject access requests; trip summaries and driving scores retained 3 years for actuarial use; GPS coordinates purged after 30 days in compliance with state privacy minimization requirements
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: CCPA requires 12-month lookback for data access requests; actuarial pricing models need 3 years of trip score history; GPS coordinate minimization required by several state insurance department telematics regulations

future_scope.value: Cornering and night driving scoring modules, real-time distracted driving detection using phone usage events, integration with third-party telematics scoring providers, actuarial Redshift data warehouse export
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 expands scoring sophistication and actuarial data access

constraints.value: Trip scoring latency target p95 <2 seconds from event arrival to trip_summary write; MSK cluster in us-east-1 VPC; telematics event retention in Kafka 7 days; all GPS data must remain in us-east-1
constraints.rationale: 2-second scoring SLA for responsive policyholder notification; GPS data residency required by state insurance department telematics filing

observability_baseline.value: Datadog for EKS pod metrics and Kafka consumer lag; custom Datadog dashboards for trip scoring throughput, scoring latency p95, Cassandra write latency, and premium adjustment event volume
observability_baseline.rationale: Engineering on-call needs streaming pipeline health visibility; actuary team needs scoring throughput metrics

execution_preference.value: Terraform-first infrastructure provisioning; Kafka Streams application deployed via Helm on EKS; phased delivery starting with MVP trip scoring before adding premium computation
execution_preference.rationale: IaC from day one prevents configuration drift; Helm enables rollback of Kafka Streams application versions

project.goal: Kafka streaming infrastructure for telematics-based auto insurance trip scoring
project.target_users: Vehicle telematics units (producers), policyholders (notification consumers), actuarial team (data consumers)
project.timeline: MVP 16 weeks, full scoring suite 24 weeks

backend.framework: Kafka Streams 3.7 for stream processing
backend.processing: Per-trip scoring topology with hard braking and speeding detection, monthly score aggregation

security.device_auth: Cognito OAuth 2.0 client credentials for vehicle units
security.encryption: KMS encryption for Cassandra at-rest data

data.primary_db: Apache Cassandra 4 for vehicle_trips, telematics_events, driving_scores
data.state_store: Redis 7 for in-flight trip accumulation state
data.kafka: Amazon MSK 3.6 with 64 partitions on telematics-events topic

open_questions.cassandra_hosting: Amazon Keyspaces vs self-managed Cassandra 4 on EKS for operational simplicity vs performance tuning

reasoner_reviews.product: The trip scoring pipeline design covering hard braking and speeding detection maps directly to NAIC Telematics white paper behavioral factors. The 2-second scoring SLA enables near-real-time feedback to policyholders after each trip. Monthly score aggregation for premium adjustment is the correct cadence for state-filed rating program compliance.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Kafka Streams with Redis for in-flight trip state is the right architecture for stateful per-vehicle trip accumulation. The Cassandra schema needs careful partition key design; vehicle_id as partition key with trip_date as clustering key enables efficient per-vehicle trip history queries. MSK partition count of 64 on telematics-events supports the target vehicle fleet scale with room for growth.
reasoner_reviews.security: Vehicle device mTLS plus Cognito OAuth client credentials provides two layers of authentication for telematics event producers. GPS coordinate purge after 30 days must be implemented as a Cassandra TTL on the coordinates column in telematics_events rather than application-level deletion to ensure reliable enforcement.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The p95 <2 second scoring latency is achievable with Kafka Streams given low per-event processing complexity. The bottleneck is likely Cassandra write latency; recommend Cassandra consistency level LOCAL_QUORUM for trip_summary writes to maintain durability while keeping write latency below 50ms.

backend.service_design: Kafka Streams topology: telematics-events (MSK topic) -> TripSegmentationProcessor (detects trip start/end using ignition-on/off events, maintains per-vehicle state in Redis) -> ScoringProcessor (scores hard braking events >0.4g, speeding events >10mph over limit, computes trip_score 0-100) -> TripSummaryWriter (writes trip_summary to Cassandra on trip end event) -> PremiumAdjustmentComputer (monthly batch: aggregates trip scores to monthly_driving_score, computes premium adjustment delta)
backend.background_jobs: Monthly score aggregation job triggered by Kafka Streams schedule topology on 1st of month; CCPA data deletion job processing opt-out requests from policyholder app; GPS coordinate TTL cleanup job verifying Cassandra TTL enforcement
backend.failure_handling: Kafka Streams exactly-once semantics via transactional producer; Redis trip state backed by RDB snapshots every 60 seconds; dead-letter topic (telematics-events-dlq) for malformed events; Cassandra write retry with LOCAL_QUORUM; trip scoring idempotent via trip_id deduplication key
backend.scaling_notes: Kafka Streams application scaled horizontally on EKS with one consumer per partition (64 maximum instances); EKS HPA on Kafka consumer lag metric exported to Datadog; Cassandra write throughput target 50K events/second across 3-node cluster in production

security.auth_design: Vehicle telematics units authenticate via Cognito OAuth 2.0 client credentials flow; each vehicle has a unique client_id tied to policy_id; Cognito issues JWT with vehicle_id claim validated by MSK SASL/OAUTHBEARER plugin
security.privacy_controls: Telematics enrollment requires explicit policyholder consent in mobile app; CCPA opt-out removes vehicle from active scoring and queues deletion job for events older than 30 days; GPS coordinates stored with TTL=30 days at Cassandra column level; monthly driving_scores retain aggregated score only with no GPS data
security.audit_and_logging_controls: All premium adjustment events written to audit_log Cassandra table with policy_id, old_score, new_score, adjustment_amount, and computed_at timestamp; CCPA deletion job logs completed deletions to S3 audit trail

data.entities: vehicle_trips (vehicle_id PARTITION KEY, trip_date CLUSTERING KEY, trip_id UUID, start_time TIMESTAMP, end_time TIMESTAMP, distance_miles DECIMAL, trip_score INT, hard_brake_count INT, speeding_event_count INT, scoring_model_version). telematics_events (vehicle_id PARTITION KEY, event_time CLUSTERING KEY, event_id UUID, event_type ENUM ignition_on/ignition_off/hard_brake/speeding/cornering, latitude DECIMAL TTL 30d, longitude DECIMAL TTL 30d, speed_mph DECIMAL, acceleration_g DECIMAL). driving_scores (policy_id PARTITION KEY, score_month CLUSTERING KEY, monthly_score INT, trip_count INT, total_miles DECIMAL, premium_adjustment_pct DECIMAL, computed_at TIMESTAMP). premium_adjustments (policy_id, effective_month, old_premium, adjustment_pct, new_premium, notification_sent_at)
data.storage_design: Amazon Keyspaces (managed Cassandra) for vehicle_trips and telematics_events with provisioned throughput; Redis ElastiCache for in-flight trip accumulation state keyed by vehicle_id; MSK telematics-events topic with 7-day retention and 64 partitions
data.retention_and_deletion: telematics_events GPS columns TTL 30 days in Cassandra; full telematics_events rows retained 13 months; driving_scores retained 3 years; CCPA opt-out triggers deletion pipeline clearing all personal data for the requesting policyholder

devops.deployment_topology: EKS cluster us-east-1 with Kafka Streams deployment (trip-scoring namespace); Amazon MSK 3.6 multi-AZ cluster in same VPC; Amazon Keyspaces (serverless Cassandra) for managed operations; ElastiCache Redis cluster; FCM/APNS notification service as Lambda function triggered by Cassandra stream via DynamoDB Streams equivalent
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: Maven build and unit tests, Kafka Streams topology unit tests with TopologyTestDriver, Docker build and ECR push, Helm chart upgrade to EKS staging namespace, MSK schema registry Avro schema compatibility check, manual approval for production
devops.observability_stack: Datadog Agent on EKS pods scraping JMX Kafka Streams metrics: streams_processing_ratio, consumer_lag by topic-partition, trip_score_processing_latency_p95. Custom Datadog dashboards: scoring throughput (trips/minute), consumer lag (alert if >10K events), Cassandra write latency p95, premium adjustment event volume. PagerDuty for consumer lag breach and scoring latency SLA miss.
devops.rollback_strategy: Helm chart versioned rollback for Kafka Streams application; Kafka consumer group offset reset procedure for replay from MSK if scoring logic bug requires reprocessing; Keyspaces point-in-time recovery for Cassandra data

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing high-scale telematics streaming infrastructure for usage-based auto insurance. Key decisions: Kafka Streams with Redis for stateful per-vehicle trip accumulation, Amazon Keyspaces (managed Cassandra) for operational simplicity over self-managed Cassandra on EKS, GPS TTL at Cassandra column level for reliable 30-day purge per state privacy requirements, Cognito OAuth 2.0 client credentials per vehicle for device authentication. Plan quality weak per manifest; sections at functional minimum with correct domain specificity.
target_output.title: Telematics Auto Insurance Real-Time Streaming Infrastructure
target_output.executive_summary: A high-scale Kafka streaming infrastructure on AWS EKS enabling per-trip driving behavior scoring for a telematics-based auto insurance carrier. Vehicle telematics units stream GPS, accelerometer, and braking events to Amazon MSK; Kafka Streams jobs segment trips, score hard braking and speeding events (p95 latency <2 seconds), and write trip summaries to Amazon Keyspaces. Monthly driving score aggregation computes premium adjustments and triggers push notifications to policyholder mobile apps. GPS coordinates purged at 30-day Cassandra TTL for CCPA and state telematics regulation compliance.
target_output.architecture_overview: Vehicle telematics units authenticate via Cognito OAuth 2.0 client credentials and publish events to the MSK telematics-events topic (64 partitions, 7-day retention) over TLS with SASL/OAUTHBEARER. Kafka Streams application on EKS consumes events, maintains per-vehicle trip accumulation state in Redis (active trip boundary detection from ignition events), scores hard braking and speeding sub-events, and on trip-end writes a trip_summary to Amazon Keyspaces with the trip_score. A monthly Kafka Streams scheduled topology aggregates trip scores to driving_scores and computes premium adjustment percentages. A Lambda function invoked via Keyspaces change data events dispatches FCM/APNS push notifications to the policyholder mobile app. GPS latitude/longitude stored with 30-day column TTL in Keyspaces telematics_events table for CCPA-compliant data minimization.
target_output.technology_stack: Stream Processor: Java 21 with Kafka Streams 3.7, Kafka producer/consumer with SASL/OAUTHBEARER for MSK auth. Event Store: Amazon MSK 3.6, 64-partition telematics-events topic, 7-day retention. Trip State: Redis 7 on ElastiCache with RDB snapshots. Time-Series DB: Amazon Keyspaces (managed Apache Cassandra) with provisioned throughput. Device Auth: AWS Cognito OAuth 2.0 client credentials flow. Push Notifications: AWS Lambda with Firebase Admin SDK (FCM) and APNs HTTP/2 library. Hosting: EKS us-east-1 for Kafka Streams pods. IaC: Terraform. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with Helm. Monitoring: Datadog Agent with JMX integration.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Telematics Event Ingestion: Vehicle units produce ignition_on, ignition_off, hard_brake, speeding, GPS events to MSK with vehicle_id as message key. Trip Segmentation: Kafka Streams TripSegmentationProcessor detects trip boundaries from ignition events; Redis holds per-vehicle active trip state. Per-Trip Scoring: ScoringProcessor computes hard_brake_count, speeding_event_count, and trip_score 0-100 per trip. Trip Summary Persistence: TripSummaryWriter writes vehicle_trips row to Keyspaces on trip_end. Monthly Score Aggregation: Scheduled topology on 1st of month aggregates trip_scores to monthly_score and premium_adjustment_pct. Premium Adjustment Notification: Lambda reads Keyspaces change event, dispatches push notification with new monthly score to policyholder app. CCPA Opt-Out: Deletion pipeline removes telematics_events and vehicle_trips for opt-out policyholder.
target_output.system_components: Amazon MSK 3.6 multi-AZ cluster with telematics-events topic (64 partitions). EKS cluster running Kafka Streams trip-scoring deployment (auto-scaled by consumer lag). Redis ElastiCache cluster for per-vehicle active trip state. Amazon Keyspaces tables: vehicle_trips, telematics_events (GPS TTL 30d), driving_scores, premium_adjustments. Cognito user pool (policyholder mobile app) and app client (vehicle devices, client credentials). AWS Lambda premium-notification function with FCM and APNs integration. Datadog Agent DaemonSet on EKS for JMX metrics and pod monitoring.
target_output.workflows: Vehicle Trip Scoring: Vehicle ignition-on event arrives at MSK with vehicle_id key routed to partition by hash. Kafka Streams TripSegmentationProcessor creates Redis trip state entry for vehicle_id. Subsequent hard_brake events increment hard_brake_count in Redis trip state. On ignition_off, ScoringProcessor reads accumulated state, computes trip_score = 100 - (hard_brake_count * 5) - (speeding_minutes * 2), clamps to 0-100. TripSummaryWriter writes vehicle_trips row to Keyspaces with LOCAL_QUORUM consistency. Trip state cleared from Redis. Full pipeline from event arrival to Keyspaces write target p95 <2 seconds. Monthly Premium Adjustment: On 1st of month, Kafka Streams monthly topology reads vehicle_trips for the prior 30-day window per policy_id, computes monthly_score as median trip_score, looks up state rate filing for premium adjustment formula, writes driving_scores row to Keyspaces. Keyspaces CDC event triggers Lambda function that reads new monthly_score, computes premium delta, calls policyholder mobile app notification API to send push: 'Your driving score this month: 87/100. Your premium adjustment: -4.2%'.
target_output.data_model: Keyspaces tables: vehicle_trips (vehicle_id TEXT PARTITION KEY, trip_date DATE, trip_id UUID, start_time TIMESTAMP, end_time TIMESTAMP, distance_miles DECIMAL, trip_score INT, hard_brake_count INT, speeding_event_count INT, scoring_model_version TEXT, PRIMARY KEY (vehicle_id, trip_date, trip_id)). telematics_events (vehicle_id TEXT PARTITION KEY, event_time TIMESTAMP CLUSTERING KEY, event_id UUID, event_type TEXT, latitude DECIMAL, longitude DECIMAL, speed_mph DECIMAL, acceleration_g DECIMAL, PRIMARY KEY (vehicle_id, event_time)) WITH default_time_to_live=2592000 for GPS columns via column-level TTL. driving_scores (policy_id TEXT PARTITION KEY, score_month DATE CLUSTERING KEY, monthly_score INT, trip_count INT, total_miles DECIMAL, premium_adjustment_pct DECIMAL, computed_at TIMESTAMP). premium_adjustments (policy_id TEXT, effective_month DATE, old_premium DECIMAL, adjustment_pct DECIMAL, new_premium DECIMAL, notification_sent_at TIMESTAMP).
target_output.api_design: No public API. Internal interfaces: MSK telematics-events topic (Kafka producer API from vehicle units, SASL/OAUTHBEARER auth). Kafka Streams REST endpoint (JMX metrics only; no management API exposed externally). Mobile App Notification API: POST /v1/notifications (Lambda internal call with policy_id and score payload, Cognito-authenticated). Actuarial Batch Export API: GET /v1/driving-scores?policy_id={id}&from={date}&to={date} (Phase 2, read-only Keyspaces query over REST for actuarial tooling).
target_output.security_and_compliance: Vehicle device authentication: Cognito OAuth 2.0 app client per vehicle with client_id bound to policy_id; MSK SASL/OAUTHBEARER validates Cognito JWT on each producer connection; invalid JWT rejected before event written to topic. TLS 1.3 for all MSK producer connections and EKS pod service mesh communication. Keyspaces encryption at rest via AWS KMS CMK for vehicle_trips and telematics_events tables. GPS coordinate TTL: telematics_events GPS columns (latitude, longitude) set with TTL=2592000 seconds (30 days) at Cassandra column level to ensure automatic purge independent of application logic. CCPA compliance: policyholder telematics consent recorded in mobile app enrollment flow; opt-out request queues deletion pipeline that removes all vehicle_trips and telematics_events rows for the policy_id within 45 days; CCPA data access request served by Keyspaces query exporting trip summaries and driving scores for 13-month lookback. NAIC Telematics white paper: data use disclosure in policyholder enrollment documentation listing behavioral factors scored.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Terraform provisions: MSK cluster (3 brokers multi-AZ, broker.t3.medium for dev, broker.m5.4xlarge for production), EKS cluster with managed node group (m5.2xlarge), ElastiCache Redis cluster (cache.r7g.large), Keyspaces tables with provisioned read/write capacity, Cognito user pools, Lambda function and IAM role, VPC with private subnets, MSK security group. GitHub Actions CI: Maven build, unit tests including Kafka Streams TopologyTestDriver tests for trip scoring logic, Docker build and ECR push, Helm chart upgrade to staging EKS namespace, integration test running 10K synthetic trip events through staging MSK and asserting trip_scores written to staging Keyspaces within 2 seconds, manual production approval. EKS HPA on Kafka Streams deployment: min 8, max 64 replicas based on MSK consumer lag CloudWatch metric exported to Datadog KEDA scaler.
target_output.observability: Datadog Agent DaemonSet on EKS collecting JMX Kafka Streams metrics via jmxfetch: kafka.consumer.records_lag_max per consumer group, kafka.streams.record_processing_rate, trip_score_latency_p95 (custom JMX bean). Datadog dashboards: scoring throughput (trips/minute), consumer lag time series (alert if >10K events sustained 5 minutes), Keyspaces write latency p95 (alert if >100ms), premium adjustment event count per day, CCPA deletion job completion rate. PagerDuty: consumer lag >50K events (scoring backlog), scoring latency p95 >2 seconds, Keyspaces write error rate >1%, MSK broker unavailable.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $8,500/month for 500K active vehicles: MSK m5.4xlarge x3 $2,800, EKS m5.2xlarge nodes $1,600, Keyspaces on-demand write capacity at 50K events/second $2,400, ElastiCache Redis r7g.large $300, Lambda notifications $200, data transfer $700, Datadog $500. Kafka Streams auto-scales from 8 to 64 pods on MSK consumer lag; Keyspaces provisioned throughput scales on-demand. GPS TTL purge reduces Keyspaces storage growth by removing high-cardinality location data after 30 days.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (16 weeks): MSK cluster and EKS provisioning via Terraform, Kafka Streams TripSegmentationProcessor and ScoringProcessor for hard braking and speeding, Redis trip state with RDB snapshots, Keyspaces vehicle_trips and telematics_events schema with GPS TTL, monthly score aggregation topology, Lambda premium adjustment notification, Cognito device OAuth and policyholder user pool, Datadog dashboards, GitHub Actions CI with TopologyTestDriver tests. Phase 2 (8 weeks): Cornering and night driving scoring modules, actuarial batch export API over Keyspaces, CCPA opt-out deletion pipeline, MSK schema registry with Avro for event schema evolution, Redshift integration for actuarial data warehouse.
target_output.development_guidelines: Java 21 with Maven. Kafka Streams topology unit tested with TopologyTestDriver; mandatory test cases: trip boundary detection from ignition events, hard brake scoring threshold (0.4g), speeding detection, monthly score aggregation, idempotent trip_id deduplication. No GPS coordinates in test fixtures; synthetic lat/lon using bounding box for fictional US city. Keyspaces schema changes via Terraform only; no ad-hoc CQL ALTER TABLE in production. Helm chart versioned releases matching application version tags. Consumer group offsets committed after successful Keyspaces write to avoid double-scoring on Kafka Streams restart. Code review required for changes to trip_score computation formula; scoring model version field in vehicle_trips enables audit trail of formula changes.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Redis trip state loss during ElastiCache failure leaves in-flight trips unscored; mitigation: RDB snapshot every 60 seconds limits maximum state loss to 60 seconds of events; Kafka consumer offset held until Redis state committed allows replay from MSK topic. Risk: Keyspaces provisioned throughput underprovisioned during peak morning commute event spikes; mitigation: Keyspaces on-demand capacity mode in production absorbs spikes; provisioned mode for dev/staging cost control. Tradeoff: Amazon Keyspaces (managed) over self-managed Cassandra 4 on EKS reduces operational burden at the cost of some Cassandra configuration flexibility; acceptable for standard time-series telematics workload. Tradeoff: Column-level TTL for GPS coordinates over application-level deletion because Cassandra TTL is enforced by the storage engine independent of application availability, ensuring CCPA 30-day GPS purge even during application outages.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Amazon Keyspaces over self-managed Cassandra 4 for operational simplicity given team has limited Cassandra operations experience. Confirmed GPS TTL at Cassandra column level over application-level deletion for reliable CCPA compliance. Confirmed 64 MSK partitions on telematics-events topic to support up to 64 Kafka Streams parallel consumer instances. MSK schema registry with Avro deferred to Phase 2 after MVP event schema is stable.

metadata.notes: Telematics streaming infrastructure for usage-based auto insurance; Kafka Streams trip scoring, Amazon Keyspaces, GPS TTL for CCPA compliance, Cognito device OAuth; weak plan quality with thinner sections.

project_goal.rationale: Core research objective to determine feasibility of parametric triggers replacing loss-adjustment for residential flood claims
