project.goal: Evaluate parametric flood trigger feasibility via backtesting on historical USGS and satellite data
project.target_users: Actuarial researchers and catastrophe modelers
project.timeline: 3-month research sprint with bi-weekly milestones

backend.framework: Jupyter notebooks with Dask for distributed batch processing
backend.api: No external API; notebook-driven workflows calling Python module library

security.auth: Google IAM project-level access
security.transport: GCS encrypted at rest; internal GCP network only

data.cache: None; results persisted to GCS between runs

devops.ci: Cloud Build for Dask worker Docker images
devops.hosting: Vertex AI Workbench, Dask on GKE Autopilot
devops.environments: single research GCP project; no staging or production tiers

open_questions.trigger_granularity: Optimal spatial resolution for trigger zones: HUC-8 vs HUC-12 watershed
open_questions.basis_risk_threshold: Acceptable basis risk level that would pass state parametric product filing review

reasoner_reviews.product: The research scope is tightly defined: ingest USGS gauge time series, process satellite inundation rasters, backtest parametric triggers against residential exposures, and compute basis risk. The bi-weekly milestone structure suits exploratory research. MVP of single-county backtesting is the right first gate before committing to national-scale compute.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: DuckDB over Parquet on GCS is the right choice for this prototype -- columnar analytics on multi-year gauge time series and residential exposure join tables will be fast without a managed database. Dask handles the raster processing burst workload well on GKE Autopilot. The critical design decision is rasterio point-sample extraction to spatially join inundation depth rasters against exposure lat/lon coordinates, which avoids materializing large raster arrays in memory.
reasoner_reviews.security: Internal-only prototype with Google IAM is adequate. The primary risk is accidental public exposure of internal residential exposure data; GCS bucket IAM with no public access and no public endpoints mitigates this. Cloud Logging should capture notebook execution events to maintain a research audit trail.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: GCP us-central1 constraint is satisfied by Vertex AI Workbench and GKE Autopilot. $2000/month budget is achievable with on-demand Dask cluster spin-up/down between runs and Workbench auto-stop schedule outside business hours.

backend.service_design: Python module library organized into: flood_ingestion (USGS NWIS API client and gauge normalization), raster_processor (rasterio-based inundation depth extraction at address coordinates), backtest_engine (trigger evaluation loop over historical flood events), basis_risk (payout vs modeled loss comparison metrics)
backend.api_patterns: No HTTP API; Dask delayed functions orchestrate parallel backtesting jobs across county-year-trigger combinations; module functions called directly from Jupyter notebooks
backend.background_jobs: Dask distributed jobs for raster spatial joins and multi-county backtesting dispatched from notebook to GKE Autopilot workers; Cloud Build triggers Docker image rebuilds when /src modules are updated
backend.failure_handling: Checkpoint intermediate Parquet outputs to GCS after each county batch completes; failed Dask tasks write error records to GCS error manifest and skip rather than aborting the full run; run can resume from last completed county

security.auth_design: Google IAM: researchers access Workbench via personal Google identities with project-level roles; Dask workers use a dedicated GCP service account scoped to GCS read/write on research buckets only
security.secrets_management: No secrets manager required; GCS bucket IAM and Workbench service account scopes control all data access; no external API keys stored in code repositories
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Cloud Logging captures all Dask scheduler and worker stdout/stderr; Workbench activity logs record notebook opens and execution events for research record-keeping

data.entities: flood_events (event_id UUID, usgs_gauge_id, peak_flow_cfs, start_date, end_date, duration_hours), gauge_readings (reading_id, gauge_id, timestamp, water_level_ft, flow_cfs, quality_code), inundation_rasters (raster_id, event_id FK, source ENUM satellite/usgs/modeled, resolution_m, gcs_key, processed_at), parametric_triggers (trigger_id, scenario_id FK, gauge_id, threshold_ft, payout_fraction, trigger_type ENUM gauge_height/inundation_depth/composite), residential_exposures (exposure_id, address_hash, lat, lon, tiv, flood_zone ENUM A/AE/AH/X, census_tract), backtesting_results (result_id, trigger_id FK, event_id FK, exposure_id FK, trigger_fired BOOL, parametric_payout, modeled_ground_up_loss, basis_risk_delta), pricing_scenarios (scenario_id, description, trigger_configs JSON, loss_ratio_target, created_by, created_at)
data.storage_design: Raw raster GeoTIFFs in GCS gs://flood-research-raw/; normalized Parquet tables in gs://flood-research-processed/; DuckDB reads Parquet directly via GCS filesystem extension; result snapshots versioned by sprint label in gs://flood-research-results/
data.retention_and_deletion: Raw rasters retained 3 years; result Parquet snapshots retained 5 years versioned by research sprint label; automated GCS lifecycle policy for deletion

devops.deployment_topology: Vertex AI Workbench instance (n1-standard-8, 30GB RAM) as primary interactive environment; ephemeral Dask on GKE Autopilot cluster with up to 20 n1-highmem-4 workers auto-scaling to zero after 15 minutes idle; GCS buckets for all persistent data
devops.ci_cd_design: Cloud Build: on commit to /src, run pytest unit tests with 70% coverage gate then build Dask worker Docker image and push to Artifact Registry; notebooks not CI-tested
devops.observability_stack: GCP Cloud Logging for all Dask job logs; Cloud Monitoring dashboard showing worker CPU utilization and GCS read throughput during batch runs; research_log.csv in GCS for manual run documentation
devops.cost_controls: Dask cluster auto-scales to zero after 15 minutes idle; Workbench instance auto-stops on weeknight schedule; committed use discounts on Workbench baseline compute

target_output.thinking_summary: This parametric flood insurance research prototype centers on geospatial backtesting: ingest USGS gauge time series and satellite inundation rasters, spatially join to residential exposure coordinates, evaluate parametric trigger rules, and compute basis risk. Key architectural choices are DuckDB over Parquet for fast columnar analytics without a managed database, Dask on GKE Autopilot for burst raster processing parallelism, and a checkpoint-based batch design that tolerates partial failures across multi-county runs. No frontend or authentication complexity is needed; Vertex AI Workbench provides the interactive research environment natively within GCP IAM controls.
target_output.title: Parametric Flood Insurance Pricing Research Prototype
target_output.executive_summary: A GCP-hosted research environment for actuarial evaluation of parametric flood insurance triggers, ingesting USGS gauge time series and satellite inundation depth rasters, backtesting trigger thresholds against residential exposure portfolios across historical flood events, and computing basis risk metrics comparing parametric payout accuracy to modeled ground-up losses. Built on Vertex AI Workbench with Dask for distributed compute and DuckDB for columnar analytics over Parquet data stored in GCS.
target_output.architecture_overview: Notebook-driven research architecture on GCP: Vertex AI Workbench (n1-standard-8) provides the interactive Python environment where actuarial researchers execute flood gauge ingestion, raster processing, and backtesting workflows via Jupyter notebooks. Large-scale county-year backtesting jobs are dispatched to an ephemeral Dask cluster on GKE Autopilot, parallelizing raster spatial joins and trigger evaluation loops across up to 20 worker nodes that auto-scale to zero after 15 minutes idle. All data flows through GCS: raw USGS gauge CSVs and GeoTIFF inundation rasters land in ingestion buckets, intermediate Parquet tables accumulate in a processed bucket, and DuckDB reads Parquet directly for actuarial analytics. No HTTP endpoints exist; all compute runs within GCP us-central1 under researcher IAM identities with no public access.
target_output.technology_stack: Compute: Python 3.12, Jupyter notebooks on Vertex AI Workbench (n1-standard-8 30GB RAM), Dask distributed on GKE Autopilot (up to 20 n1-highmem-4 workers). Geospatial: rasterio for GeoTIFF inundation depth extraction, geopandas for residential exposure spatial joins, pyproj for CRS transformations. Analytics: DuckDB reading Parquet via GCS filesystem extension, pandas for actuarial summary statistics. Storage: GCS buckets (raw rasters, processed Parquet, result snapshots). CI/CD: Cloud Build, Artifact Registry. Observability: GCP Cloud Logging, Cloud Monitoring.
target_output.functional_feature_map: USGS Gauge Ingestion: pulls historical instantaneous streamflow and water level time series from USGS NWIS API, normalizes to common Parquet schema with quality flags. Inundation Raster Loader: ingests GeoTIFF depth rasters from satellite vendor, reprojects to WGS84, extracts depth values at residential exposure coordinates via rasterio point sampling. Parametric Trigger Backtester: evaluates gauge-height, inundation-depth, and composite trigger rules against each historical flood event per exposure, recording trigger-fired status and payout fraction. Basis Risk Calculator: compares parametric payouts to modeled ground-up loss estimates, computing over-payment rate, under-payment rate, and mean absolute basis risk by FEMA flood zone and census tract. Pricing Scenario Generator: grid search over trigger threshold combinations to minimize basis risk subject to a target loss ratio constraint.
target_output.system_components: Vertex AI Workbench instance (n1-standard-8) as primary interactive research environment. Ephemeral Dask cluster on GKE Autopilot: scheduler pod plus up to 20 n1-highmem-4 workers auto-scaling to zero after idle. GCS buckets: gs://flood-research-raw/ for USGS CSVs and GeoTIFFs, gs://flood-research-processed/ for normalized Parquet, gs://flood-research-results/ for backtesting outputs versioned by sprint label. Artifact Registry repository for Dask worker Docker images. Cloud Build trigger on /src commits for pytest and image build. Cloud Logging sink for all Dask execution output.
target_output.workflows: USGS Gauge Backtesting Pipeline: researcher configures gauge station list and date range, calls flood_ingestion.fetch_gauge_history() which pages the USGS NWIS REST API with exponential backoff, normalizes flow units to common schema, and writes gauge_readings Parquet to GCS processed bucket. Distributed Multi-County Backtesting: backtest_engine.run_distributed(scenario_config, counties, years) submits a Dask delayed graph where each (county, year, trigger_config) triple is an independent task; scheduler distributes tasks to GKE Autopilot workers; checkpoint Parquet written per county batch; final backtesting_results Parquet consolidated on completion with run metadata logged to research_log.csv.
target_output.data_model: flood_events (event_id UUID PK, usgs_gauge_id VARCHAR, peak_flow_cfs DOUBLE, flood_zone_majority VARCHAR, start_date DATE, end_date DATE, duration_hours INT). gauge_readings (reading_id UUID PK, gauge_id VARCHAR, timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ, water_level_ft DOUBLE, flow_cfs DOUBLE, quality_code VARCHAR). inundation_rasters (raster_id UUID PK, event_id FK, source ENUM satellite/usgs/modeled, resolution_m INT, gcs_key VARCHAR, processed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). parametric_triggers (trigger_id UUID PK, scenario_id FK, gauge_id VARCHAR, threshold_ft DOUBLE, payout_fraction DOUBLE, trigger_type ENUM gauge_height/inundation_depth/composite). residential_exposures (exposure_id UUID PK, address_hash VARCHAR, lat DOUBLE, lon DOUBLE, tiv DOUBLE, flood_zone ENUM A/AE/AH/X, census_tract VARCHAR). backtesting_results (result_id UUID PK, trigger_id FK, event_id FK, exposure_id FK, trigger_fired BOOL, parametric_payout DOUBLE, modeled_ground_up_loss DOUBLE, basis_risk_delta DOUBLE). pricing_scenarios (scenario_id UUID PK, trigger_configs JSON, loss_ratio_target DOUBLE, created_by VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: No HTTP API. Python module interface: flood_ingestion.fetch_gauge_history(gauge_ids, start, end) returns GCS Parquet path. raster_processor.extract_depths(raster_gcs_key, exposures_parquet) returns GCS depth-mapping Parquet path. backtest_engine.run_distributed(scenario_config, counties, years) returns Dask future resolving to GCS results path. basis_risk.summary_report(results_parquet, group_by) returns pandas DataFrame. DuckDB SQL interface for ad hoc queries: SELECT flood_zone, AVG(basis_risk_delta), COUNT(*) FROM read_parquet('gs://flood-research-results/*.parquet') GROUP BY flood_zone.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Google IAM: researchers access Workbench via personal Google identities with roles/notebooks.admin for leads and roles/notebooks.viewer for reviewers. Dask workers use a dedicated GCP service account scoped to GCS read/write on research buckets only with no broader project permissions. GCS buckets: no public access, uniform bucket-level access enforced, Google-managed AES-256 encryption at rest. All GCP internal traffic encrypted in transit. Cloud Logging captures Workbench execution events and Dask job lifecycle events. NFIP regulatory context and state parametric product filing considerations noted; prototype outputs require separate actuarial certification and state approval before any commercial use.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Terraform manages GCS buckets with lifecycle policies, IAM bindings for researchers and Dask service account, GKE Autopilot cluster, and Artifact Registry repo in us-central1. Vertex AI Workbench instance provisioned via gcloud with auto-stop schedule active weekdays 08:00-20:00 PST. Dask cluster provisioned on-demand by notebook code using dask-kubernetes operator; workers pull versioned Docker image from Artifact Registry. Cloud Build pipeline on /src push: runs pytest with 70% coverage gate, builds Dask worker image, pushes to Artifact Registry. No staging environment; single isolated research GCP project. Rollback: all result Parquet outputs prefixed by sprint label in GCS; prior sprint results accessible without re-running.
target_output.observability: GCP Cloud Logging: Dask scheduler and worker stdout/stderr routed to Cloud Logging with job_id custom label for filtering by run. Cloud Monitoring dashboard: Dask worker CPU utilization, GCS read/write throughput MB/s during batch runs, GKE Autopilot pod count over time. Research run log: gs://flood-research-results/research_log.csv records run parameters, duration, county count processed, total exposures evaluated, and outcome notes per session. Key domain metrics tracked per run: flood events processed, parametric trigger evaluations, mean basis risk delta by flood zone, parametric-to-modeled payout ratio.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $1,400/month: Workbench n1-standard-8 ~$600/month with auto-stop schedule, Dask GKE Autopilot ~$600/month for 3-4 multi-county runs per month with workers scaling to zero between runs, GCS storage ~$80/month for 2TB raw rasters and Parquet, Cloud Build ~$20/month. Stays within $2,000/month constraint. Scale path: increase Dask workers to 50 nodes for national-scale backtesting; migrate result tables to BigQuery if DuckDB exceeds Workbench memory limits at 5TB+ Parquet.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): USGS gauge ingestion pipeline, inundation raster loader with rasterio point sampling, parametric trigger backtester for a single coastal county using 10 years of USGS gauge history, basis risk summary report by flood zone -- validates core hypothesis. Phase 2 (3 weeks): Dask distributed backtesting across 50 counties with checkpoint fault tolerance, pricing scenario grid search over trigger thresholds, actuarial summary tables by census tract and flood zone. Phase 3 (3 weeks): Satellite raster vendor ingestion pipeline, composite gauge-height-plus-inundation-depth trigger evaluation, basis risk comparison report between gauge-only and satellite-augmented triggers, actuarial data exports for internal white paper submission.
target_output.development_guidelines: All reusable logic in /src Python modules with pytest unit tests at 70% coverage minimum; notebooks in /notebooks for exploration only with no business logic. Type hints required on all module functions with mypy strict mode enforced in Cloud Build. Black and isort formatting in CI. Research runs documented with parameter config files stored in GCS alongside results. Dask worker Docker image pinned to specific Python 3.12 and rasterio versions in requirements.txt for cross-researcher reproducibility.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: USGS NWIS API rate limits when pulling multi-decade gauge history for large station networks. Mitigation: exponential backoff with jitter and GCS caching of raw CSV downloads to prevent re-fetching. Risk: commercial satellite inundation rasters have inconsistent spatial resolution across flood events, degrading depth extraction accuracy. Mitigation: validate resolution metadata and flag rasters below 10m resolution as low-confidence before spatial join. Tradeoff: DuckDB over BigQuery -- faster prototype iteration but will require migration if dataset exceeds Workbench RAM at national scale. Tradeoff: Dask on GKE Autopilot over Dataflow -- lower overhead for research team but less managed fault tolerance for week-long runs.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose HUC-12 watershed granularity over HUC-8 for trigger zone definitions; finer resolution reduces basis risk at cost of more zone definitions but better matches residential exposure clustering. Confirmed DuckDB over BigQuery for prototype with documented migration path. Established satellite raster vendor as primary inundation source with USGS gauge data as validation cross-check.

metadata.notes: Parametric flood insurance research prototype on GCP; geospatial backtesting with Dask distributed compute and DuckDB analytics over Parquet.

project_goal.value: Modernize legacy SAS-based ETL to a containerized Python/Airflow pipeline transforming raw claims transactions, medical bill line items, and litigation records into feature sets consumed by downstream actuarial severity models, deployed on Snowflake via ECS Fargate
project_goal.rationale: SAS licensing costs and lack of version control make the legacy ETL unmaintainable; Python on Airflow provides testability, CI/CD, and data contract enforcement

target_users.value: Data engineers maintaining the pipeline, actuarial data scientists consuming Snowflake feature tables, and data governance team auditing transformations
target_users.rationale: Three distinct consumer groups with different access needs

project_class.rationale: Batch ETL pipeline with no user-facing frontend; primary artifact is Snowflake feature tables consumed by models

capabilities.rationale: No frontend, auth, or real-time capabilities needed; pure data transformation and orchestration pipeline

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-source claims data fusion, medical billing normalization, litigation feature engineering, and dbt-based data quality contracts combine for high transformation complexity

risk_level.rationale: Pipeline processes confidential claims data including medical billing records and litigation details; errors in feature generation could silently bias actuarial severity models

data_sensitivity.rationale: Claims transactions and medical bill line items contain sensitive claimant health and financial data under carrier confidentiality obligations

external_exposure.rationale: Pipeline runs entirely within AWS VPC; Snowflake accessed via private link; no public endpoints

access_model.value: Snowflake RBAC with separate roles for pipeline writers, data scientist readers, and governance auditors; Airflow access via SSO
access_model.rationale: Separate read/write roles prevent accidental feature table overwrites by data scientists

feature_scope.value: Claims transaction ingestion and normalization, medical bill line item parsing and CPT code mapping, litigation flag derivation, severity feature engineering (paid loss development, expense ratio, claim age bucketing), data quality gate enforcement, Snowflake feature table loading, pipeline run audit trail
feature_scope.rationale: Complete feature engineering scope for actuarial severity models

mvp_scope.value: Claims transaction ingestion, three core severity features (paid loss, claim age bucket, expense ratio), dbt data quality tests, Snowflake loader, GitHub Actions CI with dbt test gate
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum viable replacement for the SAS ETL producing the three most-used severity features

security_baseline.value: Snowflake Private Link, AWS KMS encryption for ECS task secrets, no claims data written to S3 staging without server-side encryption, Airflow connections encrypted in AWS Secrets Manager
security_baseline.rationale: Confidential claims data requires encryption at rest and in transit with no plaintext secrets in code

frontend_stack.value: None; pipeline is headless; Airflow UI provides operational monitoring
frontend_stack.rationale: No consumer-facing frontend needed for a data pipeline

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Apache Airflow 2.9 for orchestration; dbt-core for transformations and data quality contracts
backend_stack.rationale: Airflow provides DAG-based scheduling and task dependency management; dbt enforces data contracts and enables SQL-based transformation testing

data_platform.value: Snowflake as primary analytical warehouse for feature tables; PostgreSQL 16 for Airflow metadata database
data_platform.rationale: Snowflake provides column-level security, time-travel for feature table rollback, and native dbt integration

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate for Airflow workers; Snowflake on AWS us-east-1; GitHub Actions for CI
hosting_target.rationale: ECS Fargate eliminates cluster management; Snowflake on same AWS region as core claims system minimizes egress

privacy_retention_policy.value: Claims data retained 7 years per state insurance department requirements; raw staging data purged after 30 days; feature table snapshots retained for 1 year via Snowflake time-travel
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: State insurance data retention laws mandate 7-year records; staging purge limits exposure of raw confidential data

future_scope.value: Real-time claim severity scoring via Kafka stream, additional medical billing vendor integrations, integration with model training platform for automatic feature store updates
future_scope.rationale: Batch pipeline is the foundation; real-time stream layer is a future phase

constraints.value: Must produce numeric parity with SAS ETL outputs for the top 10 severity features during parallel-run validation; no SAS licenses after migration cutover
constraints.rationale: Actuarial models were trained on SAS-generated features; numeric parity ensures no silent model drift during migration

observability_baseline.value: Grafana dashboards for Airflow DAG success rates and task durations; Prometheus metrics from Airflow StatsD exporter; PagerDuty alerts on DAG failures and SLA misses
observability_baseline.rationale: Corporate monitoring standard for data engineering teams

execution_preference.value: Two-week sprints with data contract reviews at each sprint end
execution_preference.rationale: Sprint cadence aligns with actuarial model retraining schedule

compliance_context.value: HIPAA where medical bill line items are processed; state insurance department data governance requirements; SOC 2 Type II controls for data handling
compliance_context.rationale: Medical billing data triggers HIPAA applicability; SOC 2 required for carrier data handling attestation

project.goal: Replace SAS-based claims severity ETL with a testable Python/Airflow/dbt pipeline producing Snowflake feature tables with numeric parity validation
project.target_users: Data engineers, actuarial data scientists, governance auditors
project.timeline: 6-month migration with parallel-run validation phase

backend.framework: Apache Airflow 2.9 with dbt-core; CeleryExecutor on ECS Fargate
backend.api: No external API; Airflow REST API for CI/CD triggering only

security.auth: Snowflake RBAC with SSO; Airflow via LDAP SSO; AWS Secrets Manager for connections
security.transport: Snowflake Private Link; ECS in private subnets; KMS encryption

data.cache: None; dbt incremental models handle stateful transformation

devops.ci: GitHub Actions with dbt test gate and parity validation
devops.environments: dev (Snowflake dev schema), staging (parallel-run vs SAS), production

constraints.parity: Numeric parity with SAS outputs for top 10 severity features during parallel-run

reasoner_reviews.product: The scope is well-defined: replace SAS ETL with Python/Airflow/dbt while proving numeric parity on the 10 most critical severity features. The parallel-run validation phase is non-negotiable given that actuarial models were trained on SAS outputs. The 6-month timeline is achievable if parity validation gates are automated in CI.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: dbt incremental models on Snowflake are the right transformation layer -- they provide SQL-based testing, data contract enforcement, and lineage documentation that SAS lacked entirely. The critical architectural constraint is the parity validation framework: a dedicated CI step that runs both the new Python pipeline and loads reference SAS outputs, then asserts column-level numeric tolerance. This must be built in sprint 1 or the migration cannot be safely validated.
reasoner_reviews.security: HIPAA applicability for medical bill line items requires a BAA documentation review with Snowflake. Column-level security in Snowflake should mask raw CPT codes from data scientist read role. AWS Secrets Manager for Airflow connections eliminates plaintext credentials from DAG code.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Numeric parity constraint is the highest-risk element; SAS floating-point arithmetic and Python/pandas behavior diverge on edge cases. Recommend establishing a tolerance band (e.g., absolute difference less than $0.01 for monetary amounts) rather than exact equality to avoid false parity failures.

backend.service_design: Airflow DAGs organized by source system: claims_ingest_dag (raw transaction extraction from core claims DB), medical_bills_dag (CPT normalization and line item parsing), litigation_dag (attorney involvement and suit flag derivation), severity_features_dag (dbt model execution and feature table loading), parity_validation_dag (parallel-run comparison against SAS reference outputs)
backend.api_patterns: No external API; Airflow REST API used by GitHub Actions to trigger DAG runs in CI; dbt CLI invoked as BashOperator tasks within Airflow
backend.background_jobs: Nightly severity_features_dag runs at 02:00 EST after upstream claims transaction close; medical_bills_dag runs twice daily at 06:00 and 18:00 to catch late billing submissions; parity_validation_dag runs on every GitHub Actions push to feature branches
backend.failure_handling: Airflow task retries (3 attempts, 10-minute backoff) for transient Snowflake connection errors; dead-letter S3 bucket for unparseable medical bill records; dbt test failures halt DAG and trigger PagerDuty alert before any feature tables are overwritten

security.auth_design: Airflow authenticates to Snowflake via service account credentials stored in AWS Secrets Manager, rotated every 90 days; data scientist access to Snowflake feature schema via SSO-bound role with column masking on raw CPT codes
security.secrets_management: All Airflow connections (Snowflake, core claims DB, AWS services) stored in AWS Secrets Manager with automatic rotation; no connection strings in DAG code or environment variables
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Airflow task execution logs forwarded to CloudWatch Logs with structured JSON; dbt model runs logged to a pipeline_audit_log Snowflake table capturing run_id, model_name, rows_affected, and execution_time; Snowflake query history retained for 365 days

data.entities: claims_transactions (claim_id, policy_id, loss_date, report_date, close_date, paid_loss, case_reserve, expense_paid, claim_type, coverage_code, status), medical_bill_line_items (line_id, claim_id, service_date, cpt_code, revenue_code, billed_amount, allowed_amount, paid_amount, provider_npi, bill_review_vendor), litigation_records (litigation_id, claim_id, attorney_involvement_date, suit_filed_date, verdict_amount, settlement_amount, jurisdiction), feature_sets (feature_set_id, claim_id, run_id, paid_loss_dev_12mo, paid_loss_dev_24mo, claim_age_bucket, expense_ratio, cpt_complexity_score, litigation_flag, med_bill_count, created_at), pipeline_runs (run_id, dag_id, run_date, record_count, quality_gate_passed, parity_delta_max, duration_seconds)
data.storage_design: Claims source DB extracts land in Snowflake raw schema (encrypted, masked CPT codes for non-privileged roles); dbt models transform raw to staging to feature schemas; model_input_snapshots table preserves feature set versions for each model training run
data.retention_and_deletion: Raw claims data in Snowflake raw schema retained 7 years per state requirement; Snowflake time-travel on feature tables enables 90-day rollback; staging data purged after 30 days via Snowflake dynamic data masking policies

devops.deployment_topology: Airflow scheduler and webserver on ECS Fargate (2 vCPU, 4GB each); CeleryExecutor with up to 8 worker tasks on ECS Fargate auto-scaling; PostgreSQL 16 on RDS for Airflow metadata; Snowflake on AWS us-east-1 via Private Link
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: on PR to main, spin up Airflow worker container, run dbt test against dev Snowflake schema, run parity validation DAG against SAS reference dataset, merge blocked if dbt tests fail or parity delta exceeds tolerance; on merge deploy updated DAG files to S3 DAG bucket synced to Airflow
devops.observability_stack: Grafana dashboards: DAG success rate by pipeline, task P95 duration, Snowflake credit consumption, parity delta trend over time; Prometheus via Airflow StatsD exporter; PagerDuty for DAG failures and SLA misses beyond 30 minutes
devops.cost_controls: ECS Fargate workers scale to zero outside nightly batch window; Snowflake warehouse auto-suspend after 5 minutes idle; dbt incremental models process only new/modified records rather than full table scans

ISS-001.title: Parity validation framework not automated in CI
ISS-001.detail: Rounds 1-2 had manual parity validation via ad hoc notebook; CI pipeline did not block merges on parity failures, creating risk of silent feature drift before model retraining.

history.note: Parity validation described in plan but not wired into GitHub Actions CI
Still manual; tolerance band not defined
parity_validation_dag added; tolerance band $0.01 documented; CI blocks merge on failure

ISS-002.title: Snowflake column masking absent from data model plan
ISS-002.detail: Data scientist read role had unrestricted access to raw CPT codes in medical bill line items, violating HIPAA minimum necessary principle.

Snowflake dynamic data masking policy added to CPT code column for data_scientist role
