best_plan.title: Claims Severity Feature Pipeline - Round 3
best_plan.executive_summary: Same as previous_plan; round 3 was the highest-scoring plan prior to this revision.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 4 focus is ISS-003: adding dbt test coverage for CPT normalization edge cases. The fix is targeted: add a dbt singular test asserting CPT codes match the 5-digit numeric regex, a dbt test asserting allowed_amount <= billed_amount on all medical bill line items, and a dbt model description documenting CPT complexity score derivation for the governance audit trail. All other plan sections from round 3 remain correct and are carried forward without regression.

fix_report.action_taken: Added dbt singular test medical_bill_cpt_format_check asserting all cpt_code values match regex '^[0-9]{5}$' across the medical_bill_line_items staging model. Added dbt relationship test asserting allowed_amount <= billed_amount with a custom generic test allowed_not_exceeds_billed. Added dbt model description block to the severity_features model documenting CPT complexity score derivation logic (count of distinct CPT codes per claim, weighted by RVU bucket) for governance audit trail.
fix_report.expected_outcome: CPT normalization regressions will now be caught in CI before feature tables are loaded; dbt test suite covers format, referential integrity, and business rule assertions for medical bill data; governance audit trail requirement satisfied by model documentation

target_output.title: Claims Severity Feature Pipeline
target_output.executive_summary: A Python/Airflow/dbt data pipeline on AWS ECS Fargate and Snowflake replacing a legacy SAS-based ETL, transforming claims transactions, medical bill line items with CPT normalization, and litigation records into actuarial severity feature tables. Automated parity validation in CI ensures numeric equivalence with SAS reference outputs during parallel-run migration. Snowflake column masking, HIPAA-aligned data handling, and dbt data contracts with CPT format and business rule tests ensure data quality and compliance.
target_output.architecture_overview: Batch ETL pipeline orchestrated by Apache Airflow 2.9 on ECS Fargate with CeleryExecutor. Five DAGs handle the full data flow: claims_ingest_dag extracts raw transactions from the core claims PostgreSQL system via a secure VPC connection, medical_bills_dag parses and CPT-normalizes line items from billing vendor SFTP drops, litigation_dag derives attorney involvement and suit flags, severity_features_dag invokes dbt models to build the analytical feature layer in Snowflake, and parity_validation_dag compares new feature outputs to SAS reference values with $0.01 tolerance assertion. All data transits within the AWS us-east-1 VPC; Snowflake connected via Private Link with no internet exposure.
target_output.technology_stack: Orchestration: Apache Airflow 2.9 with CeleryExecutor on ECS Fargate (Python 3.12). Transformation: dbt-core 1.8 with Snowflake adapter, dbt-expectations for custom test assertions. Warehouse: Snowflake with column-level security and time-travel. Metadata DB: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS for Airflow. Secrets: AWS Secrets Manager with 90-day rotation. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with dbt test gate and parity validation DAG. Observability: Grafana, Prometheus via Airflow StatsD, PagerDuty. Infrastructure: Terraform on AWS ECS Fargate, Snowflake Private Link.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Claims Transaction Ingestion: nightly extract of claims_transactions from core claims PostgreSQL via SQLAlchemy, loaded to Snowflake raw.claims_raw with incremental merge on claim_id. Medical Bill CPT Normalization: parse multi-line billing vendor SFTP files, validate CPT code format (5-digit numeric regex test), assert allowed_amount <= billed_amount, compute per-claim CPT complexity score weighted by RVU bucket. Litigation Flag Derivation: join claims to litigation_records on claim_id, derive binary litigation_flag and days_to_suit features. Severity Feature Engineering: dbt models build paid_loss_dev_12mo, paid_loss_dev_24mo, claim_age_bucket (0-30/31-90/91-180/181-365/365-plus days), expense_ratio, cpt_complexity_score, med_bill_count into feature_sets table. Parity Validation: automated CI step loads SAS reference CSV and asserts column-level absolute difference less than tolerance.
target_output.system_components: Airflow scheduler (ECS Fargate, 2 vCPU 4GB) and webserver (2 vCPU 4GB). CeleryExecutor with up to 8 ECS Fargate task workers (1 vCPU 2GB each) auto-scaling on task queue depth. Airflow metadata PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ. Snowflake warehouse (X-Small, auto-suspend 5 min) with separate raw, staging, and feature schemas. Snowflake Private Link endpoint in VPC. S3 DAG bucket synced to Airflow. GitHub Actions runners for CI. Grafana/Prometheus on ECS for observability. PagerDuty for alert routing.
target_output.workflows: Nightly Severity Feature Run: claims_ingest_dag starts at 01:00 EST, extracts new and modified claims_transactions since last watermark, loads to Snowflake raw schema; medical_bills_dag at 02:00 EST parses SFTP billing files, runs CPT format assertion, loads normalized line items; severity_features_dag at 03:00 EST invokes dbt run and dbt test; on test failure the DAG terminates and PagerDuty fires before feature table overwrite. CI Parity Validation: on GitHub PR to main, parity_validation_dag runs against dev Snowflake schema, loads SAS reference CSV, asserts column-level absolute difference within tolerance for all 10 severity features; merge is blocked if any column exceeds tolerance threshold.
target_output.data_model: claims_transactions (claim_id VARCHAR PK, policy_id, loss_date DATE, report_date DATE, close_date DATE, paid_loss DECIMAL(12,2), case_reserve DECIMAL(12,2), expense_paid DECIMAL(12,2), claim_type VARCHAR, coverage_code VARCHAR, status ENUM open/closed/reopened). medical_bill_line_items (line_id VARCHAR PK, claim_id FK, service_date DATE, cpt_code VARCHAR -- masked for data_scientist role via Snowflake dynamic masking policy, revenue_code VARCHAR, billed_amount DECIMAL(10,2), allowed_amount DECIMAL(10,2) -- dbt test: allowed_amount <= billed_amount, paid_amount DECIMAL(10,2), provider_npi VARCHAR, bill_review_vendor VARCHAR). litigation_records (litigation_id, claim_id FK, attorney_involvement_date DATE, suit_filed_date DATE, verdict_amount DECIMAL(12,2), settlement_amount DECIMAL(12,2), jurisdiction VARCHAR). feature_sets (feature_set_id UUID, claim_id FK, run_id FK, paid_loss_dev_12mo DECIMAL, paid_loss_dev_24mo DECIMAL, claim_age_bucket ENUM 0-30/31-90/91-180/181-365/365-plus, expense_ratio DECIMAL(6,4), cpt_complexity_score DOUBLE, litigation_flag BOOL, med_bill_count INT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). pipeline_runs (run_id UUID PK, dag_id, run_date, record_count, quality_gate_passed BOOL, parity_delta_max DECIMAL, duration_seconds INT).
target_output.api_design: No consumer-facing API. Internal interfaces: Airflow REST API POST /api/v1/dags/{dag_id}/dagRuns (CI trigger with JWT auth). dbt CLI: dbt run --select severity_features --vars '{run_id: xxx}'; dbt test --select medical_bill_line_items (includes cpt_format_check singular test and allowed_not_exceeds_billed generic test). Snowflake: feature tables exposed as named views to downstream model training platform via Snowflake data sharing.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Snowflake Private Link: no internet-routable Snowflake traffic; all connections from ECS VPC. AWS KMS: ECS task execution role uses KMS-encrypted secrets from Secrets Manager for Snowflake service account credentials, rotated every 90 days. Snowflake RBAC: pipeline_writer role for Airflow workers (INSERT/UPDATE on raw and staging schemas only); data_scientist_reader role for actuarial consumers (SELECT on feature schema with dynamic masking on cpt_code column); governance_auditor role (SELECT on all schemas, dbt model metadata). HIPAA: Snowflake BAA in place; CPT code column-level masking satisfies minimum necessary access principle; medical bill data retained 7 years per state insurance regulation. SOC 2 controls: Airflow audit log to CloudWatch, Snowflake query history retained 365 days, pipeline_audit_log table in Snowflake for dbt run metadata.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Infrastructure as code: Terraform manages ECS cluster, task definitions, RDS instance, Secrets Manager secrets, Snowflake Private Link endpoint, and S3 DAG bucket in us-east-1. Environments: dev (Snowflake dev database, ECS dev service, feature flags for new dbt models), staging (parallel-run against SAS reference, parity gate enforced), production (nightly batch, PagerDuty alerting). GitHub Actions CI: on PR, checkout, run dbt test against dev Snowflake schema, execute parity_validation_dag via Airflow REST API, assert parity delta, block merge on failure or dbt test failure. Deployment: merge to main updates S3 DAG bucket triggering Airflow DAG sync; no ECS task restarts required for DAG-only changes. Rollback: Snowflake time-travel allows 90-day feature table rollback; Airflow DAG version stored in S3 versioned bucket.
target_output.observability: Grafana dashboards: DAG success rate by pipeline (7-day rolling), task P95 duration per DAG, Snowflake credit consumption by warehouse, parity delta trend per severity feature over time. Prometheus metrics via Airflow StatsD exporter: airflow_dag_run_duration, airflow_task_failure_count, airflow_executor_queue_size. PagerDuty alerts: DAG failure on severity_features_dag or medical_bills_dag, SLA miss beyond 30 minutes from expected completion, parity delta exceeding $0.01 tolerance in CI. Snowflake query history dashboard for governance auditors showing which roles accessed feature tables and when.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $2,800/month: ECS Fargate Airflow scheduler/webserver ~$400, worker tasks ~$600 (nightly 3-hour window, 8 workers), RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ ~$300, Snowflake credits ~$1,200/month at X-Small warehouse with auto-suspend, S3 and data transfer ~$100. Worker auto-scaling handles claim volume growth to 500K claims/month without cost step-change; Snowflake warehouse can scale to Small for month-end peak processing.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (8 weeks): claims_ingest_dag and medical_bills_dag with CPT normalization and dbt test suite (cpt_format_check, allowed_not_exceeds_billed, not-null, row count), parity_validation_dag in CI for top 3 severity features, dev and staging environments. Phase 2 (4 weeks): litigation_dag, full severity_features_dag with all 10 features, parity validation expanded to all 10 features, Snowflake column masking for CPT codes, staging parallel-run sign-off by actuarial team. Phase 3 (4 weeks): production cutover with SAS parallel run, SAS retirement after 2-week parity confirmation, Grafana observability dashboards, governance audit log documentation finalized.
target_output.development_guidelines: All dbt models must include a description block documenting derivation logic for governance audit trail; CPT complexity score derivation must be explicitly documented (count of distinct CPT codes per claim weighted by CMS RVU bucket). New dbt tests required for every business rule in medical_bill_line_items and feature_sets models. GitHub PR requires 2 approvals from data engineering team for changes to severity_features dbt model. Feature table schema changes require actuarial sign-off before merge. dbt-expectations library for custom test assertions; no ad hoc SQL in Airflow operators -- all transformations via dbt models.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: SAS floating-point arithmetic diverges from Python/pandas on large claim amounts, causing false parity failures. Mitigation: tolerance band of $0.01 absolute difference for monetary features; document known divergence cases in the parity validation runbook. Risk: Snowflake Private Link misconfiguration could expose claims data over the public internet. Mitigation: Terraform enforces Private Link endpoint policy; automated compliance check in CI verifies no public endpoint attached to Snowflake account. Tradeoff: dbt-core over dbt Cloud -- lower cost but requires self-managed CI integration; acceptable given in-house data engineering capability. Tradeoff: CeleryExecutor over KubernetesExecutor -- simpler ECS Fargate setup but less per-task isolation; acceptable for batch workload with known task shapes.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed $0.01 absolute tolerance for monetary parity assertions, not exact equality, to accommodate SAS/Python floating-point divergence. Confirmed Snowflake time-travel as rollback mechanism for feature tables instead of S3 snapshot. Confirmed dbt-core over dbt Cloud to avoid SaaS dependency on transformation layer. CPT complexity score derivation documented: distinct CPT codes per claim weighted by CMS Relative Value Unit bucket (low/medium/high).

metadata.notes: Round 4 revision resolves ISS-003 by adding dbt CPT normalization test coverage; two prior issues resolved in rounds 2-3.

project_goal.value: Replace flat VPN architecture with a zero-trust network using identity-aware proxies and micro-segmented access to actuarial workstations and policy administration systems at a captive insurance management firm
project_goal.rationale: Flat VPN grants lateral movement across all internal systems after a single credential compromise; zero-trust enforces per-session, per-resource authorization with device posture verification

target_users.value: Actuaries, underwriters, and IT administrators accessing policy administration systems and pricing databases; external auditors with time-limited read-only access
target_users.rationale: Primary internal users plus regulated external auditor access with strong separation

project_class.rationale: Platform-level network architecture replacement using Terraform and access control infrastructure; no application code

capabilities.rationale: Identity-aware proxy, certificate infrastructure, and Terraform automation; no frontend or data pipeline capabilities

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-layer zero-trust stack combining Keycloak OIDC, HCP Vault PKI, identity-aware proxy policies, device posture verification, and privileged session recording

risk_level.rationale: Misconfiguration of access policies could lock out users from policy administration systems during business hours, causing operational outage; NYDFS 23 NYCRR 500 penalties for compliance failures

data_sensitivity.rationale: Controls access to actuarial pricing models, policy data, and reinsurance treaty information classified as confidential business data

external_exposure.rationale: Zero-trust proxy is internal-facing; no public internet endpoints; auditor access via invited device enrollment only

access_model.value: OIDC identity assertions from Keycloak with device posture checks via device trust agent; time-limited session tokens; no persistent network tunnels
access_model.rationale: Session-based access with device posture eliminates standing VPN access and reduces blast radius of compromised credentials

feature_scope.value: Identity-aware proxy with OIDC integration, Keycloak identity provider with MFA, device trust agent for posture scoring, micro-segment access policies per resource group, HCP Vault PKI for certificate issuance, privileged session recording for policy administration system access, audit trail, external auditor enrollment workflow
feature_scope.rationale: Complete zero-trust capability set per NYDFS 23 NYCRR 500 requirements

mvp_scope.value: Identity-aware proxy protecting policy administration system and actuarial pricing database; Keycloak with MFA; basic device posture check (OS version, disk encryption); privileged session recording for admin access
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum viable coverage of highest-risk systems before VPN deprecation

security_baseline.value: mTLS between all internal services; HCP Vault PKI for certificate lifecycle; Keycloak MFA required for all access; session tokens expire after 8 hours; privileged sessions recorded and indexed
security_baseline.rationale: NYDFS 23 NYCRR 500 mandates multi-factor authentication, encryption in transit, and privileged access monitoring

frontend_stack.value: None; Keycloak provides authentication UI; identity-aware proxy is network infrastructure with no custom frontend
frontend_stack.rationale: Infrastructure project; no custom application frontend required

backend_stack.value: Terraform with HCP Vault for certificate management; custom Go micro-service for device trust scoring
backend_stack.rationale: Terraform manages all infrastructure declaratively; HCP Vault handles PKI and secrets; Go for lightweight device trust agent and scoring service

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for Keycloak identity store; audit trail events written to a dedicated PostgreSQL audit database
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL is sufficient for Keycloak realm data and structured audit event storage at captive firm scale

hosting_target.value: Bare-metal Kubernetes on-premise for identity-aware proxy and Keycloak; HCP Vault on HCP cloud; Terraform Cloud for state management
hosting_target.rationale: On-premise Kubernetes keeps identity infrastructure within firm network perimeter; HCP Vault cloud reduces PKI operational burden

privacy_retention_policy.value: Privileged session recordings retained 3 years per NYDFS requirements; access audit logs retained 6 years for SOC 2 and ISO 27001 evidence
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Regulatory retention mandates for cybersecurity evidence

future_scope.value: Extend zero-trust to all SaaS integrations via OIDC federation; add continuous authorization with real-time risk scoring; integrate SIEM for anomaly-triggered session termination
future_scope.rationale: Phase 1 covers on-premise systems; SaaS and real-time scoring are phase 2

constraints.value: Must not disrupt policy administration system availability during business hours 08:00-18:00 EST; phased VPN deprecation with 90-day parallel operation window; on-premise hardware budget $150K
constraints.rationale: Policy administration downtime has direct revenue impact; budget constrains hardware choices for on-premise Kubernetes nodes

observability_baseline.value: Grafana and Prometheus on bare-metal Kubernetes; identity-aware proxy access logs to Elasticsearch; PagerDuty for proxy availability alerts
observability_baseline.rationale: Existing monitoring stack extended to cover zero-trust infrastructure

execution_preference.value: Infrastructure-as-code with staged rollout: shadow mode first, then enforce mode per resource group
execution_preference.rationale: Shadow mode allows policy validation without access disruption before enforcement

compliance_context.value: NYDFS Cybersecurity Regulation 23 NYCRR 500 mandating MFA, privileged access monitoring, and audit trail; SOC 2 Type II access control requirements; ISO 27001 access management controls
compliance_context.rationale: Captive insurance firm regulated by New York DFS with annual cybersecurity certification requirement

project.goal: Replace flat VPN with zero-trust identity-aware proxy protecting actuarial and policy administration systems
project.target_users: Actuaries, underwriters, IT admins, external auditors
project.timeline: 9-month project with 90-day VPN parallel operation window

backend.runtime: Go 1.22 for device trust agent and scoring service
backend.framework: Terraform for all infrastructure; HCP Vault for PKI and secrets
backend.api: No public API; identity-aware proxy exposes internal resource endpoints only

security.auth: Keycloak OIDC with MFA (TOTP and WebAuthn); device posture via Go trust agent
security.transport: mTLS between all internal services; TLS 1.3 for client connections; HCP Vault-issued certificates

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 for Keycloak realm and audit events
data.cache: Redis for session token cache and device trust score cache

devops.ci: GitLab CI for Terraform plan/apply with manual approval gate for production
devops.hosting: Bare-metal Kubernetes on-premise; HCP Vault cloud; Terraform Cloud
devops.environments: dev (shadow mode), staging (enforce mode limited scope), production (phased rollout by resource group)

constraints.availability: No policy administration system downtime during business hours

open_questions.auditor_enrollment: Process for external auditor device enrollment and certificate issuance for temporary access
open_questions.posture_score_threshold: Minimum device trust score required for access to highest-sensitivity pricing database

reasoner_reviews.product: The scope is well-bounded: replace VPN with zero-trust identity-aware proxy protecting the two highest-risk internal systems (policy administration and actuarial pricing database) as the MVP. The 90-day parallel operation window is critical for user trust and rollback capability. External auditor enrollment workflow needs a clear design before go-live.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The architecture stack is sound: Keycloak for identity, HCP Vault for PKI, identity-aware proxy enforcing OIDC session tokens, device trust agent for posture scoring. The key infrastructure decision is whether to run the identity-aware proxy as a Kubernetes DaemonSet (sidecar per node) or as a centralized gateway. Given the small number of protected resources and on-premise Kubernetes, a centralized gateway pattern is simpler and easier to audit. mTLS between all internal services requires all protected systems to present HCP Vault-issued certificates, which must be pre-provisioned before VPN deprecation.
reasoner_reviews.security: NYDFS 23 NYCRR 500 mandates privileged access monitoring and annual penetration testing. Privileged session recording for policy administration system access is mandatory. The device trust score threshold for the pricing database should be higher than for general internal tools. HCP Vault PKI with 90-day certificate rotation eliminates the risk of long-lived VPN credentials.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 90-day VPN parallel window and no business-hours downtime are the two hardest constraints. Shadow mode deployment -- where the proxy logs access decisions but does not enforce them -- is the right first step to validate policies without risk. Enforced cutover to a resource group must happen outside business hours with rollback plan documented.

backend.service_design: Identity-aware proxy (centralized Kubernetes Deployment with 3 replicas, HPA on connection count) handling OIDC token validation, device trust assertion, and reverse proxy to protected resources. Device Trust Agent (Go binary, deployed to each workstation via MDM) collecting OS version, disk encryption status, and patch level, posting posture score to Trust Scoring Service. Trust Scoring Service (Go microservice on Kubernetes) computing weighted device trust score (0-100) and caching in Redis with 5-minute TTL.
backend.api_patterns: Identity-aware proxy: no public API; accepts inbound HTTPS from internal clients, validates OIDC bearer token against Keycloak JWKS endpoint, retrieves device trust score from Redis, evaluates access policy, proxies to upstream resource. Trust Scoring Service: POST /v1/posture (device agent check-in), GET /v1/score/{device_id} (proxy score lookup).
backend.failure_handling: Identity-aware proxy fails closed: if Keycloak JWKS endpoint is unreachable, deny all requests and alert PagerDuty. If Redis is unavailable, proxy falls back to fresh device trust score fetch from Trust Scoring Service. HCP Vault certificate expiry monitored; auto-renew triggered 14 days before expiry with alert on renewal failure.

security.auth_design: Keycloak realm with OIDC authorization code flow; MFA required for all users (TOTP as primary, WebAuthn as secondary); access tokens expire after 15 minutes with refresh tokens valid 8 hours. External auditors enrolled via invitation with temporary Keycloak realm and dedicated device certificate from HCP Vault with 30-day validity.
security.authorization_model: Policy matrix: actuary role grants access to pricing database (requires device trust score >= 80) and policy admin system; underwriter role grants policy admin read-write (device trust score >= 70); IT admin role grants all systems with privileged session recording; auditor role grants read-only on policy admin system for 30 days.
security.secrets_management: HCP Vault PKI: issues TLS certificates to all internal services with 90-day rotation; issues client certificates to enrolled workstations. HCP Vault KV: stores Keycloak client secrets, database credentials. All secrets injected into Kubernetes workloads via Vault Agent sidecar; no secrets in ConfigMaps or environment variables.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Every access decision (allow/deny) logged to audit PostgreSQL table with user_id, device_id, resource, decision, trust_score, timestamp. Privileged sessions (IT admin and actuary pricing database access) recorded via session proxy and stored to NFS with 3-year retention. Keycloak audit events forwarded to Elasticsearch. Monthly access review report generated from audit DB for NYDFS compliance evidence.

data.entities: access_policies (policy_id, resource_id FK, role, min_trust_score, session_recording_required BOOL, created_at), identity_contexts (context_id, user_id, device_id, oidc_token_jti, trust_score, issued_at, expires_at), device_trust_scores (score_id, device_id, os_version, disk_encrypted BOOL, patch_level_days INT, score INT, evaluated_at), micro_segments (segment_id, name, resource_endpoints JSON, allowed_roles JSON), audit_trails (event_id UUID, user_id, device_id, resource_id, action ENUM allow/deny, trust_score, policy_id FK, timestamp), privileged_sessions (session_id UUID, user_id, resource_id, recording_path, start_time, end_time, terminated_by ENUM timeout/user/admin)
data.storage_design: Keycloak realm data in PostgreSQL 16 primary with streaming replica. Audit events in a separate PostgreSQL 16 audit database (append-only, no UPDATE/DELETE grants to application role). Privileged session recordings on encrypted NFS mount. Redis 7 for session token cache and device trust score cache (5-minute TTL).
data.retention_and_deletion: Audit trail records retained 6 years, then archived to cold storage. Privileged session recordings retained 3 years per NYDFS mandate. Expired identity contexts purged after 24 hours via scheduled Kubernetes Job.

devops.deployment_topology: Bare-metal Kubernetes cluster (3 control plane nodes, 5 worker nodes) on-premise. Identity-aware proxy: Deployment with 3 replicas, LoadBalancer service fronted by F5 internal load balancer. Keycloak: StatefulSet with 2 replicas backed by PostgreSQL 16 primary/replica. Trust Scoring Service: Deployment 2 replicas. Redis: 3-node cluster mode. HCP Vault managed by HashiCorp; Kubernetes auth method for workload identity.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI: Terraform plan on MR, human approval gate for Terraform apply to production. Helm chart versioning for all Kubernetes workloads. Shadow mode enabled via feature flag in identity-aware proxy ConfigMap (LOG_ONLY=true). Enforced mode cutover executed via ConfigMap update outside business hours with automated health check verifying proxy connectivity before old VPN route is decommissioned.
devops.observability_stack: Grafana dashboards: proxy request rate, allow/deny ratio by resource, trust score distribution, Keycloak authentication success/failure rate, certificate expiry countdown. Prometheus via Kubernetes ServiceMonitor. PagerDuty alerts: proxy unhealthy (3 failed health checks), Keycloak authentication error spike, certificate expiry within 7 days, Trust Scoring Service unreachable.
devops.rollback_strategy: VPN coexists for 90 days; rollback to VPN at any time by updating routing rules. Per-resource-group enforce mode rollback: update ConfigMap ENFORCE_GROUPS to remove group, restoring shadow mode within 60 seconds without pod restart.

target_output.thinking_summary: This zero-trust infrastructure project replaces a flat VPN with an identity-aware proxy enforcing per-session, per-resource authorization based on OIDC identity and device posture score. The critical design decisions are: centralized gateway proxy pattern (simpler audit trail vs DaemonSet), HCP Vault PKI for certificate lifecycle eliminating long-lived VPN credentials, shadow mode before enforcement for safe rollout, and 90-day VPN coexistence for rollback. NYDFS 23 NYCRR 500 mandates drive privileged session recording and 6-year audit trail retention.
target_output.title: Zero-Trust Network Infrastructure for Captive Insurance Management
target_output.executive_summary: A Terraform-managed zero-trust network infrastructure replacing a flat VPN architecture at a captive insurance management firm. An identity-aware proxy enforces OIDC session tokens and device trust scores before granting per-session access to policy administration systems and actuarial pricing databases. Keycloak with mandatory MFA issues identity assertions; HCP Vault PKI manages 90-day rotating certificates for all internal services; Go-based device trust agents evaluate workstation posture. Privileged session recording and a 6-year audit trail satisfy NYDFS 23 NYCRR 500 cybersecurity certification requirements.
target_output.architecture_overview: Centralized identity-aware proxy pattern on bare-metal Kubernetes: inbound HTTPS from actuarial workstations terminates at the proxy, which validates the OIDC bearer token against Keycloak's JWKS endpoint, retrieves the device trust score from a Redis cache populated by the Go trust agent on each workstation, evaluates the access policy matrix, and reverse-proxies to the upstream protected resource (policy administration system or actuarial pricing database) over mTLS using HCP Vault-issued certificates. Shadow mode (LOG_ONLY=true) enables policy validation without enforcement during the 90-day VPN parallel operation window. Per-resource-group enforcement is activated via ConfigMap update outside business hours with VPN fallback available at all times during the transition.
target_output.technology_stack: Infrastructure: Terraform with Terraform Cloud for state; Helm for Kubernetes workload management; GitLab CI with manual Terraform apply gate. Identity: Keycloak 24 on bare-metal Kubernetes (OIDC/OAuth2, MFA via TOTP and WebAuthn). PKI and Secrets: HCP Vault managed service (PKI secrets engine for 90-day rotating certificates, KV for service secrets, Kubernetes auth method). Device Trust: Go 1.22 trust agent (MDM-deployed), Go trust scoring microservice. Proxy: open-source identity-aware proxy (e.g., Pomerium) on Kubernetes. Data: PostgreSQL 16 for Keycloak and audit DB; Redis 7 cluster for session/score cache. Observability: Grafana, Prometheus, PagerDuty, Elasticsearch for Keycloak audit events.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Identity-Aware Proxy: validates OIDC token, evaluates device trust score against policy matrix, proxies to resource, logs every access decision. Device Trust Agent: Go binary deployed via MDM to actuarial workstations, collects OS version, disk encryption flag, patch age, posts posture to Trust Scoring Service. Trust Scoring Service: computes weighted device trust score (0-100); disk encryption +40 points, patch age <7 days +30 points, approved OS version +30 points; cached in Redis 5 minutes. Keycloak with MFA: OIDC authorization server, TOTP and WebAuthn second factors, external auditor temporary realm with 30-day expiry. HCP Vault PKI: issues 90-day TLS certificates to all Kubernetes services and workstation client certificates for mTLS. Privileged Session Recording: IT admin and pricing database sessions proxied through session recorder, recordings stored to encrypted NFS with 3-year retention. External Auditor Enrollment: invitation-driven Keycloak account creation, HCP Vault-issued 30-day client certificate, read-only access policy on policy administration system.
target_output.system_components: Bare-metal Kubernetes cluster (3 control plane, 5 worker nodes on-premise). Identity-aware proxy Deployment (3 replicas, HPA on connection count) behind internal F5 load balancer. Keycloak StatefulSet (2 replicas) backed by PostgreSQL 16 primary with streaming replica. Trust Scoring Service Deployment (2 replicas). Device Trust Agent Go binary on 80+ actuarial and underwriter workstations via MDM. Redis 7 cluster mode (3 nodes) for token and score caching. HCP Vault managed service on HCP cloud with Kubernetes auth method. PostgreSQL 16 audit database (append-only) on dedicated bare-metal node. Encrypted NFS mount for privileged session recording storage. Elasticsearch for Keycloak audit event forwarding.
target_output.workflows: Actuary Pricing Database Access: actuary opens browser, identity-aware proxy redirects to Keycloak login, actuary authenticates with password and TOTP, Keycloak issues OIDC access token (15-min expiry), device trust agent on workstation fetches cached score from Trust Scoring Service (score >= 80 required for pricing DB), proxy validates token and score, proxies request to pricing database over mTLS -- session is recorded and audit event written to PostgreSQL audit DB. External Auditor Time-Limited Access: CISO sends auditor invitation email with Keycloak enrollment link, auditor completes enrollment with MFA setup, HCP Vault issues 30-day client certificate to auditor device, auditor accesses policy administration system read-only; at 30-day expiry Keycloak account and certificate are automatically revoked.
target_output.data_model: access_policies (policy_id UUID PK, resource_id FK, role VARCHAR, min_trust_score INT, session_recording_required BOOL, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). identity_contexts (context_id UUID PK, user_id VARCHAR, device_id VARCHAR, oidc_token_jti VARCHAR UNIQUE, trust_score INT, issued_at TIMESTAMPTZ, expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ). device_trust_scores (score_id UUID PK, device_id VARCHAR, os_version VARCHAR, disk_encrypted BOOL, patch_level_days INT, score INT, evaluated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). micro_segments (segment_id UUID PK, name VARCHAR, resource_endpoints JSONB, allowed_roles JSONB). audit_trails (event_id UUID PK, user_id VARCHAR, device_id VARCHAR, resource_id VARCHAR, action ENUM allow/deny, trust_score INT, policy_id FK, timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ). privileged_sessions (session_id UUID PK, user_id VARCHAR, resource_id VARCHAR, recording_gcs_path VARCHAR, start_time TIMESTAMPTZ, end_time TIMESTAMPTZ, terminated_by ENUM timeout/user/admin). certificate_authorities (ca_id UUID PK, name VARCHAR, vault_mount_path VARCHAR, not_after DATE, issuing_policy VARCHAR).
target_output.api_design: Identity-aware proxy: no public API; accepts inbound HTTPS from enrolled workstations, validates Authorization: Bearer <oidc_token>, responds 200 (proxied resource), 401 (invalid token), 403 (policy deny with logged reason), 503 (upstream unreachable). Trust Scoring Service: POST /v1/posture {device_id, os_version, disk_encrypted, patch_level_days} returns {score: 85, cached_until: ISO8601}. GET /v1/score/{device_id} returns cached score or 404 if not evaluated. HCP Vault API: used by workloads for certificate issuance at startup via Vault Agent sidecar -- no application code calls Vault directly.
target_output.security_and_compliance: NYDFS 23 NYCRR 500 controls: MFA enforced for all user access (TOTP + WebAuthn), privileged access monitoring via session recording, access audit trail retained 6 years in append-only PostgreSQL audit DB, annual penetration test scope includes identity-aware proxy and Keycloak. mTLS: all internal service-to-service communication uses HCP Vault-issued certificates with 90-day rotation and automated renewal 14 days before expiry; certificate expiry alert in Grafana. Session management: OIDC access tokens expire after 15 minutes; refresh tokens expire after 8 hours; Redis token cache invalidated on token revocation event from Keycloak. Device posture: pricing database access requires trust score >= 80 (all three posture checks passing); policy admin system requires score >= 70. External auditor access: temporary Keycloak realm with 30-day account and certificate auto-expiry, read-only access policy, all sessions recorded. SOC 2 Type II and ISO 27001: monthly access review report from audit DB, quarterly certificate inventory from HCP Vault PKI.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Terraform manages: bare-metal Kubernetes cluster configuration, Helm releases for all workloads, HCP Vault PKI mount configuration, Keycloak realm and client configuration, PostgreSQL databases, Redis cluster, Elasticsearch. GitLab CI pipeline: Terraform plan on merge request with automated policy check (no security group wildcard rules), human approval gate before Terraform apply to production. Shadow mode rollout: ConfigMap LOG_ONLY=true for each resource group, monitor allow/deny ratio and trust score distribution for 2 weeks, then flip ENFORCE=true outside 08:00-18:00 EST window. VPN decommission: per-resource-group; decommission only after 2 weeks of enforce mode with zero proxy errors. Rollback: revert resource group to shadow mode by patching ConfigMap; VPN routes remain active throughout 90-day window.
target_output.observability: Grafana dashboards: identity-aware proxy request rate and P99 latency, allow/deny ratio by resource (target: deny rate < 2% after policy tuning), trust score distribution histogram, Keycloak authentication success/failure rate, HCP Vault certificate expiry countdown per service. Prometheus metrics via Kubernetes ServiceMonitor: proxy_requests_total{resource, outcome}, trust_score_histogram_bucket, keycloak_login_attempts_total{result}. PagerDuty critical alerts: proxy pods unhealthy (< 2 healthy), Keycloak cluster login error rate > 5% over 5 minutes, certificate expiry within 7 days for any service. Elasticsearch: Keycloak authentication audit events queryable for NYDFS compliance investigations.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: On-premise hardware: $150K for 8-node bare-metal Kubernetes cluster (3 control plane, 5 worker) at standard server pricing. Ongoing: HCP Vault managed service ~$400/month for PKI and KV at captive firm scale, Terraform Cloud ~$50/month, Elasticsearch ~$200/month managed. Scales to 500 concurrent users without hardware changes; HPA on proxy Deployment adds replicas automatically on connection surge.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (10 weeks): Terraform base infrastructure (Kubernetes cluster config, Keycloak, HCP Vault PKI, PostgreSQL audit DB), device trust agent deployed to 10 pilot actuarial workstations via MDM, identity-aware proxy in shadow mode for policy administration system, audit trail logging validated. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Trust Scoring Service deployment, proxy enforced for policy administration system (outside business hours cutover), pricing database shadow mode, external auditor enrollment workflow tested. Phase 3 (6 weeks): Pricing database enforce mode cutover, privileged session recording activated for all pricing DB and IT admin sessions, VPN deprecation for enrolled resource groups, NYDFS compliance evidence package assembled.
target_output.development_guidelines: All infrastructure changes via Terraform; no manual kubectl apply or cloud console changes to resources managed by Terraform. Identity-aware proxy access policy matrix stored in version-controlled YAML, not in database, to enable GitLab CI policy review. Go device trust agent and Trust Scoring Service follow table-driven unit tests for posture scoring logic. Keycloak realm configuration exported to JSON and stored in GitLab for disaster recovery. Certificate rotation failures must be treated as P0 incidents with automated rollback to previous certificate before rotation window closes.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Keycloak JWKS endpoint outage causes all proxy validation to fail, locking out all users. Mitigation: proxy caches JWKS public keys locally for 15 minutes; Keycloak runs as 2-replica StatefulSet with automated health checks. Risk: device trust agent MDM deployment delays on contractor workstations that are not MDM-managed. Mitigation: certificate-only enrollment path for non-MDM devices with lower default trust score; pricing database access blocked until MDM enrollment. Tradeoff: centralized gateway proxy over per-workload sidecar (Istio-style) -- simpler audit trail and policy management at captive firm scale; insufficient service mesh complexity to justify Istio overhead. Tradeoff: HCP Vault managed service over self-hosted Vault -- reduced operational burden for PKI at cost of HCP dependency; acceptable given small infrastructure team.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed centralized gateway proxy pattern over per-workload sidecar; audit trail simplicity outweighs any micro-segmentation benefit at this scale. Confirmed minimum device trust score of 80 for pricing database, 70 for policy administration system. Confirmed 90-day certificate rotation via HCP Vault PKI with 14-day advance renewal alert. External auditor enrollment via invitation-driven temporary Keycloak realm with 30-day auto-expiry confirmed with CISO.

metadata.notes: Zero-trust network infrastructure replacing VPN at captive insurance firm; NYDFS 23 NYCRR 500 compliance with Keycloak, HCP Vault PKI, and device posture scoring.

profile.domain: Insurance Rating SDK - ISO ERC Rate Tables

project_goal.value: Open-source Python SDK for insurance developers to interact with ISO Electronic Rating Content rate tables, providing fast in-memory rating lookups, territory resolution, and ISO circular parsing with optimized data structures for high-throughput quoting engines
project_goal.rationale: Existing ISO ERC tooling requires manual SQL queries or expensive commercial libraries; open-source SDK removes that dependency for carriers and MGAs building in-house rating engines

target_users.value: Insurance software developers and actuarial engineers at carriers, MGAs, and insurtechs building quoting engines that consume ISO ERC rate data
target_users.rationale: Developer-facing SDK; target users are engineers integrating ISO rating logic into Python quoting systems

project_class.rationale: Distributed as a PyPI package; primary artifact is the Python package API surface, not a hosted service

capabilities.rationale: Pure Python library for data loading and rating computation; no frontend, auth, or hosting capabilities

complexity_level.rationale: Performance-sensitive in-memory index construction, ISO circular XML/CSV parsing, and territory resolution logic combine for moderate complexity; no distributed systems concerns

risk_level.rationale: Open-source library with no network access or data storage; risk is limited to incorrect rating output affecting downstream quoting accuracy

data_sensitivity.rationale: SDK operates on ISO ERC rate data which is licensed but not sensitive personal or financial data; no PII or PHI processed

external_exposure.rationale: Distributed publicly on PyPI under open-source license; documentation hosted publicly on Read the Docs

access_model.value: No authentication; SDK is a local library; ISO ERC data files are provided by the user under their ISO license
access_model.rationale: SDK does not distribute ISO data; users supply their licensed rate files; no auth layer needed

feature_scope.value: ISO ERC rate table loading with in-memory index, territory definition resolution by ZIP code and state, coverage factor lookup by rating variable, ISO circular XML and CSV parsing, version diff comparison between circular updates, coverage factor application chain (base rate times factors), Python type-safe API with comprehensive docstrings
feature_scope.rationale: Full ISO ERC rating capability needed for production quoting engine integration

mvp_scope.value: RateTable loader with in-memory index, territory resolution by ZIP, coverage factor lookup, and one-millisecond p99 lookup benchmark passing in CI
mvp_scope.rationale: Core rating path must be proven fast before adding circular parsing and version diffing

security_baseline.value: No network access in library code; no subprocess calls; supply chain hardening via pinned dependencies in pyproject.toml; SLSA Level 2 provenance in GitHub Actions release
security_baseline.rationale: Open-source library supply chain risks are the primary attack surface; no network calls eliminates SSRF risk

frontend_stack.value: None; library has no UI; documentation generated by Sphinx and hosted on Read the Docs
frontend_stack.rationale: SDK library; no frontend

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with no mandatory runtime dependencies beyond standard library; optional numpy for vectorized rating in high-throughput mode
backend_stack.rationale: Minimal dependencies reduce user dependency conflicts; numpy optional to avoid imposing heavy dependency on users with simple lookup needs

data_platform.value: None; all data held in-process memory structures; no database or file I/O after initial load
data_platform.rationale: In-memory indexing is the core performance design; persistent storage would add latency to lookup path

hosting_target.value: PyPI package distributed via GitHub Actions release workflow; documentation on Read the Docs
hosting_target.rationale: Standard Python open-source distribution

privacy_retention_policy.value: No user data collected or retained; SDK is a local library with no telemetry
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Explicit no-telemetry policy to satisfy enterprise adopter security requirements

future_scope.value: Rust extension module for critical lookup path via PyO3 for sub-millisecond throughput; ACORD XML schema parsing; support for non-ISO proprietary rate table formats
future_scope.rationale: Performance ceiling of pure Python may limit very high-throughput quoting engines beyond 50K lookups/second

constraints.value: P99 lookup latency under 1ms for a loaded RateTable with 500K rows; zero mandatory third-party dependencies beyond Python stdlib; supports Python 3.10 through 3.13
constraints.rationale: Quoting engines call the SDK in the hot path; latency and dependency constraints are non-negotiable for adoption

observability_baseline.value: No runtime telemetry in the library; benchmark suite in GitHub Actions CI reporting lookup latency regressions as test failures
observability_baseline.rationale: Library observability is provided by the host application; benchmark CI gate prevents latency regressions from shipping

execution_preference.value: Semantic versioning with public changelog; feature branches merged via pull request with 2 reviewer approvals
execution_preference.rationale: Open-source hygiene and stable API guarantees for library consumers

project.goal: Python SDK for fast ISO ERC rate table lookups and territory resolution for quoting engines
project.target_users: Insurance software developers and actuarial engineers
project.timeline: 3-month MVP release, ongoing open-source maintenance

backend.framework: No framework; pure library with pyproject.toml build
backend.api: Public Python API: RateTable, TerritoryResolver, CircularParser, FactorChain classes

security.auth: None; local library
security.transport: No network access; supply chain via pinned deps and SLSA provenance

data.db: None; in-memory dict and bisect-based indexes
data.cache: In-process LRU cache on territory resolution (functools.lru_cache)

devops.ci: GitHub Actions: pytest, mypy, ruff, benchmark regression gate, PyPI publish on tag

reasoner_reviews.product: The SDK scope is well-bounded: load ISO ERC rate tables into an in-memory index, resolve territory by ZIP/state, look up coverage factors, parse circulars, and compute factor chains. The 1ms P99 constraint drives all internal design choices. Version diffing between circular updates is a differentiating feature that reduces manual audit work for actuaries upgrading their rate tables.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The in-memory index design is the core technical decision. A dict-keyed by (state, territory_code, coverage_code, rating_variable) with Python bisect for range lookups will meet the 1ms constraint for 500K rows. The alternative -- loading into DuckDB in-process -- would be simpler to implement but adds a mandatory binary dependency. The zero mandatory dependencies constraint rules it out. The circular parser must handle both XML (older ISO circulars) and CSV (newer ERC distribution format) without requiring lxml or pandas as mandatory deps.
reasoner_reviews.security: No network access and no subprocess calls are the right constraints for an open-source library distributed to enterprise users. SLSA Level 2 provenance in the GitHub Actions release workflow allows enterprise security teams to verify artifact integrity. Pinned transitive dependencies in CI prevent supply chain attacks; users of the library do not inherit these pins.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 1ms P99 constraint at 500K rows is achievable in pure Python with a pre-built index. Benchmark CI gate prevents regression on every PR. Python 3.10-3.13 matrix support is straightforward with tox and GitHub Actions.

backend.service_design: Four public classes: RateTable (loads ISO ERC CSV/XML into a nested dict index keyed by state/territory/coverage/variable, with bisect-sorted list for range factor lookups), TerritoryResolver (resolves ZIP+4 to ISO territory code using a pre-built ZIP-to-territory mapping dict with LRU cache), CircularParser (reads ISO circular XML or CSV release files, validates schema, returns structured RateTableDiff), FactorChain (applies ordered sequence of coverage factors to a base rate returning final premium).
backend.api_patterns: Pythonic fluent API: table = RateTable.load_csv(path); factor = table.lookup(state='TX', territory='005', coverage='BI', variable='age_band', value='30-35'). All public methods return typed dataclasses; no mutable state after load; thread-safe for concurrent quoting engine use.
backend.failure_handling: RateTable.load_csv raises ISOParseError (subclass of ValueError) with row number and field name on malformed ERC files; never silently returns default values for missing rating variables. TerritoryResolver raises TerritoryNotFoundError for unknown ZIP codes rather than falling back to state-average territory, preventing silent rating errors in quoting engines.

security.auth_design: No authentication; local library. No network calls anywhere in the library codebase; enforced via a CI test that imports the SDK in a network-isolated Docker container and asserts no socket connections are attempted.
security.secrets_management: No secrets managed; ISO ERC data files are user-supplied under their ISO license. SDK does not read environment variables.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: No telemetry; structured logging via Python stdlib logging module at DEBUG level only; host application controls log output. SLSA Level 2 provenance attestation on every PyPI release via GitHub Actions slsa-github-generator.

data.entities: rate_tables (loaded in-memory: {state: {territory_code: {coverage_code: {variable: [(value_range, factor)]}}}}), territory_definitions (ZIP-to-territory mapping dict: {state: {zip_prefix: territory_code}}), iso_circulars (parsed circular objects: {circular_id, effective_date, state, changes: [{coverage_code, variable, old_value, new_value}]}), coverage_factors (ordered list of (variable, value, factor) tuples per coverage), version_manifests (circular version metadata: {version_id, state, effective_date, source_filename, checksum})
data.storage_design: All data structures held in process memory after load; no disk I/O after initial load_csv or load_xml call. LRU cache (maxsize=10000) on TerritoryResolver.resolve() to avoid repeated dict traversal on the same ZIP codes in bulk quoting scenarios.
data.retention_and_deletion: No data retained outside process lifetime; garbage collected when RateTable object goes out of scope. No files written by the library.

devops.deployment_topology: Python package distributed via PyPI; no hosted infrastructure. GitHub repository with branch protection requiring 2 reviewer approvals. Docs built by Read the Docs on every merge to main.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions matrix CI: ruff lint, mypy type check, pytest with 90% coverage gate, benchmark suite asserting P99 lookup latency < 1ms at 500K rows (via pytest-benchmark), tox for Python 3.10/3.11/3.12/3.13 compatibility. Release: on git tag push, GitHub Actions builds wheel and source distribution, runs SLSA provenance generation, publishes to PyPI via trusted publisher.
devops.observability_stack: Benchmark CI reports latency percentiles per PR in GitHub Actions summary. No runtime monitoring in the library itself.
devops.cost_controls: Zero hosting cost; GitHub Actions free tier covers open-source repository; PyPI free for open-source packages.

ISS-001.title: Public API lacks type-safe error hierarchy; lookup returns None on miss
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan showed table.lookup() returning None for missing rating variables, causing silent wrong premiums in quoting engines that fail to check return value. Callers need explicit exception on miss to prevent silent errors.

history.note: lookup() returning None on miss documented in round 1 plan; auditor flagged as silent failure risk

ISS-002.title: Benchmark CI gate missing from round 1 plan
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan described 1ms P99 constraint but did not specify a CI gate that enforces it; latency regressions could ship undetected.

history.note: Benchmark described in constraints but no CI enforcement mechanism specified in deployment_and_operations

focus_issues.title: Benchmark CI gate missing from plan
focus_issues.detail: Plan specifies 1ms P99 latency constraint but deployment_and_operations section does not describe a pytest-benchmark CI step asserting this threshold; latency regressions could reach PyPI release.

Document benchmark configuration (number of rounds, warmup) in development_guidelines
Add benchmark regression comment to PR via GitHub Actions summary

target_output.title: ISO ERC Rate Table Python SDK
target_output.executive_summary: An open-source Python SDK (iso-erc-sdk on PyPI) enabling insurance engineers to load ISO Electronic Rating Content rate tables into a high-performance in-memory index, resolve ZIP codes to ISO territory codes, look up coverage factors, parse ISO circulars for version updates, and compute factor application chains. Zero mandatory third-party dependencies; P99 lookup latency under 1ms at 500K rows enforced by a pytest-benchmark CI gate; SLSA Level 2 provenance on every PyPI release.
target_output.architecture_overview: Pure Python library with no hosted infrastructure. Four public classes expose the rating API surface: RateTable builds a nested dict index keyed by (state, territory_code, coverage_code, rating_variable) with bisect-sorted factor lists for range lookups, loaded from ISO ERC CSV or XML files supplied by the user under their ISO license. TerritoryResolver maps ZIP+4 to ISO territory codes using a pre-built ZIP-prefix dict with LRU cache for quoting engine hot paths. CircularParser reads ISO circular release files and returns structured diffs for version auditing. FactorChain applies an ordered sequence of coverage factors to a base rate. All public methods return typed dataclasses; no mutable state after load; thread-safe for concurrent quoting engine use across worker processes.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: Python 3.10-3.13 (no mandatory third-party dependencies; all standard library). Optional: numpy for vectorized factor application in high-throughput mode (>50K lookups/second). Build: pyproject.toml with Hatchling. Type checking: mypy in strict mode. Linting: ruff. Testing: pytest with pytest-benchmark for latency CI gate; tox for Python version matrix. Distribution: PyPI via GitHub Actions trusted publisher. Docs: Sphinx with autodoc, hosted on Read the Docs. Supply chain: SLSA Level 2 provenance via slsa-github-generator.
target_output.functional_feature_map: RateTable Loader: loads ISO ERC CSV (new-format ERC distribution) and ISO ERC XML (legacy circular format) into nested in-memory dict; raises ISOParseError with row/field on malformed input. Territory Resolution: TerritoryResolver.resolve(zip_code, state) returns ISO territory code; LRU cache (maxsize=10000) for high-volume lookups; raises TerritoryNotFoundError for unknown ZIP codes. Coverage Factor Lookup: table.lookup(state, territory, coverage, variable, value) returns factor as Decimal; raises RatingVariableNotFoundError on miss. Circular Parsing and Diffing: CircularParser.parse(path) returns RateTableDiff with added, removed, and modified factor rows; compare_versions(old_manifest, new_manifest) returns structured changelog. Factor Chain Application: FactorChain(base_rate).apply(factor).apply(factor).result() returns final premium as Decimal.
target_output.system_components: Python package iso-erc-sdk distributed via PyPI. GitHub repository with branch protection (2 reviewer approvals required). GitHub Actions CI workflow (see deployment_and_operations). Read the Docs auto-build on merge to main. Benchmark fixture: 500K-row synthetic RateTable with 48 US states, 10 territories per state, 5 coverage codes, 8 rating variables.
target_output.workflows: Quoting Engine Integration: developer installs iso-erc-sdk, loads their ISO-licensed ERC CSV at application startup via RateTable.load_csv(path), resolves ZIP to territory for each quote request via TerritoryResolver.resolve(), calls table.lookup() for each coverage factor, builds FactorChain with base rate from carrier filing, returns final premium -- all within 1ms P99 per quote. ISO Circular Update: actuary receives new ISO circular release file, calls CircularParser.parse(new_file) to get RateTableDiff, reviews changed factors via compare_versions(old_manifest, new_manifest), loads updated RateTable, runs compare_versions output against in-force book to identify premium impact before committing to production.
target_output.data_model: In-memory structures (not persisted): rate_tables dict {state: {territory_code: {coverage_code: {variable: SortedFactorList}}}} where SortedFactorList is a bisect-managed list of (value_lower, value_upper, factor: Decimal) tuples. territory_definitions dict {state: {zip_prefix_5: territory_code}}. RateTableDiff dataclass {circular_id: str, effective_date: date, state: str, added_factors: list[FactorRow], removed_factors: list[FactorRow], modified_factors: list[FactorChange]}. VersionManifest dataclass {version_id: str, state: str, effective_date: date, source_filename: str, sha256_checksum: str}. FactorRow dataclass {coverage_code: str, variable: str, value_range: tuple, factor: Decimal}.
target_output.api_design: Public API surface: RateTable.load_csv(path: Path, state: str) -> RateTable. RateTable.load_xml(path: Path) -> RateTable. RateTable.lookup(state: str, territory: str, coverage: str, variable: str, value: str | int | Decimal) -> Decimal raises RatingVariableNotFoundError. TerritoryResolver.load(path: Path) -> TerritoryResolver. TerritoryResolver.resolve(zip_code: str, state: str) -> str raises TerritoryNotFoundError. CircularParser.parse(path: Path) -> RateTableDiff. FactorChain(base_rate: Decimal).apply(factor: Decimal) -> FactorChain. FactorChain.result() -> Decimal. Example: rating = RateTable.load_csv('erc_tx_BI.csv', state='TX'); premium = FactorChain(Decimal('500')).apply(rating.lookup('TX', '005', 'BI', 'age_band', '30-35')).result().
target_output.security_and_compliance: No network access: library code contains no socket, urllib, requests, or subprocess calls; enforced by a CI test running the SDK in a Docker container with --network=none and asserting zero import-time side effects. Supply chain: pinned dependencies in CI requirements; SLSA Level 2 provenance attestation on every PyPI release via slsa-github-generator; SHA256 checksums in VersionManifest for user-side data integrity verification. No telemetry or analytics collected. ISO licensing: SDK does not distribute ISO ERC data; users supply files under their own ISO license; NOTICE file documents this distinction. Python version security: supported Python versions (3.10-3.13) receive security updates; SDK deprecates support for EOL Python versions per standard open-source policy.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI on every PR and merge: (1) ruff lint and format check, (2) mypy strict type check, (3) pytest with 90% coverage gate across Python 3.10/3.11/3.12/3.13 via tox, (4) pytest-benchmark step: loads 500K-row RateTable fixture, executes 10000 lookups with 50 benchmark rounds and 10 warmup iterations, asserts P99 latency < 1.0ms via --benchmark-fail-max-time=0.001; reports median/P99/max latency in GitHub Actions step summary for PR reviewer visibility. Release workflow: on git tag push, builds wheel and sdist via Hatchling, runs slsa-github-generator for SLSA Level 2 provenance, publishes to PyPI via trusted publisher (no long-lived API tokens). Read the Docs builds documentation on every merge to main.
target_output.observability: No runtime observability in library code. CI benchmark reports: GitHub Actions step summary shows lookup latency histogram (median, P90, P99, max) per PR; benchmark results stored as GitHub Actions artifact for trend analysis. Latency regression alert: PR CI fails if P99 exceeds 1ms, preventing regression from reaching PyPI. Memory profiling: optional pytest-memray step in CI reports peak RSS during 500K-row RateTable load for memory regression tracking.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero hosting cost: GitHub Actions free tier for open-source repository, PyPI free for open-source packages, Read the Docs free for open-source projects. Performance scaling: pure Python in-memory index supports up to ~50K lookups/second per process on a modern CPU core; numpy vectorized mode (optional dependency) enables batch lookup of 1M+ rows per second for bulk book-of-business rating scenarios. No infrastructure scaling concerns for a local library.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): RateTable CSV loader, territory resolution, coverage factor lookup with RatingVariableNotFoundError, FactorChain, 500K-row benchmark fixture, pytest-benchmark CI gate asserting P99 < 1ms, PyPI release workflow with SLSA provenance, Sphinx docs skeleton. Phase 2 (4 weeks): ISO circular XML parser and RateTableDiff, version manifest comparison, TerritoryResolver ZIP+4 support, LRU cache tuning, full API documentation with worked examples. Phase 3 (3 weeks): optional numpy vectorized batch lookup mode, comprehensive ERC format coverage for all ISO lines of business (auto, homeowners, commercial), integration test suite against sample ERC files from major ISO states.
target_output.development_guidelines: Zero mandatory third-party dependencies enforced by CI: import the package in a test with a stdlib-only Python environment and assert no ImportError from third-party packages. All public methods must have type annotations and docstrings with a Usage example. Benchmark configuration: 50 rounds, 10 warmup, timer=time.perf_counter; benchmark test file is tests/test_benchmarks.py and must not be skipped in CI. New public API additions require a CHANGELOG entry and a migration note if they change existing method signatures. Decimal used for all factor and premium arithmetic to avoid floating-point rounding errors in premium computation.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ISO ERC file format changes in future circular releases could break CircularParser; ISO does not provide a versioned schema. Mitigation: parser accepts both CSV and XML with format detection; schema validation with informative ISOParseError includes format version in error message for rapid diagnosis. Risk: LRU cache on TerritoryResolver.resolve() defaults to maxsize=10000 which may miss for very large ZIP coverage books. Mitigation: maxsize is configurable at TerritoryResolver instantiation; documented in API reference. Tradeoff: Decimal arithmetic over float for factor computation -- correct for monetary rounding but 3-4x slower than float; acceptable given the 1ms P99 benchmark still passes with Decimal. Tradeoff: zero mandatory dependencies over DuckDB in-process -- eliminates binary dependency conflicts but limits query expressiveness; circular diffing uses dataclass comparison rather than SQL joins.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed Decimal over float for all factor arithmetic; test confirms 1ms P99 still satisfied with Decimal at 500K rows. Confirmed LRU cache maxsize=10000 as default on TerritoryResolver with configurable override. Confirmed no telemetry in any release version; documented in README and NOTICE file for enterprise adopter security review.

metadata.notes: Round 2 adds pytest-benchmark CI gate for 1ms P99 lookup enforcement; ISS-001 (silent None on miss) resolved in round 1.

profile.domain: P&C Carrier Policy Administration System Migration

project_goal.value: Migrate a regional P&C carrier's on-premise Oracle-based policy administration system to cloud-native AWS architecture, re-platforming batch rating jobs to containerized Spring Batch microservices on EKS while maintaining zero-downtime policy issuance throughout the cutover period
project_goal.rationale: Oracle licensing costs at $1.2M/year and inability to scale batch rating jobs during catastrophe events are the primary drivers; cloud-native EKS deployment enables horizontal scaling and eliminates single-node batch bottleneck

target_users.value: Internal policy administration users (underwriters, CSRs, agents), batch processing system for overnight rating and billing, and insurance department regulatory reporting jobs
target_users.rationale: Three distinct consumer categories with different availability and latency requirements

project_class.rationale: Platform migration and re-platforming of existing application; primary deliverables are infrastructure configuration and data migration scripts, not new application features

capabilities.rationale: Backend microservices, database migration, CI/CD pipeline, and batch rating job re-platforming; no new frontend or auth systems in scope

complexity_level.rationale: Dual-write replication between Oracle and PostgreSQL during cutover, numeric parity validation for rating outputs, zero-downtime cutover protocol, and Oracle PL/SQL to Java Spring Batch re-implementation combine for very high complexity

risk_level.rationale: Policy administration system outage during cutover would halt policy issuance and claims processing; state insurance department notification required for system changes; data loss during migration would be unrecoverable without full rollback

data_sensitivity.rationale: Policy data, premium transactions, endorsement histories, and agent commission records are confidential business data with state retention requirements

external_exposure.rationale: Policy administration system is internal-only; no public endpoints; agent portal access is out of scope for this migration phase

access_model.value: Keycloak OIDC SSO maintained from existing on-premise system; IAM roles for EKS workloads; no changes to user-facing authentication during migration
access_model.rationale: Minimizing auth changes reduces cutover risk; Keycloak federation maintained throughout migration

feature_scope.value: Oracle to PostgreSQL schema migration, dual-write replication during cutover, Spring Batch rating job re-implementation with Oracle PL/SQL parity, document generation service containerization, billing account migration, agent commission calculation re-platforming, EKS cluster provisioning, Jenkins CI/CD migration to EKS-native pipelines, cutover runbook with automated rollback
feature_scope.rationale: Full re-platforming scope for all policy administration system components

mvp_scope.value: EKS cluster provisioning, PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Aurora, dual-write replication framework, first two Spring Batch rating jobs (personal auto and homeowners) with numeric parity validation against Oracle output, zero-downtime cutover protocol documented and tested in staging
mvp_scope.rationale: Personal auto and homeowners represent 70% of policy volume; validating parity on these lines de-risks the remaining lines

security_baseline.value: AWS KMS encryption for RDS Aurora at rest; TLS in transit; VPC-only access to RDS; Secrets Manager for all database credentials; no Oracle listener exposed outside VPC during dual-write period
security_baseline.rationale: Confidential policy data requires encryption at rest and in transit; dual-write period must not increase attack surface

frontend_stack.value: None; infrastructure migration project; no frontend changes in scope
frontend_stack.rationale: Existing policy administration UI is out of scope; UI layer is a separate phase

backend_stack.value: Java 21 with Spring Boot 3.3 and Spring Batch 5 for rating job re-implementation; existing application backend maintained on Java 11 until cutover; no language change to application code
backend_stack.rationale: Spring Batch 5 on Java 21 provides virtual threads for batch parallelism; existing application stays on Java 11 to minimize risk during migration

data_platform.value: Oracle Database 19c migrating to Aurora PostgreSQL 16; AWS DMS for replication during dual-write; Redis 7 for rate cache; pglogical for PostgreSQL-side replication validation
data_platform.rationale: Aurora PostgreSQL provides Oracle-comparable HA, PITR, and read replica capabilities with 90% reduction in licensing cost; AWS DMS handles schema conversion and CDC replication

hosting_target.value: AWS EKS managed Kubernetes in us-east-1 multi-AZ; Aurora PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ; Jenkins on EKS; all resources in private VPC subnets
hosting_target.rationale: EKS provides managed Kubernetes with native AWS integrations; us-east-1 matches existing AWS workload region

privacy_retention_policy.value: Policy records retained 7 years per state insurance department requirements; premium transaction logs retained 10 years; soft-delete pattern for cancelled policies; no physical deletes during migration
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: State insurance data retention mandates enforced in both Oracle and PostgreSQL during dual-write period

future_scope.value: Oracle decommission after 6-month parallel operation confirmation; agent portal frontend migration to React; claims management system migration as Phase 2
future_scope.rationale: Policy administration migration is Phase 1 of a 3-phase core systems modernization program

constraints.value: Zero policy issuance downtime during business hours 07:00-22:00 EST; Oracle decommission cannot occur until 6-month parallel confirmation with zero data discrepancies; state insurance department notification filed before production cutover
constraints.rationale: Policy issuance downtime triggers state regulatory reporting and reputational risk; parallel operation window required for regulatory confidence

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM on all EKS workloads; structured JSON logging to CloudWatch; PagerDuty for dual-write replication lag alerts and batch job failures
observability_baseline.rationale: Datadog provides unified APM and infrastructure monitoring across EKS and RDS; PagerDuty for on-call escalation during cutover

execution_preference.value: Phased migration with per-line-of-business Spring Batch promotion gates; no Oracle table decommission until all consumers validated on PostgreSQL for that table
execution_preference.rationale: Per-LOB promotion gates reduce blast radius of a single batch job failure

compliance_context.value: State insurance department system change notification requirements in all 12 operating states; SOC 2 Type II controls for data handling continuity; data retention mandates per state insurance code
compliance_context.rationale: Multi-state carrier must notify insurance departments in all operating states before major system changes affecting policy administration

project.goal: Re-platform Oracle-based policy administration system to Aurora PostgreSQL and EKS with zero-downtime policy issuance
project.target_users: Underwriters, CSRs, agents, batch processing, regulatory reporting
project.timeline: 18-month migration with 6-month parallel operation before Oracle decommission

backend.runtime: Java 21 (Spring Batch 5 for new rating jobs); Java 11 maintained for existing application during migration
backend.framework: Spring Boot 3.3, Spring Batch 5 for rating jobs; Spring Data JPA for PostgreSQL
backend.api: No new external API; internal service-to-service only

security.auth: Keycloak SSO maintained; AWS IAM for EKS workloads; Secrets Manager for DB credentials
security.transport: TLS 1.3 in transit; KMS encryption for Aurora at rest; VPC-only RDS access

data.db: Oracle 19c (source, maintained 6 months post-cutover); Aurora PostgreSQL 16 (target); AWS DMS for CDC replication
data.cache: Redis 7 on ElastiCache for rate table cache

devops.ci: Jenkins on EKS; Terraform for all AWS infrastructure
devops.hosting: AWS EKS us-east-1 Multi-AZ; Aurora PostgreSQL Multi-AZ
devops.environments: migration-dev (full stack, reduced data volume), migration-staging (production data subset, full parity tests), production (dual-write then cutover)

constraints.downtime: Zero policy issuance downtime during business hours
constraints.regulatory: State DOI notification in 12 states before production cutover

open_questions.dms_lag_tolerance: Maximum acceptable replication lag from Oracle to PostgreSQL before alerting on-call during dual-write period
open_questions.rollback_rto: Recovery time objective if full rollback to Oracle-only is triggered mid-cutover

reasoner_reviews.product: The migration scope is well-defined with the right phasing: EKS and Aurora provisioning first, then dual-write replication framework, then per-LOB Spring Batch job promotion gates. The 6-month parallel operation window before Oracle decommission is the right risk mitigation given the multi-state regulatory notification requirement. Personal auto and homeowners as the first two LOBs to migrate covers 70% of volume and de-risks the remaining specialty lines.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: AWS DMS CDC is the right replication mechanism for the Oracle-to-PostgreSQL dual-write period. The critical architectural constraint is the numeric parity validation framework for Spring Batch rating outputs: every new Spring Batch job must produce identical premium results to the Oracle PL/SQL procedure it replaces, verified against a corpus of historical policy records before promotion. The parity check must be automated in the Jenkins pipeline with a merge block on failure. Aurora PostgreSQL Multi-AZ provides Oracle-comparable HA without requiring Oracle RAC licensing.
reasoner_reviews.security: KMS encryption for Aurora and Secrets Manager for credentials are the right baseline. The dual-write period must not increase the attack surface: Oracle listener should remain VPC-private throughout; DMS replication task runs within the VPC using an IAM role with least-privilege access. State DOI notification filed before production cutover is a hard compliance gate.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Zero business-hours downtime and 6-month parallel operation are the two hardest constraints. The cutover protocol must support a full rollback to Oracle-only within 15 minutes (RTO) if a data discrepancy is detected post-cutover. Replication lag alert threshold should be set at 30 seconds to allow on-call intervention before lag compounds.

backend.service_design: Spring Batch jobs organized by line of business in separate Kubernetes Jobs (not long-running Deployments): PersonalAutoRatingJob (replaces PL/SQL RATE_AUTO procedure), HomeownersRatingJob (replaces RATE_HO procedure), DocumentGenerationJob (replaces Oracle Reports batch), BillingAccountJob (replaces BILL_CYCLE procedure), CommissionCalculationJob (replaces COMM_CALC procedure). Each Spring Batch job reads from PostgreSQL, writes to PostgreSQL, and posts a parity validation event comparing output to Oracle PL/SQL for the same input set during the validation period.
backend.api_patterns: No external API for batch jobs; Spring Batch Admin REST API (internal) for job monitoring and manual rerun triggers. Jobs triggered by Kubernetes CronJob on nightly schedule matching existing Oracle job scheduler times.
backend.background_jobs: Nightly rating batch: PersonalAutoRatingJob and HomeownersRatingJob at 01:00 EST; DocumentGenerationJob at 03:00 EST after rating completes; BillingAccountJob at 04:00 EST; CommissionCalculationJob at 05:00 EST. Parity validation job runs in staging environment against production data subset on every deployment.
backend.failure_handling: Spring Batch restart-ability: all jobs use JDBC JobRepository on PostgreSQL with step-level restart; failed jobs resume from last completed chunk (1000 policies per chunk). Parity validation failure halts promotion: if parity delta exceeds $0.01 on any policy, the Kubernetes Job terminates with non-zero exit code, Jenkins build fails, and PagerDuty fires.

security.auth_design: EKS workloads use IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts) for AWS API access; no static IAM credentials in pods. Spring Batch jobs connect to Aurora via credentials fetched from Secrets Manager at startup via AWS SDK. Oracle connection during dual-write period uses a read-only service account with minimum required table grants.
security.secrets_management: AWS Secrets Manager: Aurora PostgreSQL credentials, Oracle read credentials for dual-write validation, Redis ElastiCache auth token. Rotation: Aurora credentials auto-rotated every 30 days via Secrets Manager Lambda rotation function. No secrets in Kubernetes ConfigMaps or environment variables.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: All Spring Batch job executions logged to Datadog with job_name, step_name, policy_count, duration, and parity_delta fields. AWS CloudTrail for all AWS API calls. Aurora audit log (pgaudit) for all DDL and DML on policy-critical tables. DMS replication task logs to CloudWatch with replication lag metric.

data.entities: policies (policy_id, policy_number, insured_id FK, product_code, effective_date, expiration_date, status ENUM active/cancelled/expired/pending, created_at, updated_at), coverage_endorsements (endorsement_id, policy_id FK, coverage_code, limit_amount, deductible, endorsement_type, effective_date), rating_factors (factor_id, policy_id FK, factor_code, factor_value, rating_basis, applied_at), premium_transactions (txn_id, policy_id FK, transaction_type ENUM new/renewal/endorsement/cancellation, gross_premium, net_premium, taxes, fees, effective_date, posted_at), policy_terms (term_id, policy_id FK, term_start, term_end, renewal_count), document_generations (doc_id, policy_id FK, doc_type ENUM declaration/endorsement/cancellation, generated_at, s3_key), billing_accounts (account_id, policy_id FK, billing_plan, installment_count, next_due_date, balance), agent_commissions (commission_id, policy_id FK, agent_id, commission_rate, gross_commission, paid_at)
data.storage_design: Aurora PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ (primary + 2 read replicas): policy tables partitioned by policy effective_year for archive query performance. AWS DMS CDC task replicates Oracle changes to Aurora in near-real-time during dual-write. Redis 7 ElastiCache cluster for rate table cache (ISO ERC factors loaded at Spring Batch job startup).
