S3 for generated policy documents with server-side KMS encryption.
data.migration_notes: Schema conversion via AWS Schema Conversion Tool (SCT) for Oracle-to-PostgreSQL DDL translation; PL/SQL procedures re-implemented as Spring Batch steps rather than ported to PL/pgSQL. Oracle NUMBER precision mapped to PostgreSQL NUMERIC(precision, scale) to avoid rounding drift. Sequences migrated to PostgreSQL sequences with Oracle-equivalent START value. Foreign key constraints deferred during bulk initial load, re-enabled after row-count reconciliation.

devops.deployment_topology: EKS managed cluster (us-east-1a, 1b, 1c): system node group (m6i.large, 3 nodes), batch node group (c6i.8xlarge, 2-10 nodes auto-scaling during batch windows). Aurora PostgreSQL Multi-AZ: db.r6g.2xlarge primary with 2 read replicas. ElastiCache Redis 7 cluster (3 nodes). AWS DMS replication instance (dms.r6i.large) in private subnet. Jenkins on EKS (Deployment, 2 replicas). Terraform Cloud for state.
devops.ci_cd_design: Jenkins pipelines: (1) Spring Batch job build: compile, unit test, integration test against PostgreSQL testcontainer, parity validation against Oracle reference dataset (100K policy sample), merge blocked on parity failure. (2) Infrastructure: Terraform plan/apply with human approval gate for production. (3) Cutover runbook: Jenkins job executing cutover steps with automated health checks and rollback trigger. Helm charts for all Kubernetes workloads; ArgoCD for GitOps-style deployment sync.
devops.rollback_strategy: Pre-cutover: Oracle is system of record; PostgreSQL is shadow; rollback requires disabling DMS replication task only. Post-cutover (within 6-month parallel period): dual-write active; rollback to Oracle-as-primary by stopping PostgreSQL writes and re-enabling Oracle writer in application config within 15 minutes. Post-parallel period: Oracle decommissioned; rollback from backup only (Aurora PITR within 35-day retention window).
devops.backup_and_recovery: Aurora automated backups: continuous PITR with 35-day retention; daily snapshots retained 90 days. Oracle final export backup taken immediately before cutover and retained 7 years per retention policy. S3 policy document bucket: versioned, cross-region replication to us-west-2.

target_output.thinking_summary: This Oracle-to-PostgreSQL policy administration migration centers on zero-downtime cutover via AWS DMS CDC dual-write replication and per-LOB Spring Batch parity validation gates. The critical architectural decisions are: AWS DMS for CDC replication during the 6-month parallel period, Aurora PostgreSQL Multi-AZ as the target, Spring Batch 5 on Java 21 with virtual threads for rating job re-implementation, and a Jenkins parity validation pipeline that blocks promotion if rating output diverges from Oracle PL/SQL by more than $0.01. The weak quality target means some sections are thinner than ideal, reflecting the plan_quality distribution requirement.
target_output.title: P&C Policy Administration System Migration to AWS EKS and Aurora PostgreSQL
target_output.executive_summary: An 18-month infrastructure migration moving a regional P&C carrier's Oracle-based policy administration system to AWS EKS and Aurora PostgreSQL 16, with Spring Batch 5 re-implementation of overnight rating jobs, AWS DMS CDC dual-write replication during a 6-month parallel operation window, and a Jenkins parity validation gate ensuring rating output numeric equivalence to Oracle PL/SQL before each line-of-business promotion. Zero policy issuance downtime during business hours throughout the migration.
target_output.architecture_overview: Migration architecture uses a dual-system parallel operation pattern: AWS DMS CDC task continuously replicates Oracle changes to Aurora PostgreSQL during the 6-month coexistence window, while the application is gradually re-pointed to PostgreSQL per line of business. EKS replaces Oracle RAC with horizontally scalable Spring Batch Kubernetes Jobs for overnight rating, each validated against Oracle PL/SQL output before promotion. Aurora PostgreSQL Multi-AZ in us-east-1 (three AZs) provides Oracle-comparable HA without RAC licensing. Redis ElastiCache caches ISO ERC rate factors loaded at batch job startup. ArgoCD provides GitOps deployment sync for all EKS workloads.
target_output.technology_stack: Infrastructure: Terraform on AWS EKS (us-east-1 Multi-AZ), Aurora PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ (db.r6g.2xlarge), ElastiCache Redis 7. Replication: AWS DMS CDC from Oracle 19c to Aurora PostgreSQL. Batch: Spring Batch 5 on Java 21 with Spring Boot 3.3, Spring Data JPA. CI/CD: Jenkins on EKS, ArgoCD, Helm, Terraform Cloud. Observability: Datadog APM, CloudWatch, PagerDuty. Migration tooling: AWS Schema Conversion Tool, pgaudit on Aurora.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Oracle Schema Migration: AWS SCT converts Oracle DDL to PostgreSQL-compatible DDL; manual review for PL/SQL procedure conversions; NUMBER precision mapping to NUMERIC. DMS Dual-Write: CDC replication task keeps Aurora in sync with Oracle throughout parallel period; replication lag monitored with 30-second alert threshold. Spring Batch Rating Jobs: PersonalAutoRatingJob and HomeownersRatingJob replace Oracle PL/SQL RATE_AUTO and RATE_HO procedures; each reads policies from PostgreSQL, computes premium using Java rating logic, writes results back. Parity Validation: Jenkins pipeline runs each new Spring Batch job against 100K historical policy sample, compares premium output to Oracle PL/SQL results, blocks promotion on delta > $0.01. Cutover Protocol: per-LOB traffic switch with automated health checks; rollback triggers if error rate spikes above 0.1% within 15 minutes of switch.
target_output.system_components: EKS cluster with system node group (m6i.large x3) and batch node group (c6i.8xlarge x2-10 auto-scaling). Aurora PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ (primary + 2 read replicas). AWS DMS replication instance (dms.r6i.large). ElastiCache Redis 7 cluster. Jenkins on EKS (2 replicas). ArgoCD on EKS. Oracle 19c on-premise (source, maintained 6 months post-cutover). S3 bucket for policy documents and Oracle backup. Terraform Cloud for state management.
target_output.workflows: Nightly Rating Batch: Kubernetes CronJob triggers PersonalAutoRatingJob at 01:00 EST; Spring Batch reads unrated policies from Aurora in 1000-policy chunks, computes premium factors from Redis rate cache and Java rating engine, writes premium_transactions to Aurora; job completes before business hours at 07:00 EST; DocumentGenerationJob generates declaration pages and writes to S3; BillingAccountJob posts installments; CommissionCalculationJob computes agent commissions. Per-LOB Promotion Gate: engineer submits PR adding a new Spring Batch job; Jenkins runs parity validation against 100K historical records from Oracle reference; if all policy premium deltas are within $0.01, gate passes and job is promoted to production CronJob schedule.
target_output.data_model: policies (policy_id BIGINT PK, policy_number VARCHAR UNIQUE, insured_id BIGINT FK, product_code VARCHAR, effective_date DATE, expiration_date DATE, status ENUM active/cancelled/expired/pending, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ) PARTITION BY RANGE (EXTRACT(YEAR FROM effective_date)). coverage_endorsements (endorsement_id BIGINT PK, policy_id FK, coverage_code VARCHAR, limit_amount NUMERIC(15,2), deductible NUMERIC(10,2), effective_date DATE). rating_factors (factor_id BIGINT PK, policy_id FK, factor_code VARCHAR, factor_value NUMERIC(10,6), rating_basis VARCHAR, applied_at TIMESTAMPTZ). premium_transactions (txn_id BIGINT PK, policy_id FK, transaction_type ENUM new/renewal/endorsement/cancellation, gross_premium NUMERIC(12,2), net_premium NUMERIC(12,2), taxes NUMERIC(10,2), fees NUMERIC(10,2), effective_date DATE, posted_at TIMESTAMPTZ). billing_accounts (account_id BIGINT PK, policy_id FK, billing_plan VARCHAR, installment_count INT, next_due_date DATE, balance NUMERIC(12,2)). agent_commissions (commission_id BIGINT PK, policy_id FK, agent_id BIGINT, commission_rate NUMERIC(6,4), gross_commission NUMERIC(12,2), paid_at DATE). document_generations (doc_id UUID PK, policy_id FK, doc_type ENUM declaration/endorsement/cancellation, generated_at TIMESTAMPTZ, s3_key VARCHAR).
target_output.api_design: No external API. Internal interfaces: Spring Batch Admin REST API (internal VPC only): GET /api/v1/jobs (list active jobs), POST /api/v1/jobs/{jobName}/launch (manual trigger), GET /api/v1/jobs/{executionId}/status. Jenkins pipeline API triggers parity validation job: POST /job/spring-batch-parity-validation/buildWithParameters?LOB=auto&sample_size=100000. DMS replication monitoring via AWS CLI: aws dms describe-replication-task-assessment-results to retrieve lag metrics.
target_output.security_and_compliance: AWS KMS: Aurora encrypted at rest with KMS CMK; S3 policy documents encrypted with KMS. Secrets Manager: Aurora credentials auto-rotated every 30 days; Oracle read credentials for DMS; Redis auth token. IRSA: each Spring Batch Kubernetes Job uses a dedicated IAM service account with least-privilege Secrets Manager and S3 access; no wildcards. DMS replication instance in private subnet with no public IP; Oracle TNS listener VPC-private throughout dual-write period. pgaudit on Aurora logs all DDL and DML on policy tables for SOC 2 evidence. State DOI compliance: notification filed in all 12 operating states before production cutover; system change description reviewed by compliance team. 7-year policy record retention enforced via Aurora partitioning with lifecycle archive to S3 Glacier.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Terraform manages all AWS resources: EKS cluster, node groups, Aurora cluster, DMS replication instance, ElastiCache, Secrets Manager secrets, IAM roles, S3 buckets, VPC configuration. Helm charts for all Kubernetes workloads versioned in GitLab; ArgoCD syncs from main branch to EKS. Jenkins pipelines: Spring Batch job build and parity validation (blocks merge on parity failure), Terraform plan/apply (human approval for production apply), cutover runbook job. Environments: migration-dev (full stack, 5% data volume), migration-staging (full data subset, parity validation runs nightly), production (dual-write active until LOB-by-LOB cutover complete). Cutover runbook steps: enable DMS full-load verification, switch application DB config to Aurora primary, monitor error rate for 15 minutes, decommission Oracle path if error rate < 0.1%. Rollback within parallel period: re-enable Oracle writer in application config, stop Aurora writes, validate DMS lag resumes -- RTO 15 minutes.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM: distributed traces on all Spring Batch job steps; custom metrics: policies_rated_per_minute, batch_job_duration_seconds, parity_delta_max per job run. CloudWatch: DMS replication lag (alert at 30 seconds), Aurora connections, EKS node CPU. PagerDuty escalation: DMS lag > 30 seconds, Spring Batch job failure, parity validation failure, Aurora failover event. Datadog dashboard: dual-write replication status, per-LOB migration completion percentage, nightly batch job success rate.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $18,000/month during migration (EKS nodes ~$4,000, Aurora Multi-AZ ~$7,000, DMS instance ~$600, ElastiCache ~$1,200, data transfer ~$500, Datadog ~$2,000, other ~$2,700). Post-Oracle decommission: ~$12,000/month (DMS instance eliminated, Oracle licensing savings $100K/month). Batch node group auto-scales to 10 c6i.8xlarge nodes during overnight rating window, scales to 2 minimum outside window. Aurora read replicas handle regulatory reporting queries without impacting transactional primary.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (12 weeks): EKS cluster and Aurora PostgreSQL provisioning via Terraform, DMS CDC replication task setup and initial load, PersonalAutoRatingJob and HomeownersRatingJob re-implementation with parity validation Jenkins gate, migration-staging environment with full parity confirmation. Phase 2 (12 weeks): remaining LOB Spring Batch jobs (commercial auto, commercial property, umbrella), DocumentGenerationJob and BillingAccountJob containerization, CommissionCalculationJob re-implementation, state DOI notifications filed, production cutover for personal lines. Phase 3 (24 weeks): commercial lines cutover, 6-month Oracle parallel operation monitoring, Oracle license termination and hardware decommission.
target_output.development_guidelines: All Spring Batch job implementations must achieve parity within $0.01 on 100% of the 100K-policy parity validation sample before merge; no exceptions. NUMERIC precision must match Oracle NUMBER precision for each column (documented in schema migration matrix). Spring Batch chunk size set to 1000 policies; larger chunks risk OOM on c6i.8xlarge during peak batch windows. Java 21 virtual threads enabled for Spring Batch job steps to increase throughput without increasing thread pool size. All infrastructure changes via Terraform; no manual AWS console changes to resources in Terraform state.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: AWS DMS CDC replication lag spikes during high-volume policy transaction periods, causing Oracle and PostgreSQL to diverge before cutover. Mitigation: DMS lag alert at 30 seconds allows on-call intervention; dual-write period provides rollback option at all times. Risk: Oracle NUMBER to PostgreSQL NUMERIC precision mapping introduces rounding differences in premium_transactions. Mitigation: parity validation against 100K historical records surfaces any rounding divergence before promotion; tolerance $0.01 per policy. Tradeoff: Spring Batch re-implementation of PL/SQL procedures over PL/pgSQL port -- higher initial effort but Java Spring Batch enables unit testing, CI integration, and Kubernetes scheduling that PL/pgSQL cannot provide. Tradeoff: Aurora over self-managed PostgreSQL on EC2 -- higher cost but eliminates HA management burden during a high-risk migration window.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed 30-second DMS replication lag alert threshold; on-call can investigate and resolve within 5 minutes before lag compounds to data divergence risk. Confirmed 15-minute RTO for full rollback to Oracle-only by re-enabling Oracle writer in application config; tested in migration-staging. Confirmed $0.01 absolute delta tolerance for parity validation (not percentage) to be consistent with monetary rounding conventions used in Oracle PL/SQL rating procedures.

metadata.notes: P&C policy administration migration Oracle to Aurora PostgreSQL on EKS; Spring Batch re-platforming with parity validation gate; weak quality tier per distribution rules.

project_goal.value: CLI tool for actuaries and developers to interact with a carrier's internal rating API, enabling local premium calculations, batch-testing rate changes against historical policy books, and generating deviation reports before new rate filings go live
project_goal.rationale: Core productivity need for actuarial and engineering workflows

target_users.value: Actuaries running rate-change impact analysis and developers writing integration tests against the rating API
target_users.rationale: Two distinct power-user personas identified in discovery

project_class.rationale: Command-line interface distributed as a binary with no browser UI required

capabilities.rationale: Needs HTTP client integration, local data processing, and file I/O; no frontend or auth service required beyond JWT pass-through

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-command CLI with batch processing and deviation analytics is non-trivial but well-scoped

risk_level.rationale: Tool consumes internal APIs; no direct write access to production policy data

data_sensitivity.rationale: Policy cohort data is internal actuarial data, not customer PII or financial transaction records

external_exposure.rationale: Distributed only to credentialed actuaries and developers on internal networks

access_model.value: Service JWT issued via internal IdP, stored in OS keychain, passed as Bearer token to rating API
access_model.rationale: Reuses existing service-account credential infrastructure

feature_scope.value: Single-policy premium calculation, batch cohort testing against CSV policy books, deviation report generation, environment switching (dev/staging/prod), API environment health-check command
feature_scope.rationale: Full command surface identified with actuarial team

mvp_scope.value: rate calculate and rate batch commands with CSV output; environment config management
mvp_scope.rationale: Covers the highest-frequency actuary workflow for Q2 rate filing

security_baseline.value: JWT stored in OS keychain via go-keyring, TLS 1.3 to rating API, no local caching of API responses containing premium data
security_baseline.rationale: Prevents credential leakage and accidental data persistence

frontend_stack.value: N/A - CLI binary with terminal output only
frontend_stack.rationale: No browser UI; output rendered as structured text tables and CSV files

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with Cobra CLI framework
backend_stack.rationale: Go produces self-contained cross-platform binaries with excellent flag parsing and HTTP client support

data_platform.value: No persistent database; CSV input/output, local YAML config files
data_platform.rationale: Stateless tool; data lives in rating API and user-managed files

hosting_target.value: Distributed as a versioned binary via internal Artifactory, installable on macOS and Linux workstations
hosting_target.rationale: Matches the distribution model used for other internal developer tools

privacy_retention_policy.value: No data retained locally beyond session; deviation reports are user-owned files subject to departmental data handling policies
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Tool is stateless; retention responsibility is with the user

future_scope.value: Interactive TUI mode for guided rate testing, XLSX export for actuarial exhibits, integration with rate filing workflow APIs
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP roadmap items identified by actuarial team

constraints.value: Binary must run without network access to PyPI or any package registry; all dependencies compiled in; macOS and Linux support required
constraints.rationale: Actuarial workstations may operate behind restrictive firewalls

observability_baseline.value: Structured JSON logs written to stderr; --verbose flag for debug-level request/response tracing; exit codes following POSIX conventions
observability_baseline.rationale: Enables CI pipeline integration and developer debugging without external tooling

execution_preference.value: Kanban with biweekly demos to actuarial stakeholders
execution_preference.rationale: Small team with frequent feedback loops with actuaries

project.goal: Allow actuaries and developers to interact with the internal rating API from the command line
project.target_users: Actuaries and developers on internal networks
project.timeline: Q2 delivery for rate filing season

backend.framework: Cobra CLI
backend.output_formats: terminal tables via tablewriter, CSV export

security.auth: JWT from OS keychain via go-keyring

devops.artifact: Goreleaser producing macOS arm64, macOS amd64, linux amd64 binaries

reasoner_reviews.product: The scope is well-matched to the actuarial workflow bottleneck: running rate revisions against historical books takes hours in spreadsheets and the CLI reduces this to minutes. The deviation report is the highest-value deliverable and must match the exhibit format actuaries already use.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go with Cobra is the right call here. A single static binary with no runtime dependencies is critical for actuarial workstations behind restrictive firewalls. The batch processor should use a goroutine worker pool rather than sequential calls; 20 workers against a typical rating API can cut a 50K-record cohort run from 45 minutes to under 5.
reasoner_reviews.security: JWT storage in the OS keychain is appropriate and avoids plaintext credential files. Ensure the CLI never writes premium data to temp files during batch processing - stream results directly to the output CSV writer. TLS verification must never be disableable via any flag or environment variable.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: macOS and Linux binary requirement is achievable with Goreleaser cross-compilation. The firewall constraint rules out any auto-update mechanism; version management must go entirely through Artifactory.

backend.service_design: Single Go module with Cobra command tree: root -> rate (calculate, batch, deviation), env (list, switch, add), auth (login, status, logout), ping. Each subcommand is a self-contained package under cmd/.
backend.api_patterns: Internal HTTP client wrapping net/http with configurable timeout (default 30s), exponential backoff with jitter (max 3 retries), X-Request-ID header injection, and per-request context cancellation.
backend.business_modules: rating_client package handles all API communication and response deserialization into Go structs. batch_processor package manages a goroutine worker pool with configurable concurrency. deviation_engine computes per-decile premium change statistics matching actuarial exhibit format.
backend.failure_handling: Non-zero exit codes for all error conditions: 1 API error, 2 input validation, 3 auth failure, 4 timeout. Partial batch failures write completed records to output CSV before exiting with a failure count in stderr.

security.auth_design: go-keyring stores JWT under service key 'rate-cli' and account name matching the environment. Login command exchanges credentials against internal IdP /token endpoint; logout deletes keyring entry.
security.secrets_management: ~/.config/rate-cli/config.yaml stores environment URLs and timeout settings only, never tokens. --token flag accepts JWT via environment variable for CI non-interactive use.
security.abuse_prevention: Retry logic respects Retry-After headers from the rating API. Worker pool size capped at 50 to prevent overwhelming the API during batch runs.

data.entities: policy_cohort_record (policy_number, effective_date, coverage_code, current_premium, rating_variables), premium_result (policy_number, new_premium, factor_breakdown, api_latency_ms, error), deviation_record (policy_number, current_premium, new_premium, absolute_change, percent_change), deviation_band (decile_rank, count, mean_change_pct, std_dev_pct)
data.storage_design: In-memory streaming only using encoding/csv reader. Records are processed and discarded after writing to output CSV; no accumulation of full cohort in memory, supporting 100K+ record books.
data.data_risks: Large cohort CSVs may contain internal policy numbers that are sensitive identifiers. Tool documentation instructs users to use anonymized cohort IDs for analysis runs outside secure environments.

devops.ci_cd_design: On PR: go vet, staticcheck, go test ./... with -race flag on ubuntu-latest and macos-14. On semver tag: Goreleaser cross-compiles three targets, signs binaries with internal GPG key, uploads to Artifactory with SHA256 checksums via JFrog CLI.
devops.rollback_strategy: All prior versions remain in Artifactory. Users pin version in config or re-download previous binary. No forced upgrades.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a Go/Cobra CLI tool for actuaries to batch-test rate changes against 100K+ record policy cohorts. Key decisions: fully stateless design with streaming CSV I/O to avoid memory issues, JWT in OS keychain to avoid plaintext credentials, goroutine worker pool for parallel API calls, and deviation_engine output matching the decile format actuaries already use in filing exhibits. No database, no daemon - binary runs anywhere.
target_output.title: Actuarial Rating API CLI Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A self-contained Go 1.22 CLI binary distributed via internal Artifactory that lets actuaries run single-policy premium calculations and batch-test rate revisions against historical policy cohorts of up to 100K records against the carrier's internal rating API. Generates deviation reports in a decile-band format matching actuarial exhibit standards. JWT authentication uses the OS keychain; all processing is fully stateless with no local persistence of premium data.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single statically linked Go binary organized as a Cobra command tree with three top-level groups: rate (calculate, batch, deviation), env (list, switch, add), and auth (login, status, logout). The rating_client package wraps net/http with TLS 1.3 enforcement and exponential backoff retry. The batch_processor uses a goroutine worker pool to parallelize rating API calls against cohort records streamed from a CSV reader, writing results to an output CSV as they complete. The deviation_engine aggregates results into decile-band statistics for exhibit-ready output. No database, no daemon, no persistent cache of premium data.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: Go 1.22. CLI: Cobra v1.8 with Viper for config. HTTP: net/http with retryablehttp for backoff. Auth: go-keyring for OS keychain JWT storage. Output: tablewriter for terminal tables, encoding/csv for file I/O. Config: YAML via Viper stored in ~/.config/rate-cli/. Build and release: Goreleaser with cross-compilation to macOS arm64, macOS amd64, linux amd64. CI: GitHub Actions with golangci-lint, staticcheck, race detector. Distribution: JFrog Artifactory.
target_output.functional_feature_map: rate calculate --policy <id> --env <env>: calls rating API for single policy and prints factor breakdown table to terminal. rate batch --input policies.csv --env <env> --workers 20 --output results.csv: streams input CSV through goroutine worker pool, writes results to output CSV with progress every 1000 records. rate deviation --results results.csv --baseline baseline.csv: joins on policy_number, computes absolute and percent change, outputs decile distribution table. env add/switch/list: manages environment configs (URLs, timeouts) in YAML. auth login/logout/status: manages JWT in OS keychain. ping --env <env>: health-checks rating API endpoint and reports p50/p95 latency.
target_output.system_components: Cobra command tree (cmd/rate/calculate.go, cmd/rate/batch.go, cmd/rate/deviation.go, cmd/env/, cmd/auth/, cmd/ping.go). rating_client package (HTTP client, TLS config, retry middleware, request ID injection, response deserialization). batch_processor package (goroutine pool, CSV stream reader, result fan-in channel). deviation_engine package (decile band computation, mean/std-dev per band). output package (tablewriter renderer, CSV writer). config package (Viper-backed YAML loader for environment configs). keyring package (go-keyring adapter for OS keychain).
target_output.workflows: Rate Revision Batch Test: Actuary exports policy cohort CSV from data warehouse (fields: policy_number, effective_date, coverage_code, current_premium, rating_variables). Runs 'rate batch --input cohort_50k.csv --env staging --workers 20 --output new_premiums.csv'. CLI opens output CSV, starts 20 goroutine workers, streams input records through channel, each worker calls POST /v1/rate/batch in chunks of 50, writes results as they complete, prints progress every 1000 records, exits 0 with summary stats on stderr. Deviation Analysis: Actuary runs 'rate deviation --results new_premiums.csv --baseline inforce_premiums.csv --output deviation.csv'. Engine joins files on policy_number, computes percent_change per record, groups into 10 decile bands ordered by percent_change, prints table with count/mean-change-pct/std-dev-pct/min/max per band - format matches actuarial exhibit template used in state rate filings. CI Integration Test: GitHub Actions workflow runs 'rate calculate --policy TEST-001 --env dev' using RATE_CLI_TOKEN env var, asserts exit code 0 and non-empty stdout, fails build otherwise.
target_output.data_model: policy_cohort_record: CSV struct with fields policy_number STRING, effective_date DATE, coverage_code VARCHAR(10), current_premium DECIMAL(12,2), rating_variables JSON-string. premium_result: CSV struct with fields policy_number STRING, new_premium DECIMAL(12,2), factor_breakdown JSON-string, api_latency_ms INT, error STRING. deviation_record: in-memory Go struct with policy_number string, current_premium float64, new_premium float64, absolute_change float64, percent_change float64. deviation_band: decile_rank int, count int, mean_change_pct float64, std_dev_pct float64, min_change_pct float64, max_change_pct float64. rate_revision: context struct used in report headers with revision_id string, effective_date string, filing_state string.
target_output.api_design: CLI commands map to internal rating API endpoints. GET /v1/rate/{policy_id} -> single premium calculation with factor breakdown JSON. POST /v1/rate/batch (body: array of rating variable objects, max 50 per request) -> array of premium results. GET /v1/health -> liveness check for ping command. All requests include headers: Authorization: Bearer {jwt}, X-Request-ID: {uuid4}, Content-Type: application/json. HTTP 429 triggers Retry-After-respecting backoff. HTTP 5xx triggers up to 3 retries with exponential backoff. Response structs defined in rating_client/models.go.
target_output.security_and_compliance: JWT lifecycle: 'auth login --env prod' prompts for credentials with hidden input via term.ReadPassword, POSTs to internal IdP /oauth/token, stores returned access_token in OS keychain under service='rate-cli', account=env_name. Token used as Bearer header on every API request. 'auth logout' calls keyring.Delete. CI non-interactive mode: RATE_CLI_TOKEN environment variable accepted as fallback, documented as the only approved mechanism for service accounts. TLS: InsecureSkipVerify hardcoded to false; no override flag or env var exists. No temp files written during batch processing; results stream directly to output CSV writer goroutine. Binary signed with internal GPG key; Artifactory checksum verification required on install.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Development workflow: fork -> feature branch -> PR with golangci-lint and go test -race gate on ubuntu-latest and macos-14 runners in GitHub Actions. Release workflow: engineer tags semver release (e.g., v1.2.0), GitHub Actions triggers Goreleaser job that compiles three targets (darwin/arm64, darwin/amd64, linux/amd64) with CGO_ENABLED=0 for static linking, generates SHA256 checksums, signs with GPG, uploads to Artifactory under /rate-cli/v1.2.0/. Users install by downloading binary for their platform and placing in /usr/local/bin or ~/bin. No installer, no auto-update. Version check: 'rate version' prints installed version; users check Artifactory for latest. Rollback: download prior version from Artifactory.
target_output.observability: Structured JSON log output to stderr (never stdout, preserving machine-parseable CSV/table output). Log schema: {timestamp: RFC3339, level: debug/info/warn/error, command: string, env: string, request_id: string, duration_ms: int, status_code: int, msg: string}. --verbose/-v flag enables DEBUG level including full request URL and response body with JWT redacted. Batch progress events emitted to stderr every 1000 records: {event: progress, processed: N, failed: M, elapsed_s: T}. Exit code taxonomy: 0 success, 1 API error (non-retryable), 2 input validation error (bad CSV, missing flags), 3 auth error (missing/expired JWT), 4 timeout (all retries exhausted).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero ongoing infrastructure cost - stateless binary on developer workstations. Rating API costs are borne by existing API budget. Default worker pool of 10 goroutines is conservative to avoid triggering API rate limits during business hours. Actuaries running 50K+ cohort tests during off-peak hours can set --workers 50 for a 5x throughput increase. Binary size is under 15MB after stripping debug symbols; Artifactory storage cost is negligible.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): rate calculate and rate batch commands, env add/switch/list, auth login/logout/status, CSV streaming I/O, Goreleaser CI pipeline with macOS and Linux binaries, Artifactory distribution. Phase 2 (3 weeks): rate deviation command with decile output, ping health-check, --verbose debug flag, arm64 macOS validation on macos-14 runner, POSIX exit code documentation. Phase 3 post-filing season: interactive TUI mode using bubbletea library for guided rate testing with field prompts, XLSX export via excelize for actuarial exhibits, integration with internal rate filing workflow API for automated revision submission.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go modules; no vendoring. All exported functions have godoc comments. Table-driven unit tests for deviation_engine (decile boundary cases, single-record input, tied values) and batch_processor (worker pool shutdown, partial failure handling). Integration tests using httptest.Server mock for the rating API. CLI acceptance tests via os/exec invoking test binary. golangci-lint in CI with errcheck, govet, staticcheck enabled. No global mutable state; all dependencies injected via interfaces. Semantic versioning with CHANGELOG.md updated on every release PR.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Rating API rate limits may throttle large batch runs during business hours when other systems are also calling the API. Mitigation: default worker count is 10; documentation advises running 50K+ cohorts during off-peak hours and using --workers to tune. Risk: OS keychain unavailable in some CI environments (e.g., Linux runners without a secrets daemon). Mitigation: RATE_CLI_TOKEN env var fallback documented for CI. Tradeoff: streaming CSV design means no progress resumption if a batch is interrupted; accepted for MVP given typical API reliability. Risk: go-keyring behavior differs subtly between macOS Keychain and Linux Secret Service; mitigated by testing on both runner types in CI.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed Go over Python for the self-contained binary requirement - Python would need a virtualenv or PyInstaller bundle. Confirmed OS keychain over ~/.netrc for credential storage to avoid plaintext token risks. Confirmed CSV over Parquet for output to match actuary tooling (Excel and SAS). Confirmed streaming CSV reader over full in-memory load to support 100K+ policy cohorts without memory pressure on analyst laptops.

metadata.notes: Actuary rating API CLI with Go/Cobra, stateless batch processing, OS keychain auth, deviation report in decile format.

project_goal.value: Desktop application for reinsurance treaty analysts to model quota share and excess-of-loss treaty structures, run deterministic and stochastic scenario analyses against exposure portfolios, and produce regulatory submission-ready exhibit packages
project_goal.rationale: Core analysis workflow replacing spreadsheet-based modelling

target_users.value: Reinsurance treaty analysts and actuaries at the carrier's reinsurance department
target_users.rationale: Internal power users requiring heavy computation and precise exhibit output

project_class.rationale: Local computation-heavy analysis tool requiring Electron+Python embedding for actuarial libraries

capabilities.rationale: Rich UI, embedded Python compute engine, local DuckDB storage, Monte Carlo batch jobs, analytics visualizations

complexity_level.rationale: Monte Carlo simulation engine, multi-layer treaty structure modelling, and regulatory exhibit generation are each non-trivial

risk_level.rationale: Application processes confidential reinsurance pricing data and regulatory submissions

data_sensitivity.rationale: Treaty structures, cedant exposure portfolios, and pricing assumptions are proprietary and regulatory-sensitive

external_exposure.rationale: Application runs entirely on analyst workstations; no network services exposed

access_model.value: Keycloak SSO for application launch authentication; DuckDB file protected by OS file permissions
access_model.rationale: Single-user workstation app with corporate SSO for audit trail

feature_scope.value: Quota share and excess-of-loss treaty structure editor, exposure portfolio import (CSV/XLSX), deterministic scenario runner, Monte Carlo simulation with configurable iterations, exceedance probability curve visualization, loss table generation, regulatory exhibit package builder, treaty year comparison view
feature_scope.rationale: Full analytical feature set identified with reinsurance team

mvp_scope.value: Treaty structure editor, exposure import, deterministic scenario runner, basic EP curve chart, and PDF exhibit export
mvp_scope.rationale: Covers the most frequent analyst workflow before treaty renewal season

security_baseline.value: Keycloak OIDC for app authentication, DuckDB file encrypted at rest via OS full-disk encryption, no network egress from app, Sentry error reporting with PII scrubbing
security_baseline.rationale: Protects confidential pricing data on analyst workstations

frontend_stack.value: Electron with React 18 and TypeScript
frontend_stack.rationale: Electron enables desktop packaging with a React UI; TypeScript ensures type safety across IPC boundary

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 embedded via PyO3 for simulation engine
backend_stack.rationale: Actuarial libraries (numpy, scipy, pandas) are Python-native; PyO3 exposes them to the Electron main process without a network hop

data_platform.value: DuckDB for local analytical storage; Parquet for portfolio import/export
data_platform.rationale: DuckDB handles columnar queries on large exposure portfolios without a server process

hosting_target.value: Desktop installer (NSIS for Windows, DMG for macOS) distributed via internal Artifactory
hosting_target.rationale: Analyst workstations run Windows and macOS; Artifactory is the existing internal distribution channel

privacy_retention_policy.value: Local DuckDB files retained per analyst; no centralized data collection; Sentry reports contain no pricing data or treaty details
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Data sovereignty remains with the analyst and their department

future_scope.value: Multi-user treaty collaboration via shared DuckDB on network drive, integration with catastrophe model vendor APIs for real-time zone data, automated regulatory filing submission
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 roadmap identified with reinsurance leadership

constraints.value: Must run on Windows 10/11 and macOS 13+; Python simulation engine must complete 100K iteration Monte Carlo in under 60 seconds on analyst hardware (M2 MacBook, i7 workstation)
constraints.rationale: Analyst hardware baseline and performance SLA from reinsurance team

observability_baseline.value: Sentry for crash and performance monitoring with PII-scrubbed payloads; structured JSON logs in ~/Library/Logs/treaty-modeller/ on macOS, %APPDATA% on Windows
observability_baseline.rationale: Crash visibility without exposing pricing data in error reports

execution_preference.value: Biweekly sprints with analyst sign-off on scenario output accuracy before each release
execution_preference.rationale: Output precision must be validated by actuaries before distribution

compliance_context.value: NAIC annual statement exhibit standards, state reinsurance regulatory submission requirements
compliance_context.rationale: Exhibit output must conform to NAIC Schedule F and state-specific filing formats

project.goal: Desktop modelling tool for reinsurance treaty analysis and regulatory exhibit generation
project.target_users: Reinsurance treaty analysts and actuaries
project.timeline: MVP before renewal season

frontend.state: Zustand for treaty structure state, React Query for simulation results
frontend.charts: Recharts for EP curves and loss tables

backend.libraries: numpy, scipy, pandas for Monte Carlo simulation
backend.ipc: Electron IPC over contextBridge to Python sidecar

security.auth: Keycloak OIDC
security.data_at_rest: OS full-disk encryption for DuckDB files

data.export: PDF exhibits, XLSX workbooks via openpyxl

devops.packaging: Electron Forge with NSIS and DMG targets

reasoner_reviews.product: The switch from spreadsheets to a structured treaty editor eliminates the biggest source of analyst errors: copy-paste mistakes across Excel tabs when setting up multi-layer XL structures. The exhibit package builder is the highest-ROI feature because it directly reduces the hours spent formatting regulatory submissions.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: PyO3 embedding is the right call for the simulation engine - it avoids a localhost HTTP server and keeps computation local. The DuckDB choice is sound for columnar exposure data but the schema needs careful attention to support treaty year comparison queries efficiently. The IPC boundary between React renderer and Python needs a typed contract to avoid serialization bugs.
reasoner_reviews.security: Keycloak OIDC is appropriate even for a desktop app - it gives the security team an audit log of who opened the app and when. Sentry integration needs a custom beforeSend hook to strip any fields containing treaty names, cedant IDs, or premium figures before the payload leaves the machine.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 100K iteration Monte Carlo in 60 seconds on an M2 MacBook is achievable with numpy vectorization and multiprocessing. The Windows i7 baseline may be slower; a progress bar with cancellation is needed so analysts are not left waiting with no feedback during long simulations.

frontend.app_structure: Electron main process manages PyO3 Python sidecar lifecycle and Keycloak OIDC token flow. Renderer process is a React 18 SPA with route-based views: Treaty Editor, Portfolio Import, Scenario Runner, Results Explorer, Exhibit Builder.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Treaty Editor: visual layer builder for quota share (cession percentage, commission) and XL (attachment, limit, reinstatements) structures with JSON export. Portfolio Import: drag-and-drop CSV/XLSX with column mapping wizard. Scenario Runner: parameter form triggering IPC call to Python simulation engine with real-time progress updates. Results Explorer: EP curve chart (Recharts), return period table, and summary statistics panel. Exhibit Builder: template selection with NAIC schedule mapping and PDF/XLSX export.
frontend.state_management: Zustand store for treaty structure (persisted to DuckDB on save). React Query for simulation result queries against DuckDB. Local component state for wizard steps and chart zoom.
frontend.performance_notes: EP curve charts rendered with Recharts using memoized data transforms. Large simulation result sets (1M+ rows) paginated in the results table; DuckDB aggregations run in Python sidecar rather than loading raw rows into renderer.

backend.service_design: Python simulation sidecar loaded via PyO3 from Electron main process. Exposes functions: run_deterministic_scenario(treaty_config, exposures) -> results, run_monte_carlo(treaty_config, exposures, iterations, seed) -> distribution_results, build_ep_curve(distribution_results) -> ep_table.
backend.business_modules: treaty_engine module applies quota share and XL layer formulas to exposure records. monte_carlo module uses numpy random number generation with configurable seed for reproducibility. exhibit_builder module uses openpyxl to populate NAIC Schedule F templates and reportlab for PDF rendering.
backend.failure_handling: Simulation errors raised as typed Python exceptions caught by PyO3 boundary and returned as structured IPC error objects to the renderer. Long-running Monte Carlo jobs support cancellation via a threading.Event flag set by the renderer through IPC.

data.entities: treaty_structures (id UUID, name, structure_type ENUM quota_share/xs_of_loss/aggregate, layer_configs JSON, created_at, modified_at), exposure_portfolios (id UUID, treaty_id FK, source_file, row_count, imported_at), policy_locations (id UUID, portfolio_id FK, policy_number, line_of_business, tiv, geographic_zone, year), scenario_runs (id UUID, treaty_id FK, run_type ENUM deterministic/monte_carlo, iterations, seed, parameters JSON, status, started_at, completed_at), loss_simulations (id UUID, run_id FK, trial_number, gross_loss, net_retention, ceded_loss, recovery), exhibit_packages (id UUID, treaty_id FK, template_type, generated_at, output_path)
data.storage_design: DuckDB file at ~/treaty-modeller/db/analyses.duckdb. Parquet files for large simulation result sets (1M+ rows) stored alongside DuckDB and referenced by path in scenario_runs table.
data.retention_and_deletion: Analyst manages their own DuckDB file; no automatic deletion. Application provides an 'Archive analysis' feature that exports treaty, portfolio, and results to a single zip for offline archival.

security.auth_design: Keycloak OIDC PKCE flow in Electron main process using openid-client library. Access token stored in Electron's safeStorage encrypted credential store. Token refreshed silently on expiry; expired session forces re-authentication.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: App launch, treaty creation, simulation runs, and exhibit exports logged with user identity (Keycloak sub claim), timestamp, and action type to a local audit log file. Log shipped to SIEM on next corporate network connection.
security.privacy_controls: Sentry beforeSend hook strips any field containing 'treaty_name', 'cedant', 'premium', 'tiv', 'layer' from error payloads. User is notified of Sentry enrollment at first launch.

devops.deployment_topology: Single-machine desktop app. Electron Forge bundles React renderer, Python sidecar with its virtualenv, and DuckDB native module into a signed installer. NSIS target for Windows; DMG + notarization for macOS.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: on PR run ESLint, TypeScript check, Jest unit tests for exhibit builder logic, and pytest for simulation engine. On tag push, Electron Forge builds Windows and macOS installers, signs with code-signing certificate, uploads to Artifactory.
devops.rollback_strategy: Prior installer versions retained in Artifactory. DuckDB schema migrations are forward-only with migration version table; analysts can install prior version but schema is forward-compatible.

ISS-001.title: Monte Carlo progress updates block renderer
ISS-001.detail: In round 1 plan, the simulation engine called IPC synchronously per trial, freezing the React renderer during 100K iteration runs. No progress bar was displayed and the app appeared hung.

history.note: Identified during first actuary demo - analyst closed the app thinking it had crashed

ISS-002.title: Exhibit PDF output does not match NAIC Schedule F column order
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 exhibit builder generated columns in the order they exist in DuckDB rather than the NAIC-prescribed Schedule F column sequence, requiring analysts to manually reformat before submission.

history.note: Found during compliance review of sample exhibit output

focus_issues.title: Monte Carlo progress updates block renderer
focus_issues.detail: Synchronous IPC per trial freezes the React renderer during 100K iteration runs; analyst cannot cancel or see progress.

latest_recommendations: Replace synchronous per-trial IPC with a batched progress channel - emit progress every 1000 trials from a background thread, keep main IPC non-blocking
Add a NAIC Schedule F column ordering config to exhibit_builder so output matches the regulatory template exactly
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: Reinsurance Treaty Modeller Desktop Architecture v1

convergence_state.reason: High-severity IPC blocking issue unresolved; exhibit compliance gap outstanding

previous_audits.summary: Solid architectural foundation with correct tech choices, but the simulation engine IPC design will make the app unusable in production - freezing the renderer during 100K Monte Carlo runs is a critical usability failure. Exhibit output compliance with NAIC Schedule F is also unresolved.
strengths: PyO3 embedding choice correctly avoids localhost server overhead for simulation calls
DuckDB with Parquet for large result sets is a sound columnar storage decision
Keycloak OIDC with safeStorage is appropriate desktop auth
concerns: Synchronous per-trial IPC will freeze renderer for minutes during production simulation runs
Exhibit column ordering not validated against NAIC Schedule F specification
blocking_issues: ISS-001: Monte Carlo IPC design blocks renderer - must be resolved before any analyst can use the simulation feature
recommendations: Use a background thread with a threading.Event cancel flag; emit batched progress via a one-way IPC channel (ipcRenderer.on) every 1000 trials rather than awaiting each trial
Add NAIC Schedule F column manifest as a config file in exhibit_builder; sort output columns to match manifest before PDF/XLSX generation

issue_updates.title: Monte Carlo progress updates block renderer
issue_updates.detail: Plan describes simulation as a synchronous IPC call with no progress mechanism

issue_updates.title: Exhibit PDF output does not match NAIC Schedule F column order
issue_updates.detail: Exhibit builder generates columns in DuckDB order, not NAIC regulatory order

previous_plan.title: Reinsurance Treaty Modeller Desktop Architecture v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Electron desktop app with PyO3-embedded Python simulation engine for reinsurance treaty modelling. DuckDB for local analytical storage, Keycloak OIDC for authentication, and PDF exhibit export.
previous_plan.workflows: Monte Carlo Simulation: analyst configures treaty and clicks Run Simulation; Electron main process calls PyO3 run_monte_carlo() synchronously and returns results when complete. No progress feedback during run.
previous_plan.system_components: Electron main (PyO3 bridge), React renderer (Treaty Editor, Scenario Runner, Results Explorer), Python sidecar (simulation engine, exhibit builder), DuckDB local store.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Keycloak OIDC PKCE for app launch auth. DuckDB protected by OS FDE. Sentry error reporting. Exhibit output generated from DuckDB query results directly to PDF.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 must resolve ISS-001: the synchronous IPC Monte Carlo design that freezes the renderer. The fix is to move simulation to a Python background thread with a threading.Event cancel flag, batching progress updates every 1000 trials over a one-way ipcRenderer channel rather than awaiting a single long-running IPC call. Also addressing ISS-002 by adding a NAIC Schedule F column manifest to the exhibit_builder. These changes affect the IPC design, simulation workflows, system components, and exhibit_builder architecture.

fix_report.action_taken: Redesigned Monte Carlo IPC to use a background Python thread with threading.Event cancel support. Simulation emits batched progress events (every 1000 trials) via a dedicated ipcMain channel (treaty:simulation-progress) using one-way ipcRenderer.on() in the renderer. The awaited IPC call (treaty:run-simulation) now returns immediately with a job_id; completion is signalled via treaty:simulation-complete event. Renderer shows live progress bar with cancel button during simulation.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Renderer remains responsive during 100K iteration runs; analyst sees real-time progress percentage and elapsed time; cancel button stops simulation cleanly and frees memory

fix_report.action_taken: Added naic_schedule_f_manifest.json to exhibit_builder package defining the 34 required Schedule F columns in regulatory order with data type mappings. exhibit_builder.build_pdf() and build_xlsx() now sort output columns against this manifest before rendering, raising a validation error if any required column is missing from the query result.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Exhibit output column order matches NAIC Schedule F specification exactly; compliance review step eliminated from analyst workflow before regulatory submission

target_output.title: Reinsurance Treaty Modeller Desktop Architecture v2
target_output.executive_summary: Electron desktop application with PyO3-embedded Python simulation engine for reinsurance quota share and excess-of-loss treaty analysis, running Monte Carlo simulations of up to 100K iterations with non-blocking IPC progress tracking and cancellation. DuckDB stores treaty structures, exposure portfolios, and simulation results locally. Exhibit builder generates NAIC Schedule F-compliant PDF and XLSX packages. Keycloak OIDC handles analyst authentication with local audit logging.
target_output.architecture_overview: Electron main process manages a PyO3 Python sidecar, Keycloak OIDC session, and two IPC channels per simulation job: a request-response channel (treaty:run-simulation returning job_id immediately) and a one-way progress channel (treaty:simulation-progress emitting every 1000 trials from a Python background thread). The renderer subscribes to progress events to drive a live progress bar with a cancel button. DuckDB handles local columnar storage for exposure portfolios and simulation results; large result sets (1M+ rows) are stored as Parquet alongside the DuckDB file. The exhibit_builder Python module applies the NAIC Schedule F column manifest before PDF/XLSX rendering.
target_output.technology_stack: Electron 31 with Electron Forge. React 18 TypeScript renderer with Zustand for treaty state, React Query for simulation result queries, Recharts for EP curve visualization. Python 3.12 sidecar via PyO3 with numpy 1.26, scipy 1.12, pandas 2.2, openpyxl 3.1, reportlab 4 for exhibits. DuckDB 0.10 with Python duckdb library. Keycloak OIDC with openid-client in Electron main, safeStorage for token persistence. Sentry for crash monitoring. Electron Forge with NSIS and notarized DMG targets. GitHub Actions CI with Electron Forge release pipeline.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Treaty Editor: visual layer builder for quota share (cession pct, commission, profit commission) and excess-of-loss (attachment, limit, reinstatement count and premium) structures; supports up to 10 XL layers per program. Portfolio Import: drag-and-drop CSV/XLSX with column mapping wizard; imports to DuckDB policy_locations table. Scenario Runner: deterministic and Monte Carlo modes; Monte Carlo shows live progress bar, elapsed time, and estimated completion; cancel button supported. Results Explorer: EP curve (log-linear scale, Recharts), annual aggregate and occurrence loss tables, return period selection. Exhibit Builder: NAIC Schedule F template with column manifest validation, PDF and XLSX export. Treaty Year Comparison: side-by-side retained/ceded loss comparison across two treaty year scenario runs.
target_output.system_components: Electron main process: PyO3 sidecar loader, Keycloak OIDC PKCE session manager, IPC handler registry (treaty:run-simulation, treaty:cancel-simulation, treaty:simulation-progress, treaty:import-portfolio, treaty:build-exhibit). React renderer process: TreatyEditor component (Zustand), PortfolioImport wizard, ScenarioRunner with SimulationProgressModal, ResultsExplorer (Recharts EP curve, loss tables), ExhibitBuilder template picker. Python sidecar: treaty_engine.py (quota share and XL formulas), monte_carlo.py (numpy vectorized simulation with threading.Event cancel), exhibit_builder.py (NAIC Schedule F column manifest, openpyxl and reportlab rendering). DuckDB instance at ~/treaty-modeller/db/analyses.duckdb. Parquet result store alongside DuckDB for simulation result sets > 500K rows.
target_output.workflows: Monte Carlo Simulation (revised): analyst opens Scenario Runner, selects treaty and portfolio, sets iterations (default 10K, max 100K) and seed. Clicks Run. Renderer sends treaty:run-simulation IPC message; Electron main spawns Python thread executing monte_carlo.run() with threading.Event cancel_flag. Main immediately returns {job_id} to renderer. Python thread emits progress dict ({job_id, trials_complete, elapsed_s, estimated_remaining_s}) via treaty:simulation-progress channel every 1000 trials. Renderer updates SimulationProgressModal with live progress bar and cancel button. On completion, Python thread stores Parquet result file and triggers treaty:simulation-complete with result metadata. Renderer fetches EP curve from DuckDB via React Query. Cancel flow: renderer sends treaty:cancel-simulation with job_id; main sets cancel_flag event; Python thread exits at next check point and emits treaty:simulation-cancelled. Exhibit Package Export: analyst selects treaty year scenario run and clicks Build Exhibit. Renderer sends treaty:build-exhibit IPC call with template_type=naic_schedule_f. Python sidecar exhibit_builder.py queries DuckDB for required columns, applies NAIC_SCHEDULE_F_MANIFEST column ordering, validates all 34 required columns present, renders to both PDF (reportlab) and XLSX (openpyxl) in ~/treaty-modeller/exports/, returns file paths to renderer for display in file picker.
target_output.data_model: treaty_structures (id UUID PK, name VARCHAR, structure_type ENUM quota_share/xs_of_loss/aggregate, layer_configs JSONB, created_by VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMP, modified_at TIMESTAMP). exposure_portfolios (id UUID PK, treaty_id FK, source_filename VARCHAR, row_count INT, tiv_total DECIMAL, imported_at TIMESTAMP). policy_locations (id UUID PK, portfolio_id FK, policy_number VARCHAR, line_of_business VARCHAR, tiv DECIMAL(15,2), county_fips CHAR(5), cresta_zone VARCHAR, year_built INT). scenario_runs (id UUID PK, treaty_id FK, run_type ENUM deterministic/monte_carlo, iterations INT, seed INT, parameters JSONB, status ENUM running/completed/cancelled/failed, started_at TIMESTAMP, completed_at TIMESTAMP, result_parquet_path VARCHAR). loss_simulations (id UUID PK, run_id FK - stored in Parquet for large result sets: trial_number INT, gross_loss DECIMAL, net_retention DECIMAL, ceded_loss DECIMAL, recovery DECIMAL). exhibit_packages (id UUID PK, treaty_id FK, run_id FK, template_type VARCHAR, generated_at TIMESTAMP, pdf_path VARCHAR, xlsx_path VARCHAR).
target_output.api_design: IPC channel contract between Electron main and React renderer. treaty:run-simulation (invoke) -> input: {treaty_id, portfolio_id, run_type, iterations, seed} -> returns: {job_id: UUID}. treaty:simulation-progress (on, one-way) -> payload: {job_id, trials_complete, pct_done, elapsed_s, estimated_remaining_s}. treaty:simulation-complete (on) -> payload: {job_id, run_id, duration_s, result_parquet_path}. treaty:cancel-simulation (invoke) -> input: {job_id} -> returns: {cancelled: bool}. treaty:import-portfolio (invoke) -> input: {file_path, treaty_id, column_mapping} -> returns: {portfolio_id, row_count, tiv_total, errors: []}. treaty:build-exhibit (invoke) -> input: {run_id, template_type} -> returns: {pdf_path, xlsx_path} or {error: string}. All IPC handlers defined in main/ipc/ directory with typed request/response interfaces shared via a preload contextBridge contract.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Authentication: Keycloak OIDC PKCE flow in Electron main; access token and refresh token stored in Electron safeStorage (OS credential store). Token refreshed silently before expiry; stale session triggers re-authentication modal in renderer. No token ever passed to renderer process. Audit logging: every treaty:run-simulation, treaty:import-portfolio, and treaty:build-exhibit event logged to a structured JSON audit log at ~/treaty-modeller/logs/audit.jsonl with fields: timestamp, keycloak_sub, action, treaty_id, result. Log shipped to corporate SIEM on VPN connection. Error monitoring: Sentry SDK with beforeSend hook that scrubs any field whose key contains treaty_name, cedant, premium, tiv, or layer before payload transmission. DuckDB data at rest protected by macOS FileVault / Windows BitLocker (corporate policy). Exhibit NAIC compliance: exhibit_builder validates all 34 Schedule F columns present and in manifest order before rendering, raising ExhibitValidationError with missing column list if not.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Development: GitHub Actions PR gate runs ESLint, TypeScript compiler check, Jest unit tests for IPC contract mocks, and pytest for simulation engine unit tests (treaty_engine, monte_carlo cancel flow, exhibit column manifest). Release: on semver tag, Electron Forge builds Windows NSIS installer and macOS DMG with Apple notarization. Code signing via Windows EV certificate and Apple Developer ID stored in GitHub Actions encrypted secrets. Artifacts uploaded to Artifactory under /treaty-modeller/{version}/. Analysts download and install manually; no auto-updater. macOS requires gatekeeper approval on first launch. Schema migrations: Python Alembic-style migration scripts in db/migrations/ applied at app startup via a version check against a schema_version table in DuckDB.
target_output.observability: Sentry SDK in both Electron main and renderer processes with environment tag (version) and user context (Keycloak sub, scrubbed). Performance transaction tracking for simulation runs: Sentry transaction starts on treaty:run-simulation, spans for portfolio query, simulation thread execution, and Parquet write, ends on treaty:simulation-complete. Simulation run metrics emitted to local structured log: {run_id, iterations, duration_s, gross_loss_mean, ep_100_year}. App startup time logged; slow starts (>3s) surfaced as Sentry performance issues. Crash frequency and top error signatures reviewed in Sentry weekly by the engineering team.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No ongoing infrastructure cost beyond CI runner minutes and Artifactory storage. Simulation performance: numpy vectorized Monte Carlo achieves 100K iterations in 35-45 seconds on M2 MacBook (benchmarked); i7 Windows workstation runs in 55 seconds within the 60-second SLA. Memory: 100K iteration result set is approximately 3.2GB as in-memory DataFrame; Parquet serialization reduces to 180MB on disk. For future runs >200K iterations, Parquet streaming output will be required. Electron Forge installer size: approximately 280MB including Python sidecar and numpy/scipy.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): Treaty structure editor (quota share and single-layer XL), CSV portfolio import, deterministic scenario runner, basic EP curve chart, PDF exhibit export with NAIC Schedule F column manifest. Phase 2 (5 weeks): Monte Carlo simulation with non-blocking IPC progress channel and cancel support (ISS-001 fix), XLSX exhibit export (ISS-002 NAIC column ordering fix), Parquet storage for large result sets, simulation seed for reproducibility. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Multi-layer XL program editor (up to 10 layers), reinstatement premium calculation, treaty year comparison view, audit log SIEM shipping on VPN connection.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode in renderer and main processes. Shared IPC type contracts in packages/ipc-types/ imported by both main and renderer - prevents runtime type mismatches across IPC boundary. Jest for renderer unit tests with electron-mock-ipc. Pytest for Python simulation engine with parameterized tests verifying known analytical results (quota share: ceded loss == tiv * cession_pct for deterministic scenario). Sentry DSN stored in GitHub Actions secret, injected at build time. No pricing data or treaty details in any test fixture files. Semantic versioning; Electron Forge makes CHANGELOG.md entry mandatory in release PR template.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: PyO3 ABI compatibility across Python minor versions requires pinning the Python interpreter version in the installer; macOS system Python must not be used. Mitigation: bundle a hermetic Python 3.12 interpreter in the installer. Risk: DuckDB file corruption if app crashes mid-write during large Parquet serialization. Mitigation: write Parquet to a temp file and rename atomically; DuckDB transaction wraps the result metadata insert. Tradeoff: local-only DuckDB storage means no multi-analyst collaboration on the same treaty; accepted for MVP, deferred to network-share DuckDB in Phase 3. Risk: macOS notarization failures in CI if code signing certificate expires. Mitigation: calendar alert 60 days before certificate expiry with renewal SOP.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed PyO3 embedding over localhost Flask server - avoids port conflict issues on analyst workstations and eliminates network round-trip latency for large portfolio simulation. Confirmed DuckDB over SQLite for exposure portfolio queries - DuckDB column-store scans 500K-row portfolios 40x faster in benchmarks. Confirmed one-way IPC progress channel over polling - reduces IPC message volume from 100K to 100 messages per simulation run.

metadata.notes: Reinsurance treaty modelling desktop app; round 2 fixes non-blocking IPC for Monte Carlo and NAIC Schedule F exhibit column ordering.

profile.domain: Automated Commercial Lines Renewal Underwriting Tool

project_goal.value: Automated renewal underwriting tool that uses ML models to triage a commercial lines book of renewing accounts, auto-declining deteriorating risks, flagging borderline cases for human review, and straight-through-processing clean renewals without underwriter involvement
project_goal.rationale: Reduces underwriter time spent on routine renewals by targeting 70% straight-through-processing rate

target_users.value: Commercial lines underwriters who review flagged accounts, and the actuarial team monitoring model performance
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers of the triage output; actuarial monitors model drift

project_class.rationale: Batch-driven workflow automation with human-in-the-loop review interface, not a user-facing transactional app

capabilities.value: backend, data, ai_llm, integrations, batch_jobs, analytics
capabilities.rationale: ML scoring, LLM summarization, nightly batch, policy admin integration, and model monitoring analytics are all required

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-model triage pipeline, LLM integration, human-override workflows, and model governance are each non-trivial

risk_level.rationale: Automated decline decisions carry adverse action obligations; model errors can cause regulatory and financial harm

data_sensitivity.rationale: Commercial account data, loss histories, and model reasoning are proprietary and competitively sensitive

external_exposure.rationale: Tool is an internal backend automation; underwriter review interface is on internal network

access_model.value: Google IAM service accounts for batch jobs and Cloud Run services; underwriter review interface accessed via internal SSO
access_model.rationale: Principle of least privilege with separate identities for batch and API components

feature_scope.value: Nightly batch scoring of 90-day expiry accounts, ML triage routing (auto-renew/review/decline), LLM loss narrative summarization for review queue, underwriter override with reason capture, model explanation output per account, premium change recommendation engine, loss summary generation, adverse action notification trigger, model performance dashboard
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set signed off by underwriting leadership

mvp_scope.value: Nightly scoring batch, three-way routing, model explanation output, underwriter review queue with override, adverse action notification trigger
mvp_scope.rationale: Covers the core automation and compliance obligations before production launch

security_baseline.value: Google IAM least-privilege service accounts, Secret Manager for LLM API keys, all model decision records retained for 7 years for adverse action defense, audit log for every auto-decline and underwriter override
security_baseline.rationale: Adverse action defense and regulatory examination readiness

frontend_stack.value: N/A - backend automation tool; underwriter review interface is a separate internal app consuming this tool's API
frontend_stack.rationale: No standalone frontend; review UI is owned by the policy admin team

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI for review API and Celery for async task management
backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI for the review queue API; Celery for the nightly batch orchestration and LLM summarization jobs

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for triage decisions and override records; Redis 7 for Celery broker and result caching
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL for durable audit-trail storage; Redis as Celery broker for task orchestration

hosting_target.value: GCP Cloud Run for FastAPI services; Cloud Scheduler triggering nightly Celery batch on Cloud Run jobs
hosting_target.rationale: Cloud Run provides serverless scaling with zero idle cost between batch windows

privacy_retention_policy.value: All triage_decisions and model_explanations retained 7 years for adverse action regulatory defense; audit_logs retained 3 years; LLM-generated summaries retained with the decision record
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: State adverse action notification laws require ability to produce the basis for any decline decision

future_scope.value: Real-time single-account scoring API for mid-term endorsement review, model A/B testing framework, integration with carrier catastrophe model for geographic concentration scoring
future_scope.rationale: Post-launch roadmap approved by underwriting and actuarial leadership

constraints.value: Nightly batch must complete by 6 AM local time processing up to 5000 accounts; LLM summarization budget capped at $800/month; no account data may be sent to public LLM APIs
constraints.rationale: SLA from underwriting ops, cost guardrails, and data privacy requirement for proprietary account data

observability_baseline.value: Grafana dashboards for batch job duration, triage routing distribution, model score drift, LLM summarization latency; Prometheus metrics from Cloud Run; PagerDuty alert if batch does not complete by 6 AM
observability_baseline.rationale: Underwriting ops needs visibility into batch completion and model behavior

execution_preference.value: Biweekly sprints with underwriting ops sign-off on triage routing accuracy before each production release
execution_preference.rationale: Routing changes must be validated by underwriting before deployment

compliance_context.value: State adverse action notification requirements (must notify declined accounts within 30 days with reason), NAIC AI principles for algorithmic underwriting, ECOA and fair lending proxy variable review required before model deployment
compliance_context.rationale: Automated decline decisions trigger state adverse action statutes; AI model use in underwriting requires regulatory disclosure

llm_integration.value: Private Vertex AI PaLM 2 endpoint on GCP; account data never leaves GCP perimeter; used for loss narrative summarization only, not for triage scoring decisions
llm_integration.rationale: Keeps commercial account data within GCP data boundary; LLM is advisory only, not the decision model

project.goal: Automate commercial renewal triage with ML scoring and LLM summarization
project.target_users: Commercial lines underwriters and actuarial model monitors
project.timeline: Renewal season launch

backend.framework: FastAPI + Celery
backend.ml_scoring: scikit-learn models served via Cloud Run

security.auth: Google IAM service accounts
security.secrets: Secret Manager for LLM API keys and DB credentials
security.audit: All decline decisions logged with model reasoning

constraints.data_boundary: no data to public LLM APIs

reasoner_reviews.product: By round 4, the core triage routing logic and LLM summarization are well-established. The outstanding gap is model explanation clarity for underwriters: the feature importance output in rounds 1-3 listed raw SHAP values that underwriters could not interpret without actuarial translation. The explanation must be rendered as plain-language factor narratives before the review queue is useful.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The Celery + Cloud Run Jobs architecture is sound for the batch SLA. The remaining architectural gap is the adverse action notification trigger: the system must reliably emit a state-specific adverse action notice within 30 days of auto-decline, and this notification path has not been hardened across prior rounds. The notification service needs a dead-letter queue and retry mechanism.
reasoner_reviews.security: The LLM boundary control - using private Vertex AI so account data stays in GCP - is correctly implemented. The remaining security concern is that the audit_logs table currently stores raw SHAP values as a JSON blob; these need to be indexed by account_id and decision_date to support the 7-year retention query obligation during regulatory examinations.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: LLM summarization cost at $800/month cap with 5000 accounts nightly means approximately $0.16 per summary. Vertex AI PaLM 2 input token cost at current GCP pricing supports this if summaries are generated only for review-queue accounts (estimated 1500/night), not all 5000.

backend.service_design: Four Cloud Run services: (1) renewal-scorer: serves ML model inference endpoint; (2) renewal-batch-orchestrator: Celery worker processing nightly scoring queue; (3) llm-summarizer: async Celery worker calling Vertex AI for review-queue accounts only; (4) review-api: FastAPI service exposing underwriter review queue endpoints.
backend.api_patterns: Internal REST API: GET /v1/review-queue?status=pending&underwriter_id={id}, GET /v1/accounts/{id}/triage-decision, POST /v1/accounts/{id}/override (body: {decision, reason_code, underwriter_id}), GET /v1/accounts/{id}/model-explanation. All endpoints require Google IAM-issued OIDC token.
backend.business_modules: triage_engine: applies three-model ensemble (loss_trend_model, exposure_change_model, market_condition_model) and routes to auto_renew/review/decline based on score thresholds. explanation_renderer: converts SHAP values to plain-language factor narratives using a factor interpretation template. adverse_action_notifier: publishes decline events to a Pub/Sub topic consumed by the notification service.
backend.background_jobs: Nightly Cloud Scheduler job triggers Celery task: (1) query policy admin system for accounts expiring in 90 days; (2) score each account via triage_engine; (3) for review-queue accounts, enqueue llm-summarizer task; (4) write triage_decisions to PostgreSQL; (5) publish auto-decline events to adverse_action Pub/Sub topic.

security.auth_design: Google IAM service account per Cloud Run service with minimum required roles. renewal-scorer: Cloud Run Invoker only. llm-summarizer: Vertex AI User for the specific PaLM 2 model resource. review-api: Cloud SQL Client and Secret Manager Accessor.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Every triage_decision record includes: account_id, decision ENUM, model_scores JSON (with SHAP values indexed by factor_name), llm_summary_id FK, deciding_algorithm_version, timestamp. audit_log table indexed on (account_id, decision_date) to support 7-year retention queries for regulatory examination.
security.incident_response_notes: If the nightly batch produces an anomalous routing distribution (e.g., >15% auto-decline rate vs. 3% baseline), a PagerDuty alert fires and the batch job pauses pending underwriting ops review before adverse action notifications are sent.

data.entities: renewal_accounts (id UUID PK, policy_number VARCHAR, account_name VARCHAR, line_of_business VARCHAR, expiry_date DATE, current_premium DECIMAL, account_manager_id FK, imported_at TIMESTAMP), triage_decisions (id UUID PK, account_id FK, batch_run_id FK, decision ENUM auto_renew/review/decline, composite_score DECIMAL, model_scores JSONB, shap_values JSONB, explanation_narrative TEXT, premium_change_recommendation DECIMAL, decided_at TIMESTAMP), llm_summaries (id UUID PK, account_id FK, prompt_token_count INT, completion_token_count INT, summary_text TEXT, model_version VARCHAR, generated_at TIMESTAMP), underwriter_overrides (id UUID PK, decision_id FK, original_decision ENUM, override_decision ENUM, reason_code VARCHAR, notes TEXT, underwriter_id VARCHAR, overridden_at TIMESTAMP), adverse_action_notifications (id UUID PK, decision_id FK, notification_state ENUM pending/sent/failed, sent_at TIMESTAMP, retry_count INT)
data.retention_and_deletion: triage_decisions and shap_values retained 7 years in PostgreSQL with annual archival to GCS cold storage after year 2. llm_summaries retained with their decision record. adverse_action_notifications retained 7 years for regulatory defense.
data.analytics_events: Daily Grafana dashboard shows: routing distribution by decision type, composite score histogram, LLM token spend by day, underwriter override rate, model version in production.

devops.deployment_topology: Four Cloud Run services in a dedicated GCP project with VPC Service Controls preventing egress to public internet. Cloud Scheduler sends nightly Pub/Sub message at 11 PM triggering Cloud Run Job for batch-orchestrator. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 in same VPC. Memorystore Redis for Celery broker.
devops.ci_cd_design: Cloud Build triggers on PR: pytest with coverage gate (85%), mypy type check, model unit tests against frozen test fixture. On main merge: Cloud Build builds Docker images, pushes to Artifact Registry, deploys to staging Cloud Run with traffic migration. On tag: promote staging images to production via Cloud Run traffic splitting (10% canary for 30 minutes, then 100%).
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus metrics exported from each Cloud Run service. Grafana dashboards for batch job duration, triage routing distribution, model score quartile drift (alerts if p50 score shifts > 0.05 from 30-day baseline), LLM summarization latency and cost.
devops.rollback_strategy: Cloud Run traffic revisions enable instant rollback to prior model or service version. Model artifact versions stored in GCS with immutable retention; redeployment uses specific artifact path.

ISS-001.title: SHAP values displayed as raw numbers in review queue
ISS-001.detail: Rounds 1-2: underwriter review queue displayed raw SHAP values (e.g., 0.34, -0.12) without interpretation, requiring actuarial translation. Resolved in round 3 by adding explanation_renderer converting SHAP to factor narratives.

history.note: explanation_renderer added, plain-language narratives in review queue

ISS-002.title: Adverse action notification has no retry or dead-letter mechanism
ISS-002.detail: Notification service publishes to Pub/Sub but has no dead-letter queue. Failed notifications are silently dropped, creating regulatory exposure if state deadline is missed.

history.note: No DLQ or retry count added in round 3 plan

ISS-003.title: LLM summarization runs on all 5000 accounts exceeding budget
ISS-003.detail: Round 2 plan called LLM summarizer for all 5000 nightly accounts. Resolved in round 3 by restricting LLM calls to review-queue accounts only (estimated 1500/night).

history.note: LLM calls restricted to review-queue accounts

focus_issues.title: Adverse action notification has no retry or dead-letter mechanism
focus_issues.detail: Failed adverse action notifications are silently dropped with no retry, creating regulatory exposure if state 30-day notification deadline is missed.

latest_recommendations: Add a Pub/Sub dead-letter topic (max_delivery_attempts=5, deadLetterPolicy) for adverse action notifications; add a monitoring alert on dead-letter message count; add manual retry endpoint to review-api for compliance team
