project.goal: Centralized compliance management across all holding company subsidiaries
project.target_users: Compliance officers, legal coordinators, document reviewers, executives
project.timeline: Q3 delivery before Q4 examination season

frontend.styling: Tailwind CSS with corporate design tokens

backend.framework: NestJS with TypeORM

security.auth: Azure AD SSO with MFA via MSAL
security.encryption: AES-256 at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit

data.cache: Redis 7 on Azure Cache for Redis

open_questions.notification_channel: Teams webhook vs SMTP fallback strategy

reasoner_reviews.product: The examination lifecycle and document production workflows are the highest-value features for the Q4 deadline. Remediation commitment tracking with automated escalation to the CCO is critical since missed deadlines carry regulatory penalty risk. Board dashboard needs aggregated cross-subsidiary views, not per-examination detail.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: NestJS modular architecture maps cleanly to the domain: examinations, document_production, remediation, subsidiaries, and reporting as separate modules. Multi-subsidiary data isolation should use PostgreSQL row-level security rather than separate schemas to simplify holding-company aggregation queries. Notification delivery must be async via BullMQ with explicit dead-letter queue routing and SMTP fallback.
reasoner_reviews.security: Azure AD RBAC must enforce subsidiary-scoped data access at the API layer, not just UI. Audit logging for every document production request and remediation status change is required for privilege defense. Examination communications may be attorney-client privileged; the system must support privilege tagging to prevent inadvertent disclosure to non-legal roles.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Azure App Service is cost-effective within the $4000/month budget for this traffic profile. The Q4 examination season deadline is firm; scope the MVP to examination tracking and document production, deferring board dashboard to iteration 2.

backend.service_design: NestJS application with domain modules: ExaminationsModule, DocumentProductionModule, RemediationModule, SubsidiariesModule, NotificationsModule, ReportsModule, AdminModule
backend.api_patterns: RESTful versioned at /api/v1, OpenAPI 3.0 auto-generated via @nestjs/swagger, cursor-based pagination for examination lists
backend.business_modules: Examination state machine: opened -> document_requested -> under_review -> findings_issued -> remediation_in_progress -> closed. Document production tracking with coordinator assignment, privilege flagging, and reviewer approval gates.
backend.background_jobs: BullMQ workers on Redis for notification dispatch, overdue deadline detection, and MCAS report generation. 3-attempt exponential backoff (1s, 5s, 30s). Dead-letter queue routing on exhaustion with SMTP fallback via Azure Communication Services.
backend.failure_handling: Global NestJS exception filter with structured error responses. Circuit breaker on Teams webhook integration. Graceful fallback to SMTP when webhook returns non-2xx after retries.

frontend.app_structure: React SPA with feature-based module structure: examinations, documents, remediation, dashboard, admin. Route-based code splitting via React.lazy.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Examination List, Examination Detail with document production queue, Document Review workflow with privilege flag indicator, Remediation Commitment Tracker, CCO Dashboard with cross-subsidiary heatmap, Admin subsidiary management.
frontend.state_management: React Query for all server state with optimistic updates for status changes. Zustand for cross-component notification badge counts and privilege flag UI state.

security.auth_design: Azure AD SSO via MSAL with MFA enforcement via Conditional Access. NestJS JWT guard validates Azure AD tokens on every request. Token refresh via MSAL silent acquisition.
security.authorization_model: RBAC roles (compliance_admin, subsidiary_coordinator, document_reviewer, executive_viewer, legal_counsel) enforced at controller guard level. PostgreSQL RLS policies restrict examination and document_request access by subsidiary_id from JWT claims.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Custom audit_events table capturing user_id, action, resource_type, resource_id, subsidiary_id, ip_address, timestamp for all document access and status mutations. Separate privilege_access_log table for flagged communication access by legal_counsel role.

data.entities: regulatory_examinations, document_requests, production_responses, remediation_commitments, privilege_access_log, subsidiary_entities, audit_events
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 Azure Flexible Server with point-in-time recovery and zone redundancy. Azure Blob Storage for document files with SAS-signed private URLs. Row-level security policies on examinations and document_requests keyed on subsidiary_id.
data.retention_and_deletion: Examination records retained 10 years, archived to Azure Blob cold tier after 3 years. Audit logs 7-year retention. Soft delete with 90-day purge for decommissioned subsidiaries.

devops.deployment_topology: Azure App Service P2v3 API dyno and separate BullMQ worker dyno. React SPA on Azure Static Web Apps. PostgreSQL Flexible Server. Redis Azure Cache for BullMQ queues.
devops.ci_cd_design: Azure DevOps Pipelines: lint, Jest unit tests (75% coverage gate), integration tests against ephemeral PostgreSQL, Docker build, push to Azure Container Registry, rolling deploy to App Service. SPA Vite build and Static Web Apps deploy in parallel.
devops.rollback_strategy: Azure App Service deployment slots with slot-swap blue-green cutover in staging and production. TypeORM migrations run pre-deploy with automatic rollback trigger on failed health check within 5 minutes.

ISS-002.detail: Escalation notifications to CCO rely on Teams webhooks with no retry or fallback, so delivery failures silently drop critical overdue alerts.

history.note: Identified during background job design review

focus_issues.title: No dead-letter queue handling for Teams webhook notification failures

latest_recommendations: Add privileged_flag boolean to document_requests and production_responses tables with privilege_access_log restricted to legal_counsel RBAC role
Implement BullMQ DLQ with 3-retry exponential backoff and SMTP fallback via Azure Communication Services on webhook exhaustion

previous_plan.title: Market Conduct Compliance Portal Architecture v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: NestJS compliance management platform on Azure App Service tracking regulatory examinations and document production for a multi-subsidiary insurance holding company. Used Azure AD RBAC with subsidiary row-level security but lacked privilege tagging controls and notification reliability.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: React 18 SPA on Azure Static Web Apps communicating to NestJS API on Azure App Service. PostgreSQL 16 with RLS by subsidiary_id. Redis for BullMQ. Azure AD SSO. Missing privilege communication controls and no DLQ on notification workers.
previous_plan.data_model: regulatory_examinations, document_requests, production_responses, remediation_commitments, subsidiary_entities. No privileged_flag column or privilege_access_log table present in v1.

target_output.thinking_summary: Revising the compliance portal to address two blocking issues: attorney-client privilege tagging on examination communications and BullMQ dead-letter queue with SMTP fallback for CCO escalation. The privilege model adds privileged_flag boolean to document_requests and production_responses, a separate privilege_access_log table gated behind the legal_counsel RBAC role, and excludes flagged records from non-legal role queries. Notification reliability is addressed via BullMQ 3-attempt exponential backoff, DLQ routing on exhaustion, and automatic SMTP dispatch via Azure Communication Services when Teams webhook returns non-2xx.

fix_report.action_taken: Added privileged_flag boolean to document_requests and production_responses tables. Created privilege_access_log table capturing every access attempt to flagged records restricted to legal_counsel RBAC role. Updated data_model, security_and_compliance, and system_components sections.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Attorney-client privileged communications are isolated behind role-gated access with full audit trail, eliminating inadvertent disclosure risk during regulatory access requests.

fix_report.action_taken: Configured BullMQ notification jobs with 3-attempt exponential backoff (1s, 5s, 30s), DLQ routing on exhaustion, and SMTP fallback via Azure Communication Services Email SDK. Azure Monitor alert fires when DLQ depth exceeds zero. Updated system_components, deployment_and_operations, and observability sections.
fix_report.expected_outcome: CCO overdue escalation notifications are delivered reliably with full observability into delivery failures, eliminating silent alert drops.

target_output.title: Market Conduct Compliance Portal Architecture v2
target_output.executive_summary: RBAC-gated compliance management web application for a multi-subsidiary insurance holding company, tracking state regulatory market conduct examinations, document production requests, remediation commitments, and board-level compliance dashboards. Built on NestJS with React 18 on Azure, featuring attorney-client privilege tagging on examination communications, subsidiary-scoped PostgreSQL row-level security, and reliable BullMQ notification escalation with Teams webhook and SMTP fallback via Azure Communication Services.
target_output.architecture_overview: React 18 SPA on Azure Static Web Apps communicates over HTTPS to a NestJS API on Azure App Service (P2v3) backed by PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Flexible Server with row-level security enforcing subsidiary data isolation. Redis on Azure Cache for Redis powers BullMQ queues for async notification dispatch and MCAS report generation. Azure AD SSO with MFA via Conditional Access authenticates all users; MSAL.js handles client-side token lifecycle. Document production files stored in Azure Blob Storage with private SAS-signed URLs. Privilege-flagged examination communications are gated behind the legal_counsel RBAC role with every access event recorded in the privilege_access_log table. A dedicated BullMQ worker dyno handles Teams webhook delivery with 3-attempt exponential backoff, DLQ routing on exhaustion, and SMTP fallback via Azure Communication Services.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, React Query, Zustand, MSAL.js for Azure AD token management. Backend: NestJS on Node.js 20, TypeORM, BullMQ on Redis, class-validator, @nestjs/swagger for OpenAPI 3.0 auto-generation. Database: PostgreSQL 16 Azure Flexible Server with row-level security policies. Cache and Queues: Redis 7 Azure Cache for Redis. Storage: Azure Blob Storage for document files and remediation evidence. Auth: Azure AD with Conditional Access MFA. Notifications: Azure Communication Services Email SDK for SMTP fallback. Infra: Azure App Service P2v3 (API and worker dynos), Azure Static Web Apps, Azure Container Registry. CI/CD: Azure DevOps Pipelines. Monitoring: Azure Application Insights, Azure Monitor Alerts.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Examination Lifecycle Management: create, track, and close state regulatory market conduct examinations by subsidiary with status state machine (opened, document_requested, under_review, findings_issued, remediation_in_progress, closed). Document Production Queue: manage document requests per examination with coordinator assignment, due dates, privilege flagging for attorney-client communications, reviewer approval gates, and Azure Blob upload. Remediation Commitment Tracker: log findings-based commitments with target dates, owner assignments, completion evidence uploads, and automated CCO escalation for overdue items. Board Dashboard: CCO-level cross-subsidiary heatmap of open examinations, overdue document requests, and remediation completion rates. Admin Panel: subsidiary entity management and user-role assignments.
target_output.system_components: Azure Static Web Apps serving React 18 SPA with CDN distribution. Azure App Service P2v3 API dyno running NestJS API with TypeORM, MSAL JWT guard, and RLS-enforced PostgreSQL access. Azure App Service P2v3 worker dyno running BullMQ notification workers (Teams webhook + SMTP fallback) and MCAS report generators. Azure Database for PostgreSQL 16 Flexible Server with zone redundancy and point-in-time recovery. Azure Cache for Redis 7 for BullMQ queues and session tokens. Azure Blob Storage private containers for document files and remediation evidence. Azure AD for identity, group-based RBAC, and Conditional Access MFA enforcement. Azure Communication Services for SMTP fallback delivery. Azure Application Insights for distributed tracing.
target_output.workflows: Document Production with Privilege Handling: A legal coordinator creates a document_request record for a state examination, sets privilege_flag=true for any item involving attorney communications. Coordinators upload production files to Azure Blob Storage; the API issues SAS-signed download URLs scoped to reviewer roles. Non-legal roles querying document_requests see privileged records excluded by RLS policy; every legal_counsel access to flagged records is written to privilege_access_log with user_id, timestamp, and IP. Remediation Escalation with DLQ Fallback: A BullMQ scheduled job runs hourly checking remediation_commitments for target_date < now() and status != completed. Overdue items trigger a Teams webhook notification to the assigned CCO. On non-2xx response the job retries with 1s, 5s, 30s backoff; on exhaustion the message routes to the DLQ and the worker simultaneously dispatches an SMTP email via Azure Communication Services, triggering an Azure Monitor alert on DLQ depth > 0.
target_output.data_model: regulatory_examinations (id UUID PK, subsidiary_id FK, state_code CHAR(2), exam_type ENUM market_conduct/financial, examiner_name, opened_date, status ENUM opened/document_requested/under_review/findings_issued/remediation_in_progress/closed, closed_date). document_requests (id UUID PK, examination_id FK, request_number, description, due_date, status ENUM pending/in_review/produced/overdue, assigned_coordinator_id FK, privileged_flag BOOLEAN DEFAULT false). production_responses (id UUID PK, document_request_id FK, blob_container, blob_key, uploaded_by FK, uploaded_at, reviewer_id FK, review_status ENUM pending/approved/revision_requested, privileged_flag BOOLEAN DEFAULT false). remediation_commitments (id UUID PK, examination_id FK, finding_description, committed_action, target_date, owner_id FK, status ENUM open/in_progress/completed/overdue, evidence_blob_key). privilege_access_log (id UUID PK, user_id FK, resource_type VARCHAR, resource_id UUID, accessed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, ip_address INET). subsidiary_entities (id UUID PK, name, state_licenses TEXT[], compliance_admin_id FK). audit_events (id UUID PK, user_id FK, action, resource_type, resource_id UUID, subsidiary_id FK, ip_address INET, timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: RESTful API at /api/v1 with OpenAPI 3.0 auto-generated via @nestjs/swagger. GET /examinations?subsidiary_id=&status= (cursor-paginated list, RLS-scoped). POST /examinations (open new examination). GET /examinations/:id (detail with document_request counts). POST /examinations/:id/document-requests (add request with optional privilege_flag). PATCH /document-requests/:id/status (update production status). POST /document-requests/:id/production-responses (upload response file, returns SAS URL). PATCH /document-requests/:id/privilege (toggle privilege_flag, legal_counsel role only). GET /remediation-commitments?overdue=true (CCO overdue queue). POST /remediation-commitments/:id/evidence (upload completion evidence). GET /reports/board-dashboard (aggregated cross-subsidiary metrics, executive_viewer role). All endpoints validate Azure AD JWT; RBAC guards enforce subsidiary_id scoping.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Azure AD SSO with MFA enforced via Conditional Access policies for all user roles. MSAL.js handles client-side token acquisition and silent refresh. NestJS JWT guard validates Azure AD access tokens on every API request. RBAC roles (compliance_admin, subsidiary_coordinator, document_reviewer, executive_viewer, legal_counsel) enforced at NestJS controller guard level. PostgreSQL row-level security policies restrict SELECT, UPDATE, INSERT on examinations, document_requests, and production_responses to rows matching subsidiary_id claim from JWT. Attorney-client privilege: privileged_flag=true records excluded from non-legal_counsel role queries at both API guard and RLS policy layers; every legal_counsel access logged to privilege_access_log with user, timestamp, and IP. SOC 2 Type II audit logging via audit_events table covering all document access and status mutations forwarded to Azure Monitor. TLS 1.3 in transit, AES-256 at rest on PostgreSQL Flexible Server and Azure Blob Storage.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Azure DevOps Pipelines: ESLint and TypeScript typecheck, Jest unit tests with 75% coverage gate, integration tests against ephemeral PostgreSQL containers, Docker image build, push to Azure Container Registry, rolling deploy to Azure App Service API and worker dynos. React SPA: Vite build and Azure Static Web Apps deploy run in parallel pipeline. Environments: dev (shared Flexible Server, single App Service instance), staging (dedicated Flexible Server, App Service deployment slots for blue-green swap), production (zone-redundant Flexible Server with HA standby, App Service deployment slots with manual swap approval gate). TypeORM migrations run as pre-deploy one-off App Service task; automatic rollback triggered if health check fails within 5 minutes. Secrets stored in Azure Key Vault, accessed by App Service via managed identity binding.
target_output.observability: Azure Application Insights SDK with distributed tracing across API and worker dynos, correlating examination_id and subsidiary_id across all log entries. Custom metrics tracked: document production queue depth per examination, remediation overdue count by subsidiary, BullMQ DLQ depth, Teams webhook delivery success rate per day. Azure Monitor Alerts: remediation items overdue > 5 days fires P2 page to CCO; BullMQ DLQ depth > 0 fires P1 page to on-call; API 5xx error rate > 2% for 5 minutes fires P2; PostgreSQL connection pool > 80% fires P3; App Service CPU > 75% for 10 minutes fires P3.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $2800/month: App Service P2v3 x2 dynos (~$300), PostgreSQL Flexible Server General Purpose 4 vCores zone-redundant (~$500), Redis Cache C2 (~$250), Static Web Apps ($9), Blob Storage (~$50), Application Insights ingestion (~$100), Azure Communication Services email (~$20), Container Registry and Key Vault (~$70). Headroom below $4000/month cap for peak examination season. Scale-out: API App Service manually scaled to 3 instances during Q4 examination peak; worker dyno scaled independently. PostgreSQL read replica added if board dashboard p95 query latency exceeds 150ms under CCO load.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): Azure infrastructure provisioning with Key Vault and managed identity, Azure AD RBAC and MFA Conditional Access integration, examination lifecycle state machine with status transitions, document production queue with file upload, privilege tagging, and privilege_access_log, audit_events logging, CI/CD pipeline with ephemeral test databases, basic examination list and detail UI. Phase 2 (4 weeks): Remediation commitment tracker with BullMQ overdue detection, Teams webhook escalation with DLQ and SMTP fallback, CCO board dashboard with cross-subsidiary heatmap, compliance calendar views with upcoming deadline alerts. Phase 3 (4 weeks): MCAS annual statement data export, examination finding reporting templates, advanced admin subsidiary management, privilege_access_log audit reporting tool for legal team review.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode across NestJS API and React SPA. ESLint and Prettier enforced in Azure DevOps CI gate. Jest unit tests for all NestJS service methods; integration tests for RBAC guards and RLS policies using disposable PostgreSQL containers. PRs require one senior engineer approval and passing CI. NestJS modules must not import across domain boundaries without an explicit shared interface. All BullMQ job handlers must be idempotent with explicit deduplication keys based on remediation_commitment_id and round timestamp. Azure Key Vault for all secrets; no credentials in environment variables or application code.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: PostgreSQL RLS policy complexity increasing with subsidiary count above 50 subsidiaries causing query planner overhead. Mitigation: benchmark RLS evaluation at 100 subsidiaries in staging; fall back to application-layer tenant filtering if p95 SELECT on examination lists degrades below 200ms. Risk: BullMQ Redis dependency — transient Redis outage halts notification dispatch. Mitigation: Azure Cache for Redis zone-redundant tier; SMTP fallback via Azure Communication Services ensures CCO delivery even during Redis degradation. Tradeoff: Privilege flagging at document_request record level rather than individual file level reduces granularity but substantially simplifies the access control model for MVP. Tradeoff: Azure App Service over AKS reduces operational complexity and cost but limits fine-grained horizontal scaling for the worker dyno during examination reporting peaks.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Teams webhook chosen over Microsoft Graph API for notification delivery due to lower implementation complexity and faster time-to-value; Azure Communication Services SMTP provides the reliability fallback. Cursor-based pagination selected for examination lists to handle subsidiaries accumulating 500-plus historical examinations efficiently. Azure Blob SAS-signed URLs chosen over server-side streaming proxy to eliminate App Service bandwidth costs for large document production file downloads.

metadata.notes: Round 2 revision resolving privilege tagging and BullMQ DLQ gaps for insurance market conduct compliance web portal.

project_goal.value: Mobile claims first notice of loss app for homeowners policyholders to report property damage, upload photos, schedule adjuster inspections, and track claim status in real time, migrating from a legacy hybrid WebView app to a fully native React Native build
project_goal.rationale: Policyholder experience upgrade and performance improvement on legacy WebView hybrid

target_users.value: Homeowners policyholders filing first notice of loss, adjusters receiving claim assignments and status updates
target_users.rationale: Primary end users identified in product discovery

project_class.rationale: Native mobile app replacing legacy WebView hybrid, iOS and Android targets

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, integrations, realtime, analytics
capabilities.rationale: Photo upload, real-time status push, adjuster scheduling, and claim tracking all required

complexity_level.rationale: Standard mobile CRUD plus real-time WebSocket status push and photo upload pipeline

risk_level.rationale: Handles personal policyholder data and claim financials; no payment processing

data_sensitivity.rationale: Stores policyholder PII, property damage descriptions, and inspection scheduling details

external_exposure.rationale: All endpoints require Cognito authentication; no unauthenticated API access

access_model.rationale: Claim data must be strictly scoped to the filing policyholder

mvp_scope.rationale: Core policyholder need for app store launch

frontend_stack.rationale: Cross-platform iOS and Android from single codebase, migration from legacy WebView hybrid

backend_stack.rationale: Team Python expertise, async support for WebSocket connections and photo upload handling

hosting_target.rationale: Managed containers, consistent with carrier cloud standard

constraints.rationale: Platform minimums set by policyholder device analytics; CMS integration is mandatory on day 1

observability_baseline.rationale: Corporate observability standard, mobile crash visibility critical for app store ratings

execution_preference.rationale: Established mobile delivery workflow

project.goal: Native mobile FNOL app replacing legacy WebView hybrid for homeowners claim filing
project.target_users: Homeowners policyholders and claims adjusters
project.timeline: Q3 app store submission

backend.framework: FastAPI with SQLAlchemy 2.0
backend.api: RESTful plus WebSocket for claim status push

security.auth: Cognito user pools, JWT on all endpoints
security.encryption: TLS 1.3 in transit, S3 SSE for photos

data.cache: Redis 7 on ElastiCache for WebSocket broadcast

devops.ci: Bitrise for React Native, GitHub Actions for API

constraints.integration: existing claims management system REST webhook

open_questions.websocket_fallback: polling fallback if WebSocket connection fails on poor mobile network

reasoner_reviews.product: The FNOL submission and real-time claim status tracking are the core value props of the migration. The legacy WebView app's poor photo upload performance was the primary policyholder complaint; the React Native build must address this with a resumable upload strategy. Adjuster scheduling and payment status display are table-stakes features for a complete claim journey.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: FastAPI with async endpoints handles photo upload and WebSocket connections well. The claims management system webhook integration is a hard day-1 constraint; the backend must publish claim events to the CMS synchronously on FNOL submission to avoid state divergence. WebSocket for real-time status is correct but needs a polling fallback for policyholders on poor mobile networks.
reasoner_reviews.security: Cognito JWT claim scoping is critical: every API endpoint must validate that the authenticated policyholder sub matches the claim owner_id before returning data. S3 pre-signed URL pattern for photo upload avoids routing large binaries through the API container. CCPA deletion support needs a documented deletion workflow not just a policy statement.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: iOS 16 and Android 13 minimum covers the carrier's 95th percentile device analytics. The Q3 app store deadline is achievable with MVP scope if the CMS webhook integration is de-risked in sprint 1.

backend.service_design: FastAPI application with routers: claims (FNOL submission, status), photos (pre-signed URL generation), scheduling (adjuster appointment booking), notifications (WebSocket manager), webhooks (CMS outbound events)
backend.api_patterns: RESTful for CRUD operations, WebSocket endpoint /ws/claims/{claim_id}/status for real-time status push, S3 pre-signed URLs for direct photo upload from mobile client
backend.failure_handling: Idempotent FNOL submission with client-generated UUID claim reference to prevent duplicate submissions on network retry. CMS webhook delivery retried 3 times with exponential backoff; DLQ on SNS for manual replay.
backend.scaling_notes: ECS Fargate auto-scaling on ALB request count target 500 req/minute. WebSocket connections managed via Redis pub/sub with per-claim channel, allowing horizontal scaling of FastAPI workers.

frontend.app_structure: React Native app with feature folders: fnol, claim-status, scheduling, photos, auth. Expo modules for camera and photo library access on iOS and Android.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Login, Claim Dashboard (open claims list), FNOL Submission (multi-step wizard: damage type, description, photo capture/upload, submit), Claim Detail (status timeline, adjuster info, communication log), Schedule Inspection, Payment Status.
frontend.state_management: Zustand for session and claim form state across FNOL wizard steps. React Query for claim list and detail data with background refetch. WebSocket subscription managed in claim-status Zustand slice.
frontend.performance_notes: Resumable multipart photo uploads via S3 pre-signed multipart API. Photo compression to 1080p max before upload to reduce mobile data usage. Lazy-load claim history beyond 10 most recent items.

security.auth_design: Cognito user pool with email/SMS verification. Policyholder role assigned at registration. Adjuster role provisioned by admin. JWT access token validated on every FastAPI endpoint via dependency injection.
security.authorization_model: Endpoint-level ownership check: claims/:id endpoints verify claim.owner_cognito_sub matches authenticated user sub. Adjuster role can read assigned_claims list only. No cross-policyholder data access permitted.
security.privacy_controls: CCPA deletion workflow: DELETE /account triggers soft-delete of policyholder record, queues S3 photo deletion job, and removes PII from claim records within 30-day SLA. Documented in privacy settings screen.

data.entities: claims, damage_photos, adjuster_appointments, claim_status_updates, policy_holders, inspection_reports, payment_disbursements, communication_logs
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ for claim lifecycle data. S3 bucket with SSE-S3 encryption for damage photos and inspection reports, organized by claim_id prefix. ElastiCache Redis for WebSocket pub/sub channels.
data.retention_and_deletion: Claims 7-year retention, photos 5-year with S3 Intelligent-Tiering after 1 year, communication logs 3-year retention with automated Glacier archival.

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate cluster with ALB for FastAPI API tasks and a separate WebSocket task group. Separate ECS task for CMS webhook retry worker. RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ. ElastiCache Redis for pub/sub.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions for API: lint (ruff, mypy), pytest with 70% coverage gate, Docker build, push ECR, deploy Fargate rolling update. Bitrise for React Native: TypeScript check, Jest unit tests, EAS Build for iOS TestFlight and Android internal track, EAS Submit for app store release.
devops.environments: dev (single-AZ RDS), staging (Multi-AZ, mirrors production for app store review), production (Multi-AZ RDS, Fargate auto-scaling 2-8 tasks).

ISS-001.title: No WebSocket polling fallback for poor mobile network conditions
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 architecture specified WebSocket for real-time claim status but provided no fallback for policyholders who lose WebSocket connectivity on cellular networks, leaving status stale indefinitely.

history.note: Identified during mobile network resilience review

ISS-002.title: CCPA deletion workflow not implemented, only policy-stated
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan mentioned CCPA deletion as a policy commitment but did not specify the deletion endpoint, S3 photo purge job, or PII scrubbing procedure for claim records.

history.note: Identified during compliance review

focus_issues.title: No WebSocket polling fallback for poor mobile network conditions
focus_issues.detail: WebSocket only with no fallback leaves claim status stale on cellular connectivity loss.

focus_issues.title: CCPA deletion workflow not implemented
focus_issues.detail: Deletion endpoint, S3 photo purge, and PII scrubbing procedure absent from round 1 plan.

latest_recommendations: Add React Native WebSocket with automatic polling fallback when disconnect detected, using exponential backoff reconnect with HTTP long-poll at /claims/:id/status-poll
Implement DELETE /account endpoint triggering soft-delete, SQS-queued S3 photo deletion job, and PII field nullification on claim records within 30-day CCPA SLA
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: Homeowners Claims FNOL Mobile App Architecture v1

convergence_state.reason: Two medium issues from round 1 unresolved; score below 7.5 threshold

previous_audits.summary: Reasonable React Native migration architecture with correct S3 pre-signed photo upload pattern and Cognito ownership scoping. Two compliance and resilience gaps must be resolved: no WebSocket fallback leaves policyholders with stale status on poor networks, and the CCPA deletion commitment has no implementation.
strengths: S3 pre-signed URL photo upload pattern correctly avoids routing large binaries through API containers
Cognito sub ownership check design is correct for policyholder data isolation
Resumable photo upload via S3 multipart addresses the primary legacy app complaint
concerns: WebSocket-only real-time status leaves policyholders with stale claim state on cellular connectivity loss
CCPA deletion is cited as a policy but has no endpoint, S3 purge job, or data scrubbing procedure
blocking_issues: ISS-001: Mobile users on unstable cellular lose claim status updates with no fallback mechanism
ISS-002: CCPA deletion workflow absent from implementation creates legal compliance gap
recommendations: Implement WebSocket with automatic exponential backoff reconnect and HTTP polling fallback on disconnect detection
Add DELETE /account endpoint with SQS-queued S3 photo purge job and PII nullification on claim records

issue_updates.title: No WebSocket polling fallback
issue_updates.detail: Architecture specifies WebSocket only with no fallback strategy for cellular disconnects

issue_updates.title: CCPA deletion workflow absent

previous_plan.title: Homeowners Claims FNOL Mobile App Architecture v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: React Native migration of legacy WebView FNOL app with FastAPI backend on ECS Fargate. S3 pre-signed photo upload, Cognito auth, real-time status via WebSocket, adjuster inspection scheduling. Missing WebSocket fallback and CCPA deletion implementation.
previous_plan.workflows: FNOL Submission: policyholder fills multi-step wizard, uploads photos via S3 pre-signed URLs, submits claim triggering CMS webhook. Status Tracking: WebSocket subscription to claim status channel. No fallback polling specified.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Cognito JWT with policyholder sub ownership check. CCPA deletion mentioned as policy commitment with no implementation detail.

target_output.thinking_summary: Revising the homeowners FNOL mobile architecture to address WebSocket resilience on poor cellular networks and CCPA account deletion implementation. The WebSocket fallback uses an exponential-backoff reconnect loop in the React Native WebSocket manager; after 3 failed reconnects the client switches to HTTP polling at /claims/:id/status-poll with 30-second intervals until connectivity is restored. CCPA deletion is implemented as DELETE /account triggering a soft-delete of the policyholder record, an SQS message to a Fargate deletion worker that purges S3 photos by claim_id prefix, and a PII nullification update on claim records within a 30-day processing SLA.

fix_report.action_taken: Added WebSocket reconnect manager in React Native claim-status Zustand slice with 3-attempt exponential backoff (2s, 8s, 30s). After exhaustion, client falls back to HTTP polling at GET /claims/:id/status-poll every 30 seconds. WebSocket reconnection attempted again when network-change event fires. Updated workflows, system_components, and frontend sections.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Policyholders on poor cellular connections receive claim status updates via polling fallback, eliminating stale status experience.

fix_report.action_taken: Added DELETE /account endpoint that soft-deletes the policyholder record, enqueues SQS message with cognito_sub and associated claim_id list to deletion worker, which purges all S3 objects under claims/{claim_id}/photos/ prefix and nullifies PII fields (name, address, phone, email) on claim records within 30 days. Deletion status tracked in account_deletion_requests table. Updated security_and_compliance, data_model, and api_design sections.
fix_report.expected_outcome: CCPA deletion requests are processed with a documented implementation and audit trail, satisfying the 30-day deletion SLA.

target_output.title: Homeowners Claims FNOL Mobile App Architecture v2
target_output.executive_summary: Native React Native homeowners claims app migrating from legacy WebView hybrid, enabling policyholders to file first notice of loss, upload property damage photos, schedule adjuster inspections, and track claim status in real time. FastAPI backend on ECS Fargate with Cognito auth, S3 pre-signed photo upload, WebSocket status push with cellular-resilient polling fallback, and CCPA account deletion workflow. Integrates with existing claims management system via outbound webhook on FNOL submission.
target_output.architecture_overview: React Native iOS and Android app (TypeScript, React Navigation v6) communicates over HTTPS to a FastAPI REST API and WebSocket endpoint on ECS Fargate behind ALB. Cognito user pool issues JWTs validated on every request with policyholder sub ownership scoping. Damage photos are uploaded directly from the mobile client to S3 via pre-signed multipart URLs, bypassing the API container. Real-time claim status pushed over WebSocket with per-claim Redis pub/sub channel; React Native WebSocket manager automatically falls back to 30-second HTTP polling after 3 failed reconnect attempts. FNOL submissions trigger a synchronous webhook to the existing claims management system. A separate ECS Fargate deletion worker processes SQS-queued CCPA account deletion requests, purging S3 photos and nullifying PII on claim records.
target_output.technology_stack: Mobile: React Native with TypeScript, React Navigation v6, Zustand, React Query, Expo Camera and MediaLibrary modules, New Relic Mobile SDK. Backend: FastAPI on Python 3.12, SQLAlchemy 2.0, Pydantic v2, boto3 for S3 and SQS, python-jose for Cognito JWT validation. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ. Cache and Pub/Sub: Redis 7 on ElastiCache. Storage: S3 with SSE-S3 for damage photos. Auth: AWS Cognito user pools. Queuing: SQS for CCPA deletion worker and CMS webhook retry. CI/CD: Bitrise with EAS Build for mobile, GitHub Actions for API. Monitoring: New Relic Mobile SDK and APM, PagerDuty.
target_output.functional_feature_map: FNOL Submission: multi-step wizard (damage type selection, free-text description, photo capture or library upload via Expo modules, policy number confirmation, submit). Photo Upload: resumable multipart S3 upload using pre-signed URLs with 1080p compression before upload. Claim Status Tracking: real-time status updates via WebSocket subscription to per-claim Redis channel, automatic polling fallback on disconnect. Adjuster Scheduling: inspection appointment booking against adjuster availability calendar. Communication Log: in-app messages between policyholder and adjuster displayed in claim detail. Payment Disbursement Status: read-only disbursement status from CMS webhook events. Account Deletion: CCPA-compliant DELETE /account with 30-day SLA and status tracking.
target_output.system_components: ALB routing HTTPS to ECS Fargate API task group (FastAPI with REST and WebSocket endpoints) and separate deletion worker task group (SQS consumer). Cognito user pool for policyholder and adjuster authentication. RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ for claim lifecycle data. ElastiCache Redis 7 for WebSocket pub/sub channels per claim. S3 bucket with SSE-S3 for damage photos organized by claims/{claim_id}/photos/ prefix. SQS FIFO queue for CCPA deletion jobs and CMS webhook retry DLQ. New Relic APM sidecar on API tasks. Bitrise pipeline for React Native EAS Build and EAS Submit.
target_output.workflows: FNOL Submission and CMS Integration: Policyholder completes multi-step wizard, selects damage type (wind, water, fire, theft), enters description, captures photos via Expo Camera or photo library. App requests S3 pre-signed multipart URL from POST /claims/photo-upload-url; uploads compressed photos directly to S3. On submit, POST /claims creates claim record in PostgreSQL, publishes initial status event to Redis claim channel, and synchronously POST webhooks the CMS with claim metadata and S3 photo keys within 5 seconds; on CMS webhook failure retries 3 times then routes to SQS DLQ for manual replay. Claim Status Real-Time and Fallback: App opens WebSocket to /ws/claims/{claim_id}/status authenticated via Cognito JWT query param. FastAPI subscribes to Redis channel claims:{claim_id}:status and broadcasts events to all connected clients. React Native WebSocket manager detects disconnect and attempts reconnect with 2s, 8s, 30s backoff; after 3 exhausted attempts switches to HTTP polling at GET /claims/{claim_id}/status-poll every 30 seconds until NetInfo network-change event triggers WebSocket reconnect attempt.
target_output.data_model: claims (id UUID PK, owner_cognito_sub VARCHAR NOT NULL, policy_number, damage_type ENUM wind/water/fire/theft/other, description TEXT, status ENUM submitted/assigned/inspection_scheduled/under_review/settled/closed, cms_claim_reference, created_at, updated_at). damage_photos (id UUID PK, claim_id FK, s3_key, s3_bucket, upload_status ENUM pending/completed/failed, file_size_bytes, captured_at). adjuster_appointments (id UUID PK, claim_id FK, adjuster_id FK, scheduled_at TIMESTAMPTZ, address, status ENUM proposed/confirmed/completed/cancelled). claim_status_updates (id UUID PK, claim_id FK, previous_status, new_status, changed_by, changed_at, notes). policy_holders (id UUID PK, cognito_sub UNIQUE, first_name, last_name, email, phone, address, deleted_at TIMESTAMPTZ). account_deletion_requests (id UUID PK, cognito_sub, requested_at, status ENUM queued/processing/completed, completed_at, photos_purged_count). communication_logs (id UUID PK, claim_id FK, sender_role ENUM policyholder/adjuster, message TEXT, sent_at).
target_output.api_design: REST API at /api/v1. POST /auth/token (Cognito token exchange). POST /claims (FNOL submission, idempotent on client_reference_id). GET /claims (policyholder's own claims list). GET /claims/:id (claim detail, ownership enforced). POST /claims/:id/photo-upload-url (generate S3 pre-signed multipart URL). GET /claims/:id/status-poll (HTTP polling fallback endpoint, returns current status). POST /claims/:id/appointments (book adjuster inspection). GET /claims/:id/communications (message thread). POST /claims/:id/communications (send message). DELETE /account (initiate CCPA deletion, returns deletion_request_id). GET /account/deletion-status/:id (poll deletion progress). WebSocket: /ws/claims/:id/status (real-time status channel, Cognito JWT via query param).
target_output.security_and_compliance: Cognito user pool with email verification. Policyholder role assigned on registration; adjuster role provisioned by admin. FastAPI dependency enforces JWT validation and policyholder sub ownership check on every /claims/:id endpoint. S3 pre-signed URLs scoped to the specific claim photo prefix with 15-minute expiry. CCPA deletion: DELETE /account soft-deletes policy_holders row (sets deleted_at), enqueues SQS FIFO message with cognito_sub and claim_id list. Deletion worker purges all S3 objects under claims/{claim_id}/photos/ and nullifies first_name, last_name, email, phone, address fields on policy_holders and free-text fields on communication_logs within 30-day SLA. Deletion progress tracked in account_deletion_requests. State fair claims settlement acts: CMS webhook delivers claim acknowledgment event back to app within mandated timeframes. TLS 1.3 in transit, SSE-S3 for photos at rest.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions for API: ruff lint, mypy type-check, pytest with 70% coverage gate (claims router, ownership checks, deletion workflow), Docker build, push ECR, ECS Fargate rolling update with health check gate. Separate ECS task definition for deletion worker consuming SQS FIFO queue. Bitrise for mobile: TypeScript type-check, Jest unit tests, EAS Build on every PR for iOS simulator and Android emulator smoke test; EAS Submit to App Store Connect TestFlight and Google Play internal track on main merge. Environments: dev (single-AZ RDS, shared Redis), staging (Multi-AZ RDS, isolated Redis, app binary distributed via TestFlight for QA), production (Multi-AZ RDS, Fargate auto-scaling 2-8 API tasks and 1-3 deletion worker tasks, CloudFront for API HTTPS termination).
target_output.observability: New Relic Mobile SDK for crash reporting, HTTP error tracking, and session replay on FNOL wizard. New Relic APM agent on FastAPI tasks tracking WebSocket connection counts, S3 pre-signed URL generation latency, and CMS webhook delivery success rate. Custom metrics: FNOL submissions per hour, WebSocket fallback-to-polling activation rate, CCPA deletion queue depth, deletion completion time p95. PagerDuty alerts: FNOL submission failure rate > 5% fires P1; WebSocket fallback activation rate > 20% fires P2 (possible Redis pub/sub issue); SQS DLQ message count > 0 fires P2; API p99 latency > 3s fires P3.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $1100/month: ECS Fargate API tasks 2-8 at ~$300, deletion worker tasks at ~$50, RDS Multi-AZ db.t3.medium ~$220, ElastiCache Redis cache.t3.small ~$70, S3 storage and request costs ~$80, Cognito MAU under 50K free tier, New Relic ~$150, SQS near-zero at this scale. Photo storage grows ~2GB per 1000 claims; S3 Intelligent-Tiering applied to photos older than 90 days. Fargate scales on ALB target 500 req/minute; WebSocket task group scales on connection count.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): React Native project scaffolding with React Navigation, Cognito auth flows, FNOL multi-step wizard with photo upload via S3 pre-signed multipart, claim status display with WebSocket and polling fallback, CMS webhook integration, GitHub Actions and Bitrise CI/CD, TestFlight and internal track distribution. Phase 2 (4 weeks): Adjuster inspection scheduling with calendar UI, in-app communication log, payment disbursement status display, CCPA deletion workflow with SQS worker, app store production release. Phase 3 (4 weeks): New Relic Mobile session replay integration, push notification for claim status changes via SNS, performance optimization for photo capture on older devices.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode in React Native codebase. ESLint with React Native community config in Bitrise CI gate. Jest unit tests for Zustand slices (WebSocket fallback state machine) and FastAPI route handlers (ownership check, deletion workflow). All FastAPI endpoints must return structured error responses with request_id correlation. React Native WebSocket manager must be tested with mock disconnect events in Jest. Photo compression must target under 500KB per photo before S3 upload. All S3 pre-signed URLs must include content-type validation to prevent non-image uploads.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: CMS webhook synchronous call on FNOL submission adds latency to the submission response if CMS is slow. Mitigation: 3-second timeout on webhook call with async retry via SQS; policyholder sees success immediately, CMS delivery confirmed asynchronously. Risk: S3 CCPA deletion worker processing delay if SQS queue backs up during high claim volume. Mitigation: deletion worker auto-scales on SQS queue depth; 30-day SLA provides buffer. Tradeoff: React Navigation v6 over Expo Router chosen for more granular screen lifecycle control during FNOL wizard multi-step state. Tradeoff: Redis pub/sub over SQS for WebSocket broadcast chosen for sub-100ms latency; Redis failure degrades to polling fallback automatically.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: WebSocket polling fallback interval set to 30 seconds on cellular, balancing battery usage against status freshness. S3 pre-signed URL expiry set to 15 minutes covering typical photo upload duration including compression. CMS webhook timeout set to 3 seconds with async retry queue to prevent FNOL submission latency from CMS response time variability.

metadata.notes: Round 2 revision addressing WebSocket polling fallback and CCPA deletion implementation for homeowners FNOL mobile app migration from WebView hybrid.

project_goal.value: Cloud infrastructure foundation for a greenfield specialty admitted carrier, establishing multi-account AWS organization with landing zones, centralized logging, secrets management, and network segmentation for policy administration and claims platforms
project_goal.rationale: Greenfield carrier needs secure, compliant cloud foundation before any application workloads can be deployed

target_users.value: Platform engineering team, application teams building policy admin and claims platforms, security and compliance auditors
target_users.rationale: Internal teams consuming the infrastructure foundation

project_class.rationale: Pure infrastructure provisioning with Terraform and AWS CDK; no application runtime

capabilities.rationale: Infrastructure provisioning and platform automation are the only capabilities; no frontend or data platform

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-account AWS organization, Transit Gateway networking, centralized security tooling, and compliance automation

risk_level.rationale: Infrastructure controls protect all future policyholder and financial data across the carrier's platforms

data_sensitivity.rationale: Infrastructure configuration and IAM policies contain sensitive access controls for financial and insurance data systems

external_exposure.rationale: Infrastructure management plane is internal only; no public access to control plane

access_model.value: AWS IAM Identity Center with SAML federation to corporate IdP; role-based account access per team with least-privilege SCPs at organization root
access_model.rationale: Centralized identity management for multi-account access with auditable session provisioning

feature_scope.value: AWS Organizations multi-account structure with landing zone automation, VPC and Transit Gateway network topology, centralized CloudTrail and Security Hub, AWS Secrets Manager with rotation, service control policies, CIS benchmark compliance automation, IAM Identity Center federation
feature_scope.rationale: Complete foundation feature set from platform engineering requirements

mvp_scope.value: AWS Organizations hierarchy with SCPs, VPC landing zones for policy-admin and claims accounts, centralized CloudTrail lake, IAM Identity Center with corporate IdP federation
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum required before application teams can begin workload deployment

security_baseline.value: CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark Level 2, Security Hub enabled in all accounts aggregated to security account, CloudTrail enabled in all accounts forwarding to central S3 lake, GuardDuty enabled, SCPs denying disabling of security tooling
security_baseline.rationale: NYDFS 23 NYCRR 500 and SOC 2 Type II minimum security controls

frontend_stack.value: none - infrastructure project with no application frontend
frontend_stack.rationale: Infrastructure project; platform engineering CLI and AWS Console serve as interfaces

backend_stack.value: Terraform with AWS CDK for infrastructure provisioning; Python 3.12 Lambda for compliance automation
backend_stack.rationale: Terraform for declarative multi-account IaC; CDK for complex automation constructs; Lambda for event-driven Config remediation

data_platform.value: S3 with Athena for CloudTrail lake queries; no application database
data_platform.rationale: Log aggregation and querying via S3-native lake; no stateful application data in infrastructure layer

hosting_target.value: AWS Organizations multi-account: management, security, log-archive, shared-services, and per-application workload accounts
hosting_target.rationale: AWS multi-account best practice for blast radius isolation and billing separation

privacy_retention_policy.value: CloudTrail logs retained 7 years in log-archive account S3 with Object Lock WORM; Config snapshots retained 3 years; VPC flow logs retained 1 year
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: NYDFS 23 NYCRR 500 audit log retention requirements

future_scope.value: Addition of workload accounts for telematics data pipeline, agent portal, and partner API gateway as carrier grows
future_scope.rationale: Foundation designed to vend new accounts consistently via landing zone automation

constraints.value: All resources must reside in us-east-1 and us-west-2 only for data residency; Terraform state stored in S3 with DynamoDB locking in management account; GitLab CI must be the pipeline runner
constraints.rationale: State insurance department data residency requirements and existing GitLab enterprise license

observability_baseline.value: AWS CloudWatch for platform metrics, Security Hub findings aggregated to security account, PagerDuty integration for critical Config rule violations and GuardDuty High findings
observability_baseline.rationale: Security-first observability for infrastructure compliance posture

execution_preference.value: Infrastructure as code pull-request workflow on GitLab; all changes via Terraform plan/apply pipeline; no manual console changes in production accounts
execution_preference.rationale: GitOps discipline required for audit traceability

compliance_context.value: NYDFS Cybersecurity Regulation 23 NYCRR 500 (multi-factor authentication, encryption, audit logs), SOC 2 Type II (CC6, CC7 controls), CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark Level 2, state insurance department IT governance notification requirements for system changes
compliance_context.rationale: High risk level and confidential data sensitivity on carrier infrastructure triggers compliance context

project.goal: Provision secure multi-account AWS foundation for greenfield specialty admitted carrier
project.target_users: Platform engineering, application teams, security auditors
project.timeline: 6 weeks to MVP landing zones before application teams onboard

backend.framework: Terraform modules with account vending machine pattern
backend.automation: Python Lambda for AWS Config auto-remediation

security.auth: IAM Identity Center with corporate SAML IdP
security.controls: SCPs, GuardDuty, Security Hub, CloudTrail WORM logs

data.logging: CloudTrail lake on S3 in log-archive account
data.state: Terraform state in S3 with DynamoDB locking

devops.pipeline: Terraform plan on MR, apply on main merge

open_questions.account_vending: manual vs automated account vending for new workload accounts

reasoner_reviews.product: The landing zone foundation is the critical path dependency for all carrier application development. The MVP scope of Organizations hierarchy, VPC topology, CloudTrail, and IAM Identity Center is the right minimum. Account vending automation should be included in Phase 1 even at MVP to prevent a manual bottleneck as application teams onboard.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Terraform module structure should mirror the account hierarchy: one module per account type (management, security, log-archive, workload) with a shared networking module for Transit Gateway. AWS Control Tower could accelerate landing zone setup but adds constraints on SCP customization that may conflict with NYDFS requirements; evaluate carefully. CIS Level 2 benchmark automation via AWS Config managed rules with Lambda auto-remediation is the right pattern.
reasoner_reviews.security: SCPs must deny disabling CloudTrail, Security Hub, GuardDuty, and Config in all non-management accounts. NYDFS 23 NYCRR 500 requires MFA for all privileged access; IAM Identity Center must enforce MFA for all roles above read-only. CloudTrail Object Lock WORM retention is required for regulatory audit defensibility.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: GitLab CI pipeline must use OIDC federation to AWS for short-lived credentials; long-lived access keys in GitLab CI variables would violate NYDFS least-privilege requirements. Terraform state encryption at rest must be enforced on the S3 backend bucket.

backend.service_design: Terraform module hierarchy: root modules per account type consuming shared child modules for VPC, IAM, logging, and security tooling. Account vending machine Lambda triggered by DynamoDB account request table.
backend.business_modules: Management account module (Organizations, SCPs, IAM Identity Center). Security account module (Security Hub aggregator, GuardDuty master, IAM Access Analyzer). Log-archive account module (CloudTrail S3 lake with Object Lock, VPC flow log aggregation). Shared-services account module (Transit Gateway, Route 53 private zones, shared ECR). Workload account module (VPC with subnets, NAT gateways, Security Hub member enrollment, CloudTrail member).
backend.background_jobs: Lambda functions for: AWS Config auto-remediation of CIS Level 2 findings, new account enrollment into Security Hub and GuardDuty aggregation, daily compliance drift report generation to S3.
backend.scaling_notes: Landing zone pattern supports up to 200 accounts without architectural changes. Transit Gateway route tables structured to allow workload account addition without modifying shared-services module.

security.auth_design: IAM Identity Center with SAML 2.0 federation to corporate IdP. Permission sets: PlatformAdmin (break-glass only, requires MFA step-up), SecurityAuditor (read-only Security Hub), AppTeamDeveloper (workload account write, log-archive read-only), BillingReader. MFA enforcement policy on all permission sets above BillingReader.
security.secrets_management: AWS Secrets Manager in shared-services account for cross-account secret sharing. Rotation enabled for all database credentials with 30-day rotation schedule. KMS customer-managed keys per account for secrets encryption. No hardcoded credentials permitted in Terraform; all sensitive values via Secrets Manager data sources.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: CloudTrail multi-region trail enabled in all accounts forwarding to log-archive account S3 bucket with Object Lock Compliance mode, 7-year retention. Security Hub findings forwarded to EventBridge for PagerDuty integration. AWS Config continuous recording in all accounts with snapshot delivery to log-archive.

data.entities: aws_accounts, landing_zones, network_segments, iam_roles, secrets_vaults, log_aggregation_configs, compliance_controls, service_control_policies
data.storage_design: CloudTrail lake: S3 in log-archive account with Object Lock WORM, versioning enabled, server-side encryption with KMS CMK. Athena workgroup for CloudTrail queries with results stored in separate S3 prefix. Terraform state: S3 with versioning, encryption, DynamoDB state lock table per environment.
data.analytics_events: Daily AWS Config compliance summary Lambda exports findings count by account and rule to S3 for weekly security posture reporting.

devops.deployment_topology: GitLab CI pipelines with OIDC federation to AWS assume-role. Terraform workspaces per account. Manual approval gate for management and security account applies. Workload account Terraform apply automated on main merge.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI stages: terraform-validate (tflint, tfsec static analysis), terraform-plan (output posted as MR comment), manual-approve (for management/security accounts), terraform-apply. SCP changes require security team approval in GitLab CODEOWNERS.
devops.observability_stack: Security Hub aggregated findings dashboard in security account. CloudWatch alarms on Config non-compliant resource count. PagerDuty P1 for GuardDuty High/Critical findings and SCP violation events.
devops.backup_and_recovery: Terraform state S3 versioning enables rollback to prior state. AWS Backup for any stateful resources in shared-services account. Config snapshots provide point-in-time resource inventory.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a multi-account AWS landing zone foundation for a greenfield specialty admitted carrier with NYDFS 23 NYCRR 500 and SOC 2 Type II compliance requirements. Key decisions: Terraform module hierarchy mirroring account structure, GitLab CI with OIDC federation for short-lived credentials, CloudTrail Object Lock WORM for regulatory audit defensibility, and SCPs denying security tooling disablement. The weak quality reflects that several plan sections are at minimum depth — the data model, cost, and phased implementation sections are less detailed than they could be.
target_output.title: AWS Multi-Account Landing Zone Foundation for Specialty Admitted Carrier
target_output.executive_summary: Terraform and AWS CDK provisioned multi-account AWS organization landing zone for a greenfield specialty admitted carrier, establishing account hierarchy, Transit Gateway networking, centralized CloudTrail log lake with Object Lock WORM, Security Hub aggregation, IAM Identity Center with corporate SAML federation, and CIS Level 2 compliance automation. Designed to meet NYDFS 23 NYCRR 500 and SOC 2 Type II requirements with GitOps-only change management.
target_output.architecture_overview: AWS Organizations hierarchy with management account at root, dedicated security, log-archive, and shared-services accounts, and per-application workload accounts vended via landing zone automation. Transit Gateway in shared-services account provides hub-and-spoke connectivity between workload account VPCs without public internet routing. All AWS API calls across all accounts flow to a centralized CloudTrail lake in the log-archive account with Object Lock Compliance mode for WORM retention. Security Hub aggregates findings from all member accounts to the security account. GitLab CI pipelines with OIDC federation to AWS execute Terraform plan and apply; no long-lived access keys are used. IAM Identity Center federates the corporate SAML IdP with permission sets enforcing MFA for all roles above read-only.
target_output.technology_stack: Infrastructure as Code: Terraform 1.8 with HCL modules per account type, AWS CDK Python 3.12 for complex Lambda constructs. Provisioning targets: AWS Organizations, Transit Gateway, VPC, Security Hub, GuardDuty, CloudTrail, AWS Config, Secrets Manager, IAM Identity Center, KMS. Compliance automation: Python Lambda for Config auto-remediation and account enrollment. Pipeline: GitLab CI with OIDC AWS federation. State backend: S3 with DynamoDB locking and KMS encryption. Log querying: Amazon Athena on CloudTrail lake.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Account Vending: Lambda-driven account creation enrolling new workload accounts into Organizations, SCPs, Security Hub, GuardDuty, and CloudTrail aggregation without manual steps. Network Topology: Transit Gateway hub-and-spoke with VPC per workload account, NAT gateway egress, no cross-account direct VPC peering. Centralized Logging: CloudTrail multi-region trails from all accounts to log-archive S3 lake with Object Lock WORM. Compliance Automation: AWS Config managed rules mapped to CIS Level 2 benchmarks with Lambda auto-remediation for fixable violations. Secrets Management: Secrets Manager in shared-services with cross-account resource policy, KMS CMK per account.
target_output.system_components: AWS Organizations with management account, security account (Security Hub aggregator, GuardDuty master), log-archive account (CloudTrail S3 lake with Object Lock, Config snapshots), shared-services account (Transit Gateway, Route 53 private zones, Secrets Manager, ECR), workload accounts (application VPCs, Security Hub member, CloudTrail member). GitLab CI runner with OIDC federation. Lambda functions for account vending enrollment and Config auto-remediation.
target_output.workflows: New Workload Account Vending: Platform engineer merges a Terraform workload account definition; GitLab CI plan runs, security team approves for any SCP modification, apply executes: Organizations CreateAccount, baseline SCP attachment, VPC provisioning, Transit Gateway attachment, Security Hub member enrollment, GuardDuty member invite, CloudTrail member trail enablement, IAM Identity Center permission set assignment. All steps idempotent via Terraform state. Compliance Drift Detection: AWS Config continuously evaluates all resources against CIS Level 2 rules; non-compliant resources trigger EventBridge events. Lambda auto-remediation handles fixable violations (S3 bucket public access block, CloudTrail log file validation). Unfixable violations publish Security Hub findings forwarded via EventBridge to PagerDuty.
target_output.data_model: Terraform state files per account in S3 with DynamoDB lock table: account_type, workspace_name, lock_id, last_modified. Account inventory tracked in a DynamoDB table: account_id, account_name, account_type ENUM management/security/log-archive/shared-services/workload, ou_id, created_at, owner_team, compliance_enrolled BOOLEAN. CloudTrail lake stored in S3 with prefix structure org-id/account-id/year/month/day/region/log-files. Config snapshots in S3 per account per delivery channel.
target_output.api_design: No external API surface. Internal automation interfaces: Lambda account vending function triggered by DynamoDB Streams on account_requests table insert. Config rule Lambda evaluators registered via AWS Config custom rules. GitLab CI pipeline OIDC token exchange with STS AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity for scoped Terraform execution roles per account.
target_output.security_and_compliance: SCPs at organization root denying: disabling CloudTrail, disabling Config, disabling GuardDuty, disabling Security Hub, creating IAM users with console access, leaving the organization. NYDFS 23 NYCRR 500: MFA enforced on all IAM Identity Center permission sets above BillingReader via Conditional Access policy. CloudTrail Object Lock Compliance mode with 7-year retention in log-archive account satisfies audit log immutability. GuardDuty threat detection enabled in all accounts. AWS Macie enabled in log-archive account to detect sensitive data patterns in CloudTrail logs. Terraform state S3 bucket: versioning, SSE-KMS, block public access, access logging enabled. SOC 2 CC6 and CC7: Security Hub findings forwarded to PagerDuty; monthly CIS benchmark compliance report generated by Lambda to S3.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitLab CI pipeline stages: tflint validate (HCL syntax and best practice), tfsec security static analysis (blocks on HIGH findings), terraform plan (output posted as MR comment with resource diff), manual-approve stage (required for management, security, and log-archive account changes; auto-approved for workload accounts), terraform apply. CODEOWNERS requires security team review for any SCP or IAM Identity Center permission set changes. Terraform workspaces: one per account, state stored in management account S3 with per-workspace KMS CMK encryption. Rollback via Terraform state restore from S3 versioned prior state. No manual console changes permitted; AWS Config detects manual changes and fires non-compliant finding.
target_output.observability: Security Hub aggregated findings dashboard in security account with weekly executive summary report. CloudWatch alarm on Config non-compliant resource count per account firing P3 alert for count > 5, P1 for count > 20. GuardDuty High and Critical findings trigger P1 PagerDuty page via EventBridge. SCP violation CloudTrail events trigger P2 PagerDuty alert for unauthorized action attempts. Terraform apply pipeline failures alert on-call platform engineer.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $800/month for foundation accounts: CloudTrail data events at scale ~$200, Security Hub ~$150, GuardDuty ~$200, Config rules ~$150, Transit Gateway attachment fees ~$100. Scales linearly with workload account count; each new account adds approximately $50/month in Security Hub and GuardDuty member costs.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (4 weeks): Terraform management account bootstrap, Organizations hierarchy with SCPs, IAM Identity Center with corporate SAML federation, log-archive account with CloudTrail Object Lock lake, security account with Security Hub and GuardDuty aggregation, GitLab CI pipeline with OIDC federation. Phase 2 (2 weeks): Shared-services account with Transit Gateway, VPC landing zone module for workload accounts, policy-admin and claims workload accounts provisioned. Phase 3 (2 weeks): AWS Config CIS Level 2 rules with Lambda auto-remediation, account vending DynamoDB automation, Secrets Manager cross-account setup, Athena CloudTrail query workgroup.
target_output.development_guidelines: All infrastructure changes via GitLab CI pull request; zero manual console changes in production accounts enforced by Config rules. Terraform modules must pass tflint and tfsec in CI before merge. Every new AWS resource must have cost_center and environment tags or Config rule fires non-compliant finding. Lambda automation functions must be Python 3.12 with type hints and pytest coverage above 60%. SCP changes require security team CODEOWNERS approval. Sensitive variable values sourced from Secrets Manager; no plaintext secrets in Terraform variable files or GitLab CI variables.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Terraform state corruption in management account S3 due to concurrent apply runs. Mitigation: DynamoDB state locking with pipeline serialization ensures single concurrent apply per workspace. Risk: Corporate IdP outage blocks all IAM Identity Center access. Mitigation: break-glass IAM user in management account stored in physical safe with MFA hardware token. Tradeoff: Custom Terraform modules over AWS Control Tower gives full SCP flexibility at the cost of longer initial build time; required to meet NYDFS custom SCP requirements. Tradeoff: Lambda account vending over Service Catalog reduces UI complexity for platform engineers who already use the GitLab CLI.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: GitLab CI OIDC federation selected over long-lived access keys to satisfy NYDFS least-privilege credential requirements. Account vending Lambda chosen over manual Terraform workspace creation to prevent platform team bottleneck as carrier grows. us-east-1 primary and us-west-2 disaster recovery regions selected per state insurance department data residency constraint.

metadata.notes: First-pass greenfield AWS multi-account landing zone for specialty admitted carrier with NYDFS and SOC 2 compliance; plan is thin in data model and cost sections.

project_goal.value: CLI tool for insurance data engineers to validate and profile incoming bordereaux files from managing general agents, checking field completeness, data type conformance, premium totals reconciliation, and business rule compliance before loading into the carrier's data warehouse
project_goal.rationale: Pre-load validation prevents corrupt or non-compliant MGA data from polluting the carrier data warehouse

target_users.value: Data engineers and data analysts at the carrier who receive and validate bordereaux submissions from managing general agents before ETL pipeline ingestion
target_users.rationale: Internal technical users running validation in local and CI environments

project_class.rationale: Local command-line tool; no server runtime, no browser UI, no database server

capabilities.rationale: File parsing, validation logic, and report generation; no auth, frontend, or integrations needed

complexity_level.rationale: Single-process CLI with file I/O and rule evaluation; no distributed components

risk_level.rationale: Internal tooling operating on already-received files; no external exposure or sensitive data transmission

data_sensitivity.rationale: Processes MGA bordereaux containing internal premium and policy data but not individual policyholder PII

external_exposure.rationale: Runs on local workstations and CI runners; no network service

access_model.value: No authentication; operator is already an authenticated internal employee running locally
access_model.rationale: Local tool with no multi-user access control requirements

feature_scope.value: Bordereaux CSV and Excel parsing, per-MGA validation configuration in YAML, field nullability checks, data type conformance validation, premium sum reconciliation against header totals, business rule evaluation (policy date ranges, coverage code lists), row-level error manifests, summary validation reports in JSON and CSV
feature_scope.rationale: Complete validation suite from data engineering requirements

mvp_scope.value: CSV bordereaux parsing with YAML validation config, nullability and type checks, premium reconciliation, JSON validation report output
mvp_scope.rationale: Core validation loop needed for first MGA onboarding

security_baseline.value: No secrets stored; input files treated as untrusted; no execution of content from bordereaux files; output reports written to local filesystem only
security_baseline.rationale: Local tool with minimal attack surface

frontend_stack.value: none - CLI tool with stdout and file output only
frontend_stack.rationale: CLI tool; terminal output and report files serve as user interface

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Typer for CLI framework
backend_stack.rationale: Team Python expertise; Typer provides clean command and flag definitions with automatic help generation

data_platform.value: Polars for in-memory bordereaux dataframe parsing; no persistent database
data_platform.rationale: Polars provides fast CSV and Excel parsing with lazy evaluation suitable for large bordereaux files

hosting_target.value: Distributed as a PyPI package and GitHub Releases binary via PyInstaller; runs on local workstations and CI runners
hosting_target.rationale: Simple distribution to internal team without requiring Python environment on every machine

privacy_retention_policy.value: Tool does not persist input data; validation reports written to caller-specified output directory and not retained by the tool itself
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: No data persistence by the tool; retention responsibility belongs to the invoking pipeline

future_scope.value: Excel bordereaux format support, direct integration with S3 for reading bordereaux from carrier SFTP drop zone, HTML report output for business users
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 enhancements after initial MGA onboarding validation

constraints.value: Must process bordereaux files up to 500,000 rows within 60 seconds on standard CI runner hardware; YAML config files must be versionable in Git alongside the ETL pipeline code
constraints.rationale: Performance requirement from largest MGA monthly file size; Git versioning enables drift tracking per MGA

observability_baseline.value: Structured JSON logs to stdout at DEBUG and INFO levels; validation report written to --output-dir; exit code 0 for pass, 1 for validation failures, 2 for tool errors
observability_baseline.rationale: CI pipeline integration requires machine-readable exit codes and structured output

execution_preference.value: Feature-branch development with GitHub Actions CI running tests and building binary artifacts
execution_preference.rationale: Standard Python project workflow

project.goal: Validate MGA bordereaux files before ETL pipeline ingestion
project.target_users: Data engineers running locally and in CI
project.timeline: First MGA onboarding in 3 weeks

backend.framework: Typer CLI, Polars dataframes

open_questions.excel_support: openpyxl vs calamine for Excel parsing speed

reasoner_reviews.product: The validator must be simple enough for a data engineer to run in one command with one config file. The JSON report output is the right default for CI integration; adding a human-readable summary table to stdout is a usability improvement worth including even in MVP. Per-MGA YAML config versioned in Git is the correct approach for tracking schema drift between MGA reporting periods.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Polars lazy evaluation handles 500K-row CSV files well within the 60-second constraint. The YAML config schema needs a clear extension point for new rule types without requiring changes to the core validation engine. A plugin-style rule registry allowing new rule classes to be registered by name from YAML is the right architecture for extensibility.
reasoner_reviews.security: The tool must sanitize YAML config loading against arbitrary code execution; use PyYAML safe_load only, never full_load. Input CSV parsing must not eval any cell content. Output directory must be validated to prevent path traversal in --output-dir flag.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: PyInstaller binary distribution eliminates the Python environment requirement on CI runners. The 60-second performance requirement is achievable with Polars lazy scan even at 500K rows; benchmark at 1M rows to establish the ceiling.

backend.service_design: Typer CLI application with commands: validate (run validation against a bordereaux file), profile (generate field statistics without rule checking), config-check (validate a YAML config file syntax), list-rules (display all registered rule types).
backend.api_patterns: CLI command interface: bxval validate --file path/to/bx.csv --config path/to/mga-config.yaml --output-dir ./reports --format json. Exit codes: 0 pass, 1 validation failures present, 2 tool error.
backend.business_modules: ConfigLoader: YAML safe_load with JSON Schema validation of config structure. BordereauxParser: Polars lazy scan for CSV, scan_excel for XLSX. RuleEngine: registry-based rule evaluation dispatching each YAML rule entry to a registered RuleClass. ReportWriter: serializes ValidationResult to JSON and CSV in --output-dir.
backend.failure_handling: File not found raises ToolError (exit code 2) with structured error message. YAML parse error reports line number of invalid config. Polars parse errors on malformed CSV report row numbers of unparseable records without aborting the full validation run.

data.entities: bordereaux_files, validation_rules, field_profiles, reconciliation_checks, business_rule_configs, validation_reports, mga_schemas
data.schema_notes: YAML config schema per MGA: mga_id, schema_version, fields (list of field definitions with nullability, dtype, allowed_values), reconciliation (premium_total_field, count_field), business_rules (list of rule name, params). JSON report schema: mga_id, file_path, processed_at, row_count, pass BOOLEAN, error_count, warning_count, field_errors (list), reconciliation_result, rule_results.
data.consistency_notes: Field dtype coercion rules must match the downstream Snowflake target types to prevent silent type mismatches that pass validation but fail on warehouse COPY. Validation config schema_version enables drift detection when MGA changes their file layout between reporting periods.

previous_plan.title: MGA Bordereaux Validator CLI Architecture v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Python Typer CLI with Polars lazy CSV parsing, YAML-configurable validation rules per MGA, rule registry pattern, and JSON/CSV report output. Fast 500K-row performance validated. Missing Excel support and unsafe YAML loading.
